the  Ninth  Circuit,  which  includes  the  states  of Alaska,
California,  Hawaii,  Idaho,  Montana,  Nevada,  Oregon,

13

and  Washington,  the  citizens  of each  state  are  free  to
amend  their  state  constitutions  to  adopt  policies  of
strict  racial  neutrality  in the  areas  of public  education,
employment,  and  contracting.
But  voters  in  states
within  the  jurisdiction  of  the  Sixth  Circuit,  which
includes  Kentucky,  Michigan,  Ohio,  and  Tennessee,
may  not.

C.  The  Sixth  Circuit’s  Decision

Directly  Conflicts  with  the
California  Supreme  Court’s
Decision  in  Coral  Construction  v.
City  &  County  of San  Francisco

The  conflict  between  the  federal  circuits  1s  not
the  only  justification  for  review  in  this  case  under
Supreme  Court  Rule  10(a).
The  Sixth  Circuit's
decision  invalidating  §  26  also  conflicts  with  the
decision  of  the  California  Supreme  Court  in  Coral
Construction,  Inc.  v.  City  & County  of San  Francisco,
235  P.3d  947.  Coral  Construction  involved  a  challenge
to  a  local  ordinance  that  required  the  City  and  County
of  San  Francisco  (San  Francisco)  to  grant  race-  and
sex-based  preferences  in  the  operation  of  public
contracting  to  minority-owned  business  enterprises
and  women-owned  business  enterprises.  Construction
companies  not  entitled  to  San  Francisco’s  racial
preferences  challenged  the  ordinance  as  violating
Proposition  209.
Resorting  to  the  same  arguments
used  by the  plaintiffs  in  Wilson  JJ,  and  in  Coalition  Il
with  respect  to § 26, San  Francisco  claimed  Proposition
209  was  unconstitutional  under  the  Hunter  and  Seattle
political  structure  doctrine.  Coral  Construction,  235
P.3d  at  956.  In  2010,  the  California  Supreme  Court
“exercising  [its]  independent  judgment  on  the  matter,”
concluded  in  a  6-1  decision  that  Proposition  209  does

14

not  violate  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  under  a
Coral  Construction,
political  structure  analysis.
235  P.3d  at  959.

In  Coral  Construction,  San  Francisco  argued
that  with  the  passage  of  Proposition  209,  minority
groups  secking  race-  or  gender-based  preferences  in
public  education,  emyloyment,  and  contracting  must
first  overcome  the  o »stacle  of  amending  the  state
constitution,  while  groups  that  seek  preferences  on
other  bases  (e.g.,  disakility  or  veteran  status)  need  not.
Coral  Construction,  235  P.3d  at  958.
The  court,
according  “great  weigl.t”  tothe  Ninth  Circuit's  decision
in  Wilson  lI, found  tha:  the  “political  structure  doctrine
does  not  invalidate  :itate  laws  that  broadly  forbid
preferences  and  discrimination  based  on  race,  gender,
and  other  similar  clas:sifications.”  Coral  Construction,
235  P.3d  at  958  (citing  Wilson  L/,  122  F.3d  at  708-09).
The  court  found  nothing  in  Hunter  or  Seattle  that
defined  “beneficial  legislation”  to  include  race-  or
gender-based  prefereaces.
Coral  Construction,  235
P.3d  at  959.  Following  the  Ninth  Circuit's  decision  in
Wilson  II,  the  court  held:  “‘Even  a  state  law  that  does
restructure  the  political  process  can  only  deny  equal
protection  if it  burdens  an  individual's  mght  to  equal
treatment.”  Jd.  at  960  (quoting  Wilson  //,  122  F.3d  at
707).
But  instead  o°  burdening  the  right  to  equal
treatment,  the  court  concluded  that  Proposition  209
directly  serves  the  principle  that  “all  governmental  use
of  race  must  have  a  logical  end  point.”
Coral
Construction,  238  P.3d  at  960.

Thus,  the California  Supreme  Court  stands  with
the  Ninth  Circuit  in holding  that  the political  structure
doctrine  does  not  invalidate  a  state’s  constitutional
provision  which  declé<  res  that  the  state,  including  its

15

political  subdivisions,  “shall  not  discriminate  against,
or  grant  preferential  treatment  to,  any  individual  or
group  on  the  basis  of  race.  sex,  color,  ethnicity,  or
national  origin  in the  operation  of public  employment,
public  education,  or  public  contracting.”  Cal.  Const.
art.  I, § 31(a);  Coral  Construction,  235  P.3d  at  953.

D.  The  Sixth  Circuit’s  Decision
Conflicts  with  the  Louisiana
Supreme  Court’s  Decision  in
Louisiana  Associated  General
Contractors,  Inc.  v.  State  of Louisiana

The  Sixth  Circuit’s  decision  also  merits  review
because  it  conflicts  with  the  Louisiana  Supreme
Court’s  decision  in  Louistana  Associated  General
Contractors,  Inc.  v.  Louisiana,  669  So.  2d  1185  (La.
AGC),  to  the  extent  it  prevents  the  State  of Michigan
from  amending  its  constitution  to  ban  all  forms  of
government  racial  discrimination.

Louisiana's  current  constitution  was  adopted  at
a constitutional  convention  in  1974,  and  ratified  by the
voters  of  Louisiana  on  April  20,  1974.  ‘The  Louisiana
equal  protection  clause  does  not  contain  the  same
language  as  the  federal  Equal  Protection  Clause.
Article  1, § 3, of the  Louisiana  Constitution  provides  in
pertinent  part  that  “No  person  shall  be  denied  the
equal  protection  of the  laws.  No law  shall  discriminate
against  a  person  because  of  race  or  religious  ideas,
beliefs,  or  affiliations.”  La.  Const.  art.  1, § 3.  In  other
words,  like  California,  government  classifications
based  on  race  are  absolutely  prohibited  in  Louisiana.
La.  AGC,  669  So.  2d at  1197  (citing  Lee  Hargrave,  The
Declaration  of Rights  of the  Louisiana  Constitution  of
1974,  35  La.  L.  Rev.  1, 6 (1974);  see  Wilson  I],  122  F.3d
at  702  (“Rather  than  classifying  individuals  by race  or

16

gender,  Proposition  £09  prohibits  [California]  from
classifying  individuals.  by race  or  gender.’).

In  La.  AGC,  the  Louisiana  Supreme  Court
its  holding  from  Sibley  v.  Board  of
reiterated
Supervisors  of Louisicna  State  University,  477  So.  2d
1094  (La.  19385).  “that  when  a  law  discriminates
against  a  person  by classifying  him  or  her  on  the  basis
of race,  it shall  be repudiated  completely,  regardless  of
the  justification  behind  the  racial  discrimination.”  669
La.  AGC  further  explained  that  the
So.  2d  at  1198.

discriminates  in  favor  of certain  races  to  remedy  past
discrimination”  is prohibited.  Jd.  at  1199  n.12.  Under
the  Louisiana  Const  tution,  racial  preferences  are

another  race....”  Jd  The  court  explained  that “the

on  the  basis  of race  fo:  any  reason  whatsoever.”  /d.  at

Courts  should  not  interpret  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  to  prevent  voters  from  prohibiting  all
government  discrimination.  In terms  of procedure  and
structure,  making  it  more  difficult  to  engage  in  such
discrimination  and  preferential  treatment  is  the
purpose  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
The  very
existence  of  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  shows  that
that  this  is  precisely  the  sort  of  discrimination  that
must  not  be  left  to  everyday  politics,  and  the  voters  in
Michigan  agreed.
The  United  States  has  seen
institutionalized  discrimination  in  favor  of  whites
replaced  by  institutionalized  discrimination  against
whites  (and  Asians)  in  less  than  a  generation,  and
racial  spoils  will  always  be attractive  to  politicians  and

THE  SIXTH  CIRCUIT'S  DECISION
PREVENTS  MICHIGAN  VOTERS
FROM  ENFORCING  THE  SAME
UNIFORM  STATEWIDE  BAN
ON  RACIAL  DISCRIMINATION
AND  PREFERENCES  THAT
HAS  BEEN  PASSED  AND
UPHELD  IN  OTHER  STATES

The  Sixth  Circuit's  decision  prohibits  Michigan
voters  from  enforcing  an  amendment  to  their  state
constitution,  even  though  identical  amendments  are
being  enforced  by voters  in  other  states.  Currently,  in
addition  to  California  and  Louisiana,  described  supra,
voters  from  four  other  states  have  amended  their  state
constitutions  to “adopt|]  a  position  of strict  neutrality.”
Hunter,  393  U.S.  at  395  (Harlan,  J.,  concurring).  Two
other  states  have  passed  legislation  prohibiting  racial
Review  by  this
preferences  in  college  admissions.
Court  is  thus  necessary  to  prevent  widespread  voter
disillusionment,  hopelessness,  apathy,  and  eventual
anger  caused  by  inconsistent  judicial  decisions  on
nationally  important  issues  of constitutional  law  and
public  policy.

A.  The  Sixth  Circuit’s  Decision

Prevents  Michigan  Voters  from
Enforcing  Their  Constitutional
Amendment,  While  Voters  in
Other  States  Enforce  Nearly
Identical  Amendments

On  November  3,  1998,  the  people  of the  State  of
Washington  passed  Initiative  Measure  200  by  58%  of
the  vote.  Measure  200  declares  that  “the  state  shall

Smith  v.  Univ.  of

In  November  of  2008,  Nebraska  voters  passed
the  Nebraska  Civil  Rights  Ininative  424,  which
amended  the  state  constitution  to  prohibit  the  state
from  discriminating  against,  or  granting  preferential
treatment  to,  “any  individual  or  group  on  the  basis  of
race,  sex,  color,  ethnicity,  or  national  origin  in  the
operation  of  public  employment,  public  education,  or
public  contracting.”  Neb.  Const.  art  .  I, § 30  (2012).  It
passed  with  58%  of the  vote.  The  Nebraska  Supreme
Court  mentioned  th.s  amendment  with  approval  in
Nebraska  v.  Henderson,  762  N.W.2d  1,  15  (Neb.),  cert.
denied,  557  U.S.  921  (2009).

On  November  2, 2010,  Arizona  voters  approved
the  Arizona  Civil  R:ghts  Initiative,  Proposition  107,
which  amended  the  state  constitution  to  prohibit  the
state  from  “grant[ing]  preferential  treatment  to  or

In  such  a  country  governed  by  documents
grounded  in  fundamental  principles  of equality,  it  is

In  the
United  States,  the  cnly  viable  racial  policy  is  one
where  the  government  plays  no  favorites.  Anything
else  leads  to  division,  strife,  and  balkanization.

In  November,  1999,  Florida  Governor  Jeb  Bush
signed  Executive  Order  99-281,  the  “One  Florida
Initiative,”  ending  the  use  of race/ethnicity  or  gender
in  the  state’s  employment,  contracting,  and  higher
education  admission  decisions.  NAACP  v.  Fla.  Bd.  of
Regents,  822  So.  2d  1,  2  (Fla.  Dist.  Ct.  App.  2002),
quashed,  remanded  on  standing  issues,  863  So.  2d  294
(Fla.  2003),  dismissed  on  mootness,  876  So.  2d 636  (Fla.
Dist.  Ct.  App.  2004).  Section  3 of the  Executive  Order
is titled  “Non-Discrimination  in Higher  Education.”  In
paragraph  (b) of that  section,  the  governor  “requested
that  the  Board  of  Regents  implement  a_  policy
prohibiting  the  use  of  racial  or  gender  set-asides,
preferences  or  quotas  in  admissions  to  all  Florida
institutions
effective
immediately.”.
To  comply  with  the  governor's
request,  the  Board  of Regents  adopted  amendments  to
its  rules  in  2000,  setting  general  requirements  for
student
admissions,  admission  requirements  for
entering  freshmen,  and  admission  requirements  for
entering  or  transferring  graduate  and  professional
students.
876  So.  2d  at  2.  The  Board  of  Education
approved  the  amendments  on  February  22,  2000.  The
Board  of  Governors,  which  replaced  the  Board  of
Regents,  came  into  existence  on  January  7, 2003,  Fla.
Const.  art.  XI,  § 5(d),  and  adopted  all  of  the  race-
neutral  rules  established  by the  Regents.  876  So.  2d at
639.

QUESTION  PRESENTED

One  by  one  in  the  pursuit  of genuine  racial  equality
that  has  eluded  the  Republic  since  its  founding,
states  are  promulgating  in  their  constitutions  and
statutes  laws  that  strip  government  agencies  and
their  subdivisions  —  in  this  case,  universities,  with
respect  to  their  admissions  policies  ~  of the  ability  to
use  racial  preferences;  in  reviewing  those  laws,  lower
courts  are  extending  and  applying  this  Court’s  “politi-
cal  process”  doctrine  with  results  that  are  markedly
different;  as  such,  Petitioner’s  application  for  review
by this  Court  as  to  the  application,  clarity,  and  proper
reach  of  the  doctrine  is  of  the  greatest  urgency.
Should  this  Court  grant  Petitioner’s  application  for
Writ  of Certiorari?

INTEREST  OF AMICI  CURIAE'’

The  amici  represented  in  this  brief  are  citizens
and  elected  officials  of their  states  who  have  champi-
oned  a  quantum  leap  forward  to achieve  the  dreamed-
of  society  that  is  conscious  only  of  one’s  character,
irrespective  of  race,  sex,  color,  ethnicity,  or  national
origin.  They  have  worked  diligently  within  their
respective  jurisdictions  to  convince  their  fellow  citi-
zens  that  discrimination  based  on  race,  sex,  color,
ethnicity,  or  national  origin  with  respect  to,  inter  alia,
college  admissions,  ultimately  harms  all  of humanity
—  the  purpose  or  good  intentions  of such  discrimina-
tion  notwithstanding.  The  amici,  therefore,  represent
the  collective  voices  of  millions  of  Americans  who
want  their  governments  to  practice  in  law  what  the
amici  and  their  fellow  citizens  do  in  their  own  daily
lives.

'  Pursuant  to  Rule  37  of the  Rules  of the  Supreme  Court,
counsel  of record  for  all  parties  received  notice  at  least  10  days
prior  to  the  due  date  of the  amici  curiae’s  intention  to  file  this
brief.  All  parties  have  consented  to  the  filing  of this  brief.  Those
consents  are  being  lodged  herewith.  No  counsel  for  any  party
authored  this  brief  in  whole  or  in  part,  and  no  counsel  or  party
made  a  monetary  contribution  intended  to  fund  the  preparation
or  submission  of this  brief.  No  person  other  than  amici  curiae,
its  members,  or  its  counsel  made  a  monetary  contribution  to  its
preparation  or  submission.

2

INTRODUCTION

This  case  represents  a  waypoint  with  two  distinct
characteristics:  First,  it  posits  a  marker  upon  the
legal  landscape  of  Fourteenth  Amendment  jurispru-
dence  and,  in  so  doing,  represents  a  stark  departure
from  the  determination  in  Coalition  for  Economic
Equity  v.  Wilson,  122  F.3d  692  (9th  Cir  1997),  its
progeny,  and  those  courts  that  have  followed  its
reasoning  with  respe:t  to  the  relationship  between
this  Court’s  political-jrocess  doctrine  and  the  Four-
teenth  Amendment’s  Equal  Protection  Clause.  This
legal  marker  is  no  small  matter:  While  Western
history  is  repleat  with  animus  between  peoples
separated  by  religious  identity  or  socio-economic
class,  this  Republic  stands  alone  in  its  struggle  to
come  to  terms  with  its  racial  identity  and  the  equal
protection.  of  the  law  to  all  persons.  Therefore,  the
applicability,  proper  reach,  and  perhaps  even  the
continued  viability  o°  the  political-process  doctrine
laid  out  by this  Court  in Hunter  v.  Erickson,  393  U.S.
385  (1969)  and  Washington  v.  Seattle  School  District
No.  1, 458  U.S.  457  (1982),  and  applied  by the  court
below  in  this  case,  méztters  greatly  in  this  struggle  to
achieve  the  dreamed-cf  society,  wherein  the  content  of
one’s  character  obliverates  any  consideration  by
government  of a  person’s  race,  sex,  color,  ethnicity,  or
national  origin.  Such  disparate  application  of  the
doctrine  will  continue  to  vex  the  public  policy  direc-
tion  of this  country  unless  and  until  this  Court  acts
decisively  on  the  issues  raised  in Petitioner’s  applica-

tion.

3

The  second  marker  occasioned  by this  case  is  far
more  subtle  but just  as  nctable  as  the  first:  the  people
of  the  Republic  have  “moved  on”  in  their  attitudes,
norms,  and  practices  with  respect  to  race,  sex,  color,
ethnicity,  and  nationa!  origin;  moreover,  their  percep-
tions  and  treatment  of one  another  along  those  classi-
fications  has  evolved  far  more  quickly  than  the  speed
or  dexterity  with  which  our  courts  —  including  this
Court  —  have.  So  while  our  courts  toil  among  the
thistles  of predecent  and  the  jurisprudential  history
of  law  and  race  in  America,  the  citizens  themselves
have  moved  far  beyond  the  current  legal  horizon  and
walk  with  a  determination  of their  own  toward  the
dreamed-of  Republic  that  is  racially  tolerant  and
discrimination-free.  In  a  nutshell,  the  ground  below
the  legal  landscape  has  shifted.

In  this  case,  the  pilgrimage  toward  equality  is
embodied  by  Michigan’s  Civil  Rights  Initiative,  now
MicuH.  Const.  art.  I, § 26,  and  amici  respectfully  call
upon  this  Court  to  grant  Petitioner’s  application  and
address  the  legal  questions  raised  below.

*

4

ARGUMENT

I.

Lower  courts’  application  of the  political-
process  doctrine  with  respect  to  alleged
violations  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment’s
Equal  Protection  Clause  has  been  narrow,
and  applied  to  only  avoid  illicit  govern-
ment  discrimination,  never  to  mandate  it,
as  the  result  below  would  require;  as
such,  this  Court  should  grant  Petitioner’s
request  for  Writ  of Certiorari.

This  Court  should  grant  Petitioner’s  application
because  lower  courts  need  firm,  clear  direction  as  to
the  applicability  of  :he  political-process  doctrine  to
citizen-led  efforts,  wt.ere  the  effort  is one  which  seeks
to  amend  the  state's  Jaws  to  prohibit,  inter  alia,  racial
discrimination.  Without  such  clarity,
states  and
federal  courts  will  continue  to  struggle  with  race  and
gender  equity  and,  in  so  doing,  stymie  citizen-led
progress  toward  racizl  equality.

Indeed,  applicab:  lity of the  doctrine  has  proven  to
be  somewhat  vexatio 1s for courts  as  the  ground  below
the  legal  landscape  of the  doctrine  has  continued  to
shift.

Under  the  political-process  doctrine,  a  state  “may
no  more  disadvantage  any  particular  group  by mak-
ing  it  more  difficult  to  enact  legislation  in  its  behalf
than  it may  dilute  ary  person’s  vote  or  give  any  group
a  smaller  representation  than  another  of comparable
size,”  Hunter,  at  39%.  In  Hunter,  the  City  of Akron,
Ohio,  sought  to  enact  a  fair-housing  scheme  to

5)

ameliorate  substandard  housing,  which  also  acted  as
an  agent  of  racial  segregation.  As  this  Court  in
Hunter  observed,  the  scheme  was:

[P]remised  on  a  recognition  of the  social  and
economic  losses  to  society  which  flow  from
substandard,  ghetto  housing  and  its  tenden-
cy  to  breed  discrimination  and  segregation
contrary  to  the  policy  of  the  city  to  assure
equal  opportunity  to  all  persons  to  live  in  de-
cent  housing  facilities  regardless  of race,  col-
or,  religion,  ancestry  or  national  origin.  A
Commission  on  Equal  Opportunity  in  Hous-
ing  was  established  by the  ordinance  in  the
office  of  the  Mayor  to  enforce  the  anti-
of  the  ordinance
discrimination
through  conciliation  or  persuasion  if  possi-
ble,  but,  if not,  then  through  such  order  as
the  facts  warrant,  based  upon  a  hearing  at
which  witnesses  may  be  subpoenaed,  and  en-
titled  to  enforcement  in the  courts.

sections

Hunter  at  386.  |Internal  citations  omitted. |

Akron  voters,  though,  subsequently  approved  an
amendment  to  the  city’s  charter  which  prohibited  the
city  from  enacting  any  ordinance  regulating  “the  use,
sale,  advertisement,  transfer,
listing  assignment,
lease,  sublease  or  financing  of  real  property  of  any
kind  or  of  any  interest  therein  on  the  basis  of  race,
color,  religion,  national  origin  or  ancestry  (without
first  being)  approved  by  a  majority  of  the  electors
voting  on  the  question  at  a  regular  or  general  election
before  said  ordinance  shall  be  effective.”  Hunter  at
387.  The  amendment,  Akron  City  Charter  § 137,  was

6

also  retroactive  such  that  it directly  affected  the  city’s
ameliorative  fair-housing  scheme.

The  result  in  Hunter,  an  8-1  decision,  was  that
this  Court  determined  :he  charter  amendment  violated
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  because  the  amendment
stripped  government  of  an  ability  (the  fair-housing
scheme)  to  thwart  discrimination  in  housing,  which
discrimination  was  racially  based.

More  than  a  decade  later  in  a  much  closer  deci-
sion  (5-4),  this  Court  n  Seattle  extended  application
of  the  political-process  doctrine  to  a  statewide  initia-
tive  approved  by  the  state  of  Washington's  voters,
Initiative  350,  that  proscribed  any  school  district’s
use  of mandatory  assignments  of students  outside  of
their  loca]  districts  to  achieve  racial  integration  in
schools.”

The  Seattle  court  relied  almost  entirely  in  its
decision  on  Hunter  to  extend  the  doctrine  to  the
initiative's  prohibitory  effect  and  declared  it unconsti-
tutionally  violative  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause.

[Bly  specifically  2xempting  from  Initiative
350’s  proscriptions  most  nonracial  reasons
for  assigning  stadents  away  from  their
neighborhood  schcols,  the  initiative  expressly

-  The  provision  at  issue  read:  “[N]o  school  board  ...  shall
directly  or  indirectly  require  any  student  to  attend  a  school
other  than  the  school  which  is  geographically  nearest  or  next
.  and  which  offers  the
nearest  the  student's  place  of residence  .
.  .” Seattle  at 462.
course  of study  pursued  by such  student.  .

.

7

requires  those  championing  school  integra-
tion  to surmount  a considerably  higher  hurdle
than  persons  seeking  comparable  legislative
action.  As  in  Hunter,  then,  the  community’s
political  mechanisms  are  modified  to  place
effective  decisionmaking  authority  over  a  ra-
cial  issue  at  a  different  level  of government.
In  a  very  obvious  sense,  the  initiative  thus
“disadvantages  those  who  would  benefit  from
as
laws  barring  de  facto  desegregation
against  those  who  ...  would  otherwise  regu-
late”  student  assignment  decisions;  the  reali-
ty  is  that  the  law’s  impact  falls  on  the
minority.

Seattle  at  474-475,  citing  Hunter  at  391.

In this  case  the  court  below  extended  Hunter  and
Seattle  into  the  realm  of using  race  as  a  “plus  factor”
in  college  admissions  as  though  the  use  of race  were
mandatory  to  achieve  a  compelling  governmental
interest  (racial  diversity  of the  student  body),  relying
on  this  Court’s  decision  in  Grutter  v.  Bollinger,  539
U.S.  306  (2003).

As  the  Sixth  Circuit  framed  the  issue,

the  sole  issue  before  us  is whether  Proposal  2
runs  afoul  of the  constitutional  guarantee  of
equal  protection  by  removing  the  power  of
university  officials  to  even  consider  using
race  as  a  factor  in  admissions  decisions  —
something  they  are  specifically  allowed  to  do
under  Grutter.

8

Coalition  to  Defend  Affirmative  Action,  Integration  &
Immigrant  Rights,  et  cl.  v.  Regents  of Univ.  of Michi-
gan,  08-1387,  2012  WL  5519918  (6th  Cir.  Nov.  15,
2012)  (emphasis  original).

This  Court  determuned  in  Grutter  that  the  Uni-
versity  of Michigan’s  use  of multiple  criteria  —  not just
race  alone  —  justified  the  university’s  use  of race  as  a
“plus”  factor  to  achieve  a  diverse  law  student  popula-
tion.

The  entire  gist of the  analysis  in Grutter  was
that  the  admissio1s  program  at  issue  there
focused  on  each  applicant  as  an  individual,
and  not  simply  as  a  member  of a  particular
racial  group.  The  :lassification  of applicants
by race  upheld  in (srutter  was  only  as  part  of
a  “highly  individuz.lized,  holistic  review.”

Parents  Involved  in  Community  Schools  v.  Seattle
School  District  No.  1,  et  al.,  551  U.S.  701,  722-723
(2007),  quoting  Grutter  at  337.  Thus,  whereas  in
Hunter  and  Seattle,  rece  was  the  only  consideration
when  applying  the  poli:ical-process  doctrine,  the  court
below  ignores  the  “holistic”  character  of  Grutter’s
reasoning.

Therefore,  amici  ask  this  Court  to  grant  Petition-
er’s  application  to  settle  the  question  of the  extent  to
which  Grutter  —  if at  al  —  affects  the  political-process
doctrine  and  determine  that  the  doctrine  is not  appli-
cable  to  MicH.  Const.  art.  I, § 26.

9

Il.  Without  this  Court’s  guidance  to  resolve
the  dispute  among  the  circuits,  colleges
and  would-be  students  face  the  menace
of  misapplication  of  the  political  process
doctrine
of
measures  to  end  racial  preferences.

as  more

approve

states

This  Court  should  grant  Petitioner’s  application
because  numerous  states,  including  those  within  and
without  the  Ninth  and  Sixth  Circuit  courts,’  have
already  approved  measures  to  end  racial  preferences;
as  such,  without  this  Court’s  guidance,  other  circuit
courts  are  highly  likely  to  apply  (or  misapply)  the
political-process  doctrine  and  thus  cause  disparate
treatment  of  college  applicants  based  on  race.  As
such,  Petitioner’s  application  raises  a  timely,  im-
portant  question  of law.

This  Court  will  review  important  questions  of
law  arising  in  separate  cases  decided  differently
among  the  circuits.  See  Tapia  v.  United  States,  564
U.S.  __  (2011),  resolving  a  split  among  circuit  courts
to  determine  whether  sentencing  courts  can  impose
prison  sentences  longer  than  normal  in  order  to
provide  drug  treatment;  AT&T  v.  Hulteen,  556  U.S.
701,  707  (2009),  resolving  a  split  to determine  whether
reliance  on  a  certain  pension  benefit  rule  constituted
a  violation  of Title  VII;  Jrizarry  v  United  States,  553
U.S.  708  (2008),  resolving  a  split  to determine  whether

*  See  Petitioner’s  brief,  at  3.

10

Feb.  R.  Crim.  P.  3201)  applies  to  sentencing  varia-
tions.

In  this  case,  Petitioner  asks  that  this  Court
resolve  the  distinct  split  between  the  Ninth  Circuit’s
decision  in  Coalition  for  Economic  Equity  v.  Wilson
and  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  decision  below,  especially  with
respect  to  the  reach  cf  the  political  process  doctrine
and  in light  of this  Court’s  decision  in Grutter  and  the
Ninth  Circuit’s  recent  decision  in  Coalition  to  Defend
Affirmative  Action  v.  .3rown,  674  F.3d  1128  (9th  Cir.
2012),  which  reaffirmed  its holding  in Wilson.

4

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  amici  respectfully  ask
that  this  Court  grant  Petitioner’s  application  for  Writ
of Certiorari.

Hon.  David  M.  Lawson

The  Confidential  Deposition  of  SARAH  ZEARFOSS,

ll

[Page  208]
{Q. Mr.  Potchen}
{A. Witness  Sarah  Zearfoss}

A.  Well,  the  ‘07  policy  is  the  one  that’s  currently  in
effect,  yes.

Q.  And  that’s  where  I'm  going to find  what  the criteria
is considered  for  students  being  admitted  to  the  law
school?

A.  Well,  what  you'll  see  there  is  a  rather  general
description  of the  type  of class  we  hope  to  matriculate.
You  won't  find  a list of particular  criteria.  In general,  I
would  say  we  consider  everything  in the universe  that’s
not  illegal  for  me  to  consider.

Q.  Okay.
That  does  answer  one  of  my  questions
ahead,  but  after  the  policy  is  set,  what  is the  process
for changing  the  policy?

A.  The  faculty  committee  could  choose  to  initiate  a
change  to  the  policy.
If  I was  unhappy  or  worried
about  the way the policy was  being effectuated,  I would
go to the committee  or  to the  dean  and  suggest  that  we
do that,  or  the  dean  himself  could  ask  that  the  faculty
committee  consider  a  new  policy.

Q.  Okay.  So  the  decision  can  be  made  by the  faculty
committee?

A.  Mm-hmm.

Q.  You  can  go  to  the  faculty  committee  and  ask  them
to change  it; correct?

[Page  209]
A.  Yes.

Q.  The  dean  can  go  to  the  faculty  committee  and  ask
them  to  change  it?

A.  Yes.

Q.  You  can’t  change  it on  your  own  or  the  dean  can’t
change  it on  their  own;  correct?

A.  Correct.

Q.  Does  the  law  school  have  a  procedure  to  allow
individuals  who  are  not  part  of that  faculty  committee
to  petition  the  faculty  committee  to  change  the  policy?

A.  We  have  no  set  procedure,  no.

Q.  Has  anyone  ever  gone  to  the  faculty  admissions
committee  to  ask  them  to  include  a  specific  factor  in
their  admissions  policies?

MR.  WASHINGTON:  Objection.  You're  speaking

as  --  when  you  say  ever,  I'd  object  to  foundation.

Okay.

BY  MR.  POTCHEN:
Q.
Since  you  have  been  the  director  of
admissions,  at  least  since  2001,  has  anyone  ever
petitioned  the  faculty  admissions  committee  to include
a  specific  factor  in the  admissions  policy?

A.  Other  law  faculty  have  approached  members  of the
faculty  committee  and  me  since  Proposal  2 was  voted

13

[Page  210}
upon  suggesting  various  approaches  that  we  should
take.

Q.  How  many  people  have  done  that?

A.  I could  think  of three  off the  top  of my  head,  and
there  very  well  could  be  more  whom  I'm  forgetting.

Q.  And  those  would  be three  faculty  members;  correct?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Has  any  student  or  prospective  student  gone  and
requested  the faculty  admissions  committee  to consider
a  factor  in their  admissions  policy?

A.  Students  have  indicated,  you  know,  that  they  want
us  to do certain  things  with  regard  to  the  policy,  yes,  |
guess  they have.  Never  in writing,  usually  in meetings
that  I can  recall.

Q.  Since  Proposal  2 has  passed,  are  students  barred
from  going  to  the  faculty  admissions  committee  and
seeking  a  change  in the  policy?

A.  No.

Q.  Since  Proposal  2  has  passed,  are  faculty  barred
from  going  to the  faculty  admissions  committee  to  seek
a  change  in the  policy?

A.  No.

Q.  And  you  said  some  proposals  were  presented  to you
by faculty  members  to  change  the  policy?

14

A.  Yes.

[Page  211]
Q.  What  were  those  proposals?

A.  Oh,  they’re  really  complicated.  Ideas  that  people
had  that  weren't  particularly  practical.  I recall  that
one  person  said  that  they  basically  didn’t  view
Proposal  2 as  changing  anything,  that  if I recall  the
logic  it  was  that  race,  you  know  --  the  language  of
Proposal  2 I think  uses  preferential  treatment  on  the
basis  --  is that  night,  preferential  treatment?  And  her
logic  was  that  race  was  one  of  many  factors,  and,
therefore,  wasn’t  being preferred.  and,  therefore,  there
was  no  reason  whatsoever  to  change  the  policy  in  the
wake  of  Proposal  2.  So  that  was  one  proposal,  and
then  there  are  various  --  there  are  variations  on  that
theme.

Q.  Has  Proposal  2 changed  anyone’s  ability  to  petition
the  faculty  admissions  committee  to  include  a  specific
factor  in the  admissions  policy?

A.  Not  that  I can  think  of,  no.

Q.  If the  law  school  declines  to include  a specific  factor
in its admissions  policy,  is there  some  sort  of an  appeal
process?

A.  Well,  there’s  no  formalized  process  for  any  of this,
so  we  could  keep  getting  e-mails  over  and  over  on  the
same  subject,  for  example.

Q.  If someone  saw  this  new  policy,  Exhibit  No.  1, this

[Page  212]
March  23,  2007  policy,  and  they felt  that  it was  --  that
there  should  be  a  factor  included  in  it;  let’s  say,  for
example,  you  should  include  people  missing  a  limb  or
something  like  that,  and  the  faculty  admissions
committee  didn't  agree  with  them.  could  they appeal to
a higher  body and  say,  hey,  I want  that  policy changed
or  I want  to  include a factor?

MR.  NIEHOFF:  Joe,  I’m  going  to  interpose  an
objection just based  on  the  word  appeal  because  I don't
know  whether  by  that  you're  signifying  a  formal
process,  or  whether  you're  asking  is there  some  other
body  that  they could  go  to  that  could  order  the  faculty
committee  to doit.  I don’t  know  whether  it makes  any
several
word
difference,
connotations  to me  and  I'm just not  sure  which  one  vou
mean.

appeal

has_

but

the

MR.  POTCHEN:  I can  appreciate  that  objection.

BY  MR.  POTCHEN:
Q.  I guess  that  is  my  question,  though.  If somebody
wasn't  happy  with  the  policy,  could  they  go to another
higher  body  and  seek  a  change  in the  policy?

A.  | would  imagine  they could  ask  anybody,  right.  The
dean,  you  know,  has  authority  over  the faculty  in many
ways,  and  he himself  doesn’t  change  the  policy,  but  he

[Page  213]
does  have  authority  over  faculty,  and  so  he  I guess  is a
higher  body  from  that  context.

Q.  Okay.  Who  could  change  the  policy  other  than  the
faculty  admissions  committee?

16

A.  No  one  that  | --  well,  the  faculty  itself.  I mean  the
faculty admissions  committee  would  propose  something
to  the  faculty  and  the  faculty  would  vote  upon  it.  You
know,  | should  caution  that  in  the,  you  know,  the
minutia  of  faculty  governance.  I'm  sure  there  are
exceptions,  so  if  there  was  some  change  that  was
required  that  was  verv  minor,  I think  the dean  and the
faculty  committee  might  say  we're  comfortable  just
doing  this  without  taking  it to  the  faculty  for  a  vote.
You  know,  we'll  just  make  this  small  change  that
involves  making  sure  that  someone's  name  is stamped
on  a  file  four  times  or  something  like  that.  I don't
know.  Just  something  that  doesn’t  have  much  to  do
with  substance  --

Q.  But  I’m  looking  more  toward  substantive  change.

A.  But  of substance,  the  whole  faculty  has  to  vote  on
it, yes.

Q.  Okay.  Now  let’s  say  the  faculty  votes  on  it  and
someone  is  unhappy  with  that  decision.  Could  they
bring  it to  a  higher  authority  for  a  change?

MR.  NIEHOFF:  Let  me  interpose  --

Q. Okay.  All  right.  Can  you  tell  me  or  describe  for  me
the  process  by which  revisions  to the  September  19th,
2002  Admissions  Policies  and  Procedures  resulting  in
this  current  document  would  take  place,  what’s  the
process”

A. To  make  sure  | understand,  are  you  referring  to  the
process  prior  to  September  19th,  2002?

Q. No.  I want  to  know  the  process  that  was  utilized  or,
utilized  to  make  the  revisions  to  this  particular,  to  the
policy,  as  reflected  in this current  copy  May 24th,  2007.

A. The  process  to change  or  update  Admission  Policies
and  Procedures  is  for  the  AEC  to  recommend  any
changes  or  updates  to the Assistant  Dean  of Admission
who  would  carry  them  forward  to  the  Associate  Dean
for  Student  Programs,  who  would  carry  them  forward
to  the  Executive  Committee  for  their  input  and
subsequent  approval.

A.  Well,  certainly  anyone  who  would  like  to  do  that
has  that  opportunity.
Its  very  rare,  however,  that
those  kind  of  situations  occur,  at  least  for  me.  My

people  who  have  come  to  me  to request  changing  of the
overall  mission  statement  of LS&A  or  engineering.

[Page  235]
to be considered  for  purposes  of reviewing  admissions

1.  My name  is William  G.  Bowen.  | have  agreed  to

2.1  am  currently  President  Emeritus  of  the
Andrew  W.  Mellon  Foundation,  a  charitable  grant-
making  institution  with  special  focus  on  higher
education  and  scholarship.  I have  held  this  position
since  2006.  From  1988  to 2006,  I served  as  President  of
the  Andrew  W.  Mellon  Foundation.  Prior  to  that,  I
served  as  President  of Princeton  University  for sixteen
years  and  as  Provost  for five years.  I was  a Professor  of
Economics  at  Princeton  University  from  1965  until
1988,  having  been  member  of the  faculty  since  1958.  I
have  served  as  a  member  of several  corporate  boards,
including  American  Express  and  Merck  & Co.,  Inc.

3.

Lhave  written  extensively  about  issues  in higher

Derek  Bok,  I  have  conducted  extensive  original
research  on  the  impact  of race-conscious  admissions
policies  in  American  higher  education.  Many  of  the
conclusions  we  drew  from  this  study  are  set  forth  in

6.  Since  its inception,  the  consideration  of race  in
university
an _ important
contributor  to  the  socioeconomic  mobility  and  well-

admissions

8.  Many  successful  African-American  students
would  have  been  rejected  by their  alma  mater  under  a
race-neutral  admissions  regime.

9.  Minority  students  who  have  benefited  from  the
consideration  of  race  in  university  admissions  have
made  a  clear  impact  in  the  political  and  professional
world.  There  are,  for  instance,  a  growing  number  of
African-American  doctors  and lawyers  practicing  in the
United  States.  In  addition,  the  number  of  African-
American  elected  officials  in  this  country  has  risen
dramatically  since  the  first  race-conscious  admissions
programs  were  implemented.

38

10.  My  research  with  Derek  Bok  also  shows  that
race-conscious  admissions  programs  have  had  an
important  impact  upon  economic  outcomes  for  racial
minorities,  including  per  capita  earnings.

11.  The  available  evidence  indicates  that  race-
neutral  alternatives  are  unlikely  to  be  as  effective  as
race-conscious  admissions  in  enrolling  a  diverse
student  body.

12.  In  particular,  income-based  strategies  are
unlikely  to  be  good  substitutes  for  race-conscious
admissions  policies.  Although  African  Americans,
Latinos  and  Native  Americans  are  overrepresented  in
the  country’s  poor  population,  the  great  majority  of
that  population  is  still  white.  Children  from  poor
African-American  and  Latino  families  make  up  less
than  half of all  poor  children.

13.  Thus,  according  to  my  analysis  published  in
Equity  and  Excellence,  moving  from  race-conscious
admissions
would
substantially  reduce  the  minority  enrollments  at
selective  institutions,  and  severely  impair  current
efforts  to  achieve  racial  diversity.

class-based

policy

DECLARATION  OF  KRISTINA  WILFORE
Cantrell  v.  Granholm,  No.  06-15637  (E.D.  Mich.)

I. Background  and  expertise.

1.  My  name  is  Kristina  Wilfore.  I  submit  this
Declaration  in  support  of  the  Cantrell  Plaintiffs’
motion  for summary  judgment.  If needed,  I will  submit
an  expert  report  at  the  appropriate  time  setting  forth
in full  my  areas  of expertise,  opinions  and  bases.

2.  I  received  a  Bachelor's  Degree  in  Political
Science  and  Broadcasting  from  Washington  State
University  in  1995.  I received  a  Master’s  Degree  in
Public  Administration
of
Washington  in  1998.

University

from

the

3.  Since  April  2002,  I have  been  the  Executive
Director  of  Ballot  Initiative  Strategy  Center,  Inc.
(“BISC”).  BISC,  a  non-profit  organization  located  in
Washington  D.C.,  provides  education,  training,  and
research  to  a  wide  range  of groups  on  the  voter  ballot
initiative  process.  BISC  studies  initiative  trends,
monitors  major campaigns  and  donor  activities,  assists
committees  and  organizations  promoting  progressive
initiatives  and  publishes  a  comprehensive  Campaign
Manual  on  the  topic of ballot  initiatives.  In my  position
as  Executive  Director,  I am  responsible  for  strategic
planning  and  implementation  of programs;  acting  as
chief  contact  to  the  Board  of  Directors;  directing
national  initiative  strategies;  being  the  principle
contact  for  the  media;  raising  and  overseeing  the
organization’s  budget;  providing  staff management  and
and
oversight
consultants;  directing  political  programs;  building  state
campaigns;
coalitions

and  working

employees

fulltime

BISC’s

state

with

to

40

maintaining  BISC’s  organizational  profile  within  and
outside  of Washington,  DC,  and;  shaping  polling  and
research  projects.

4.  In  the  past  five  years.  I  have  worked  on
approximately  150  ballot  initiative  campaigns  in  30
different  states  in  the  United  States.  From  2002  to
2004,  I worked  on  seven  ballot  initiative  campaigns  in
the  state  of Michigan.

5. My role  as  Executive  Director  of BISC  places  me
in  a  unique  position  to  opine  on  ballot  initiative
campaigns.  To  my  knowledge,  BISC  is  the  only
organization  in  the  United  States  that  conducts
research  on  multi-issue,  multi-state  voter  initiative
campaigns.  Other  organizations  that  research  ballot
initiative  campaigns  or  that  are  involved  in campaign
advocacy  tend  to  consider  single  state,  single  issue
campaigns  or  are  academic  and  research  groups  that
examine  narrow  aspects  of the  voter  ballot  initiative
process  (e.g., turnout,  election  effect,  civic  engagement,
expansion  of  the  process).  BISC,  by  contrast,  is
involved  in  every  phase  of  the  initiative  camps  gn
process,  including  pre-campaign  evaluation  regarding
initiative  viability.

Il.  Statewide  ballot  initiative  campaigns  are
lengthy,  complex,  difficult  and  expensive.

6. Statewide  voter  ballot  initiatives  are  in use  in 24
states  and the District  of Columbia.  Although  the voter
ballot  initiative  mechanism  is contained  in each  state's
constitution,  some  aspects  of the  initiative  process  are
governed  by  statute  (e.g.  signature  requirements,
timing  deadlines,  requirements  on  initiative  petition
formatting).

41

7. Measures  may  be either  direct  or  indirect.  Under
the direct  initiative,  a measure  is put directly  to a vote
after  being submitted  by a petition.  Under  the indirect
initiative,  a  measure  is first  referred  to the legislature,
and  then  put  to  a  popular  vote  only  if not  enacted  by
the  legislature.

of  countering

8. Voter  ballot  initiatives  were  initially  promoted
by civic-minded  reformers  in the  early  20th  century  as
governmental
a  means
corruption  and  enabling  ordinary  citizens  to  shape
public  policy.  Alongside  these  goals,  reformers  also
considered  that  the  initiative  process  had  an  educative
value,  encouraging  citizens  to  become  more  civically
and politically  engaged  with  the process  of government.

endemic

9. Voter  ballot  initiatives  today  have  dramatically
different  goals  from  those  that  motivated  their
inception.  First,  ballot  initiatives  are  no  longer  only
about  allowing  citizens  to  shape  public  policy.  Ballot
initiative  sponsors  today  often  choose  to  insert
controversial  or  “wedge”  issues  into  elections  in  order
to  drive  certain  voters  to  the  polls  and  affect  the
electoral  result.  Second,  because  issue  committees  may
raise  unlimited  sums  of  “soft”  money,  candidates
running  for statewide  office  increasingly  use  initiatives
to  provide  alternative  electoral  vehicles  that  are
unconstrained  by  candidate  campaign  contribution
limits.

10.  Today,  in  every  state  that  has  a  voter  ballot
initiative,  campaigns  are  generally  lengthy,  complex,
difficult  and  expensive.  Although  originally  conceived
as  a  means  for ordinary  citizens  to  involve  themselves
directly  in  the  political  process,  ballot  initiative

42

campaigns  have  become  highly  professional  and
managed,  benefiting  organized  and well-funded  groups.

11.  The  length  of a  ballot  initiative  campaign  may
run  as  long  as  two  to  three  years  if  one  counts
coalition-building  and  research  before  filing a measure,
although  most  campaigns  take  approximately  a  year
from  start  to  finish.

12.

Successful

require
initiative
connections  with  a  network  of  supporters,  political
capital  with  the  major  players  in  the  relevant  state,
and  access  to  significant  financial  resources  as  well  as
a  substantial  base  of willing  volunteer  supporters.

campaigns

13.  In  order  to  have  any  chances  of  success,
individuals  and  groups  seeking  to  enact  laws  through
ballot  initiatives  must  go  through  a  lengthy  and
complex  process  that  can  be considered  in three  stages:
(1)  the  initial  stage,  where  proponents  of a  measure
must  decide  whether  they  have  a  viable  proposal;  (2)
the  signature-gathering  stage,  where  a  requisite
number  of  voter  signatures  are  collected  in  order  to
secure  the  initiative’s  placement  on  the  ballot;  and  (3)
the  voting  stage,  where  the  measure  is  submitted  to
the  electorate.

A.  Initiating  Stage.

14.  The  initial  stage  of a  ballot  initiative  campaign
involves  a  series  of  processes,  including  (among
others):

*  conducting  policy  research  to  evaluate  the
validity  of the  proposal;

43

-  building  a  support  coalition  by  contacting
donors  and  other  stakeholders:

*  pre-polling  fundraising;

*  polling  to  determine  the  popularity  of the
proposal.  At  a minimum,  pre-campaign  polling
results  should  show  support  of  between
approximately  60-70%  of the  electorate.  The
industry  standard  for such  pre-campaign  polls
involves  taking  a  representative  sample  of
likely  voters.  Sample  sizes  range  from  400  to
of
700  persons  with  a  margin  of  error
approximately  3%;

*  testing  possible  ballot  language  through  the
use  of focus  groups;

*  preparing  petition  language;  and

submitting  proposed  petition  language,
*
summaries  and/or  titles  to the designated  state
authorities  for  approval  or  certification  as
required  by applicable  state  law.

15.  Depending  on  the  complexity  and  controversy
of the  issue  being  undertaken,  the  initial  stage  of  a
ballot  initiative  campaign  can  take  anywhere  from  six
months  to  two  years  of advance  work.

16.  At the  end  of the  initial  stage  the  state  advises
the  proponents  whether  they  are  allowed  to  begin
circulating  the  petition.  The  signature-gathering  stage
commences  once  the  state  approves  the  petition.

44

B.  Signature-Gathering  Stage.

17.  The  signature-gathering  stage  of the  initiative
is  both  difficult  and  expensive.  All  states  require  a
certain  number  of  valid  signatures  to  place  an
initiative  on  the ballot.  Most  states  require  between  5-
10%  of  the  voters  who  voted  in  the  last  election  or
voted  for a  particular  race,  such  as  governor,  although
that  number  can  be higher  or  lower  depending  on  the
that  allow  both  statutory  and
state.
constitutional  initiatives  there  is  usually  a  different
number  of  signatures  needed,  with  more  being
required  for  a  constitutional  amendment.  Michigan
requires  signatures  from  10%  of the  total  vote  cast  for
all  candidates  for  governor  in  the  last  preceding
general  election  in  which  governor  was  elected  for  a
constitutional  amendment.

In  states

firms.

Gathering  signatures

18.  Signature  gathering  is  done  primarily  by
professional
is  an
expensive  and  timely  proposition,  especially  if  the
timeline  is relatively  short.  Signature  gathering  drives
can  take  months  and  most  campaigns  do  not  have
enough  volunteers  who  are  willing  to commit  the hours
needed  to  complete  a  drive.  There  are  a  number  of
signature  gathering  firms  in the  U.S.,  including  but not
limited  to  National  Voter  Outreach,  National!  Petition
and  Democracy
Management,  PCI
Resources.

Consultants

19.  The  success  of obtaining  a sufficient  number  of
signatures  depends  on  a  number  of factors,  including:

-  the  number  of  other  competing  measures
that  season;

45

*  the  use  of paid  media  campaigns:

*  the  cost  of gathering  signatures.  which  can
range  from  $1.25  to  $6.00  per  signature;  and

*
the  need  to  gather  at  least  20%  more
signatures  than  required  to account  for invalid
or  duplicative  signatures.

20.  Proponents  of  ballot  initiatives  may  have  to
contend  with  legal  disputes  involving  signature
verification.  States  such  as  Michigan  coilect  a  sample
number  of  signatures  in  order  to  measure  validity.
When  the  statistical  sampie,  which  determines  the
error  rate,  is  available  for  review,  the  Board  of  the
State  of  Canvassers  sets  deadlines  for  objections.
Individuals  may  inspect  these  signatures  or  pay  the
costs  to copy  them  in order  to challenge  the  signatures
in the  sample.

C. The  Voting  Stage.

21.  Putting  the  ballot  initiative  to  the  electorate
increases  complexity  and costs  of the  initiative  process
significantly.  This  stage  of  the  campaign  requires
considerably  more  volunteers,  staff  and  funding.

22.  The  greatest  expenditure  of  funds  is  on
television  advertising,  which  is  essential  to  anv
campaign.  In  addition  to  television
advertising,
campaigns  use  a  variety  of methods  simultaneously  to
reach  the  electorate,  including  direct  mail,  phone  calls
and  canvassing.

23.  In some  cases,  proponents  of  a measure  may  be
forced  to spend  additional  funds  to defend  the  measure

46

from  legal  challenges  after  it  has  already  been
approved  by the  electorate.

24.  The  2006  election  cycle  was  the  third  most
active  cycle for ballot  initiatives  since  the first  measure
was  proposed  in  1904.  Voters  decided  204  statewide
measures  in 37 states  in that cycle.  Seventy-nine  of the
204  measures  were  citizen-petitioned  and  125 referred
by the  government  (legislative  referenda).  There  were
22  more  initiatives  on  the  ballot  than  in  2004.  Voters
approved  29  initiatives  and  108  legislative  referenda.

25.  According  to  industry  sources,  not  only has  the
number  of  initiatives  increased,  but  so  has  the
intensity,  coordination,  and  money  spent.  While  2004
was  a  record-breaking  year  with  $393  million  spent  on
ballot  initiatives,  last  election  cycle  just  twelve  of the
most  expensive  initiatives
in  the  country  spent
$349,239,173.  That  figure  includes  the  most  expensive
initiative  campaign  ever  -  $153  million  spent  by
proponents  and  opponents  on  California’s  renewable
energy  campaign.

26.  With  all  the  money  spent,  very  few  proposed
initiatives  make  it to  the  ballot,  and  less  than  half  of
those  pass.  Incorporating  results  from  elections
through  2006,  industry  data  indicate  that  a  total  of
2212  initiatives  have  qualified  for the  ballot  since  the
process  began.  Less  than  half  of  all  measures  have
been  approved  by voters;  900  of the  measures  passed,
or  40.7%.  The  passage  rate  of the  2006  initiatives  was
lower  than  the  historical  average,  as  only  29  out  of 79
initiatives,  or  36.7%,  were  approved  by voters.

47

Ill.  The  Michigan  ballot  initiative  process  is
particularly  onerous  in  comparison  to  ballot
initiative  processes  in other  states.

27.  The  ballot  initiative  process  in  Michigan
contains  various  features  that  make  it  particularly
onerous,  expensive  and burdensome  when  compared  to
other  states.

28.  Michigan's  ballot  initiative  process  is set  out  in

the  Michigan  State  Constitution.

29. Michigan  is a politically  competitive  state,  with
expensive  media  markets  and  a  large  number  of
initiatives  vying  for  voters’  attention  on  any  given
ballot.

30. The  Michigan  media  market  is expensive  when
compared  to other  ballot  initiative  states.  In Michigan,
measures  can  cost  as  much  as  $10-$15  million  with  $5
million  being  a  practical  minimum.  Media  costs  alone
can  account  for as  much  as  $4 million.

31.  Michigan  requires  a large  amount  of signatures
due  to  the  size  of its  population.  The  Constitution  of
Michiga.  requires  signatures  from  10%  of  the  total
vote  cast  for  all  candidates  for  governor  in  the  last
preceding  general  election  in  which  governor  was
elected.  In  addition,  a  20%  safety  margin  must  be
added
and _ duplicative
signatures.  Signature  gathering  alone  can  cost  from
$750,000  to  $1  million.

to  account

invalid

for

32.  Michigan  has an  unusually  short  time  frame  for
the  gathering  of  signatures  and  filing  of  petitions.
Petitions  must  be  filed  at  least  120  days  before  the

48

election  at  which  the  proposed  amendment  is  to  be
voted  on.  The  circulation  period  is limited  to  180  days.
Other  states,  such  as  Oregon  and  Arizona,  allow  as
long  as  two  years.  This  means  that  in  Michigan
proponents  must  submit  signatures  gathered  within
the  same  180  day period.  The  short  circulation  period
adds  to  the  cost  of  gathering  signatures  because  it
requires  the  use  of  a  greater  number  of  circulators
(who  are  commercially  engaged  to  gather  signatures)
and  adds  an  urgencv  premium  by  requiring  the
gathering  of signatures  within  a  comparatively  short
time  frame.  In  addition,  Michigan  law  requires
circulators  to  have  resided  in Michigan  for  at  least  30
days  in  order  to  circulate  a  petition,  limiting  the
number  of available  qualified  circulators.

33. Anyone  seeking  to have  a voter  ballot  initiative
passed  in Michigan  in 2008  will  also  have  to  take  into
account  the  proposed  reforms  to  Michigan  law
regarding  voter  ballot  initiatives.  These  reforms,
originally  requested  by  the  Michigan  Board  of
Canvassers  and  submitted  to  the  Secretary  of State,
are  currently  with  the  Michigan  legislature  for
consideration.  The  reforms,  if accepted,  will  make  the
voter  ballot  initiative  process  in Michigan  more  costly
and  time  consuming.

QOverturning

burdensome.

34.  Any  individual  or  group  seeking  to  mount  a
campaign  to overturn  Proposal  2 must  not  only face the
particular  difficulties  of the ballot  initiative  process  in
Michigan  but  also  the  peculiar  and  unique  problems

49

associated  with  pro-affirmative  action  ballot  initiatives
and  minority  protection  measures  generally.

35. Two factors  in particular  make  the possibility  of
seeking  a  successful  pro-affirmative  action  voter  ballot
initiative
onerous,
expensive  and  burdensome.

extraordinarily

in  Michigan

36.  First,  the  subject  matter  involved  in  an
affirmative  action  campaign  places  proponents  of
affirmative  action  at  a  particular  disadvantage  as
compared  to most  other  political  issues.  One  reason  for
this  is  that  polling  data  is  unreliable  in  this  area,
which  adds  to  the  cost  and  complexity  of  developing
successful  proposal  language  and predicting  successful
strategies  for targeting  the  electorate  through  the  use
of focus  groups.  In my  experience,  citizens  do  not  vote
predictably  when  it  comes  to  race  issues  in  polling
booths  when  compared  to  providing  data  for  pollsters.
Another  reason  for  the  particular  disadvantage  faced
by proponents  of affirmative  action  measures  is  that
these  measures  are  particularly  difficult  to  market
because  they  tend  to  elicit  highly  emotive  responses
from  voters.  Voters  often  mistakenly  link  these
measures  in their  minds  to broader  politically  charged
issues,  not  the  subject  of  the  measure,  such  as
questions  of  unfair  economic  advantages.  In  this
regard,  ballot  initiative  campaigns  benefit  proponents
who have  simple  messages  rather  than  those  who have
messages,  such  as  pro-affirmative  action  campaigns,
which  are,  by their  nature,  more  complex.

37.  Second,  in  the  context  of  having  just  lost
Proposition  2, there  is no  natural  constituency  among
pro-affirmative  action  groups  in  Michigan  that  would
form  an  appropriate  base  for  those  groups  to

50

financially  coalesce.  There  is  no  single  obvious
financial  benefactor  in  Michigan  who  would  support
the  pro-affirmative  action  position.

38.  Overturning  any  recently  passed  measure
requires  more  efforts,  both  physical  and  financial,  than
seeking  to  approve  a  new  measure.  As  a  result,  it is
extremely  unlikely  that  groups  supporting  affirmative
action  would  commit  any  significant  resources  to a new
measure  in  Michigan.  These  groups  are  much  more
likely  to  expend  their  efforts  and  resources  in  other
states,  such  as  Missouri,  Colorado,  Arizona,  Nebraska
and  Oklahoma,  where  similar  anti-affirmative  action
initiatives  have  been  filed.

39.  As  a  means  of achieving  political  change,  what
was  once  an  attempt  to  involve  citizens  in  the
lawmaking  process
has  since  become  a_  highly
professionai  and  managed  process  by  which  well-
organized  and  well-funded  groups  are  able  to  achieve
change  on  substantively  simple  political  issues.  The
ballot  initiative  campaign  in  any  state  is  extremely
onerous,  complex,  lengthy  and expensive.  The  difficulty
involved  in  the  ballot  process  is  even  more  acute  in
Michigan  due  to  the  particularities  of  the  initiative
process  in that  state.  Proponents  of initiatives  seeking
to  benefit  minorities  on  the  basis  of  race,  such  as
initiatives  dealing  with  affirmative  action,  are  even
more  disadvantaged  because  of the  subject  matter  of
such  initiatives.  A voter  initiative  campaign  targeted
at  overturning  Proposal  2  in  the  area  of  race  in
universities  admissions  faces  overwhelming  odds.

I declare  under  penalty  of perjury  under  the  laws
of  the  United  States  that  the  foregoing  is  true  and

correct.  Executed  this  16'*  day  of November,  2007  in
Washington  D.C.,  District  of Columbia.

The  Viability  of Race-Neutral  Approaches  for
Achieving  Diversity  at  the  University  of
Michigan

Jeannie  Oakes,  Presidential  Professor  in  Education
Equity  Graduate  School  of Education  and
Information  Studies  UCLA

I. Introduction

1 am  Presidential  Professor  in  Educational  Equity  in
UCLA’s  Graduate  School  of Education  and Information
Studies,  Director  of the  University  of California’s  All
Campus  Consortium  on  Research  for  Diversity
(ACCORD);  and  Co-Director  of UCLA’s  Institute  for
Democracy,  Education  & Access  (IDEA.)  My  research
examines  inequalities  in  U.S.  schools,  (including
inequalities  in  access  to  college),  and  follows  the
progress  of equity-minded  reform.  As  detailed  in  my
curriculum  vitae  (Appendix  A),  I am  the  author  of  17
scholarly  books  and  monographs  and  more  than  100
published  research  reports,  chapters,  and  articles.  My
awards  for  this  work  include  the  Distinguished
Achievement  Award  from  the  Educational  Press
Association  of America;  three  major  awards  from  the
American  Educational  Research  Association  (Early
Career
Article;
Outstanding  Book),  and  the  National  Association  for
Multicultural
Education’s  Multicultural  Research
Award.  I am  also  the  recipient  of Southern  Christian
Leadership  Conference’s  Ralph  David  Abernathy
Award  for  Public  Service.  One  of  my  recent  books,
Becoming  Good  American  Schools:  The  Struggle  for
Civic  Virtue  in  Education  Reform  won  AERA’s
Outstanding  Book  Award  in  2001.  In  addition  to  my
scholarly  publications,  I  have  assisted  state  and

Outstanding

Research

Award;

08

national  policymakers  in  developing  equity-focused
education  reform  and  most  recently  served  as  an
advisor  to  Joint  Legislative  Committee  for  the
California  Educational  Master  Plan,  K-University.  I
have  also  conducted  research  and  testified  in  three
school  desegregation  cases—San  Jose,  California;
New
Castle  County,  Delaware;  and  Rockford,  Illinois.  In
addition,  I served  as  an  expert  consultant  in a  fourth
desegregation  case,  the  Hoots  case  in Woodland  Hills,
Pennsylvania  and,  most  recently,  in  the  Williams  v.
California  litigation  focused  on  students’  access  to
basic  learning  opportunities.

Il.  Nature  of the  Assignment

This  report  examines  whether  the  racial  diversity
the  University  of Michigan  requires  can  be  achieved
without  considering  race  in  the  admissions  process.
Specifically,
it  investigates  the  extent  to  which
permitted  race-neutral  university  admissions  policies
are  likely  to  produce  the  diversity  the  university
requires.

III.  Research  Approach

To examine  these  questions,  this report  draws  from
the  experiences  of Texas  and  California  and  contrasts
relevant  circumstances  in  these  states  with  those  in
Michigan.  It also  investigates  admissions  data  from  the
University  of Michigan  about  the  pool  of applicants,
admits,  and enrollees  as  well  as  publicly  available  data
about  Michigan’s  K-  12 schools  to in order  to assess  the
likely  outcomes  of  “race-neutral”  strategies  on  the
diversity  of  freshman  admits  to  the  University  of
Michigan.  These  analyses  consider  the  composition  of
the  2005  pool  of  University  of  Michigan  freshman

of  the

diversity

IV.  Conclusions

A.  Race-neutral  admissions  strategies  (e.g.,  Percent
Plans)  have  not  been  effective  as  replacements  for race-
conscious  strategies  in  maintaining  or  increasing
diversity  in  the  public  universities  in  Texas  and
California,  two  states  with  restrictions  on
race-
conscious  practices  in university  admissions  similar  to
those  required  under  Michigan's  Proposal  2.

that

Plans

Percent

Race-neutral

guarantee
admission  to  the  top  ranked  graduates  of each  public
high  school  have  not  been  successful
in  either
California  or  Texas  in  maintaining  levels  of  racial
diversity  comparable  to  those  achieved  with  race-
conscious  policies  prior  to  the  Hopwood  case  in Texas

61

or  the  passage  of  Proposition  209  in  California  that
made  race-conscious  policies  impermissible.

B. Michigan  differs  from  California  and  Texas  in ways
that  make  it  even  more  unlikely  that  race-neutral
approaches  would  result  in  the  diversity  required  at
the  University  of  Michigan.  In  fact,  such  strategies
would  likely  exclude  from  admission  in-state  African
American  and  Latino  high  school  graduates  with
academic  preparation  sufficient  to  succeed  at  the
university.

in  racial

Most  state  systems  that  ended  race-conscious
admissions  replaced  them  with  “race-neutral”  policies
to attempt  to  address  inequities  in K-12  schooling  that
result
in  higher  education
admission.  The  most  commonly  utilized  approach  is a
Percent  Plan,  which  guarantees  admission  for  a  fixed
percentage  of  students  who  rank  at  the  top  of  their
high  school  class  in  the  state.  The  specifics  of  these

disparities

64

plans  vary  substantially,  for  example,  the  plans  of
California,  Texas  and  Florida  (Horn  and  Flores  2003).
Percent  Plans’  efforts
to  diversify  admission  to
selective  public  universities  rest  on  the  assumption
that  secondary  schools  are  in  large  part  highly
segregated  by race,  and  thus,  top  students  in  a  high-
minority  school  will  have  a  relative  advantage  in
gaining  admission  to  the  state's  flagship  universities.
Other  school-based  “Outreach”  approaches  seek  to
increase  the  academic  performance  and  college  going of
students  at  low  performing  schools  with  academic
preparation  programs,  mentoring,  assistance  in  the
college  applcation  process,  and  other  college-going
supports.
by
changing  the  weights  given  to  various  non-racial
student  characteristics  in  the  admissions  process.

also  responded

Universities

have

Details  about  the  specific  impact  in Cahfornia  and

Texas  are  summarized  below.

California.  The  University  of California  is  made
up  of  ten  campuses  that  offer  undergraduate  and
graduate  education.  It  is  a  highly  selective  system,
targeting  admission  to only the top  12.5%  of the  state's
high  school  graduates  under  California’s  Master  Plan
for  Higher  Education.'!  Students  become  eligible  for

!  For  more  detail,  see  Grodsky  &  Kurlaender  (2006),  The
Demography  of  Higher  Education  in  the  Wake  of  Affirmative
Action,  Paper  Prepared  for the Conference—-Equal  Opportunity  in
Higher  Education:  The  Past  and  Future  of  Proposition  209,
Sponsored  by the  Chief  Justice  Earl  Warren  Institute  on  Race,
Ethnicity,  and  Diversity  at  Boalt  Hall,  University  of California
Berkeley  (October,  2006).  The  1960  Master  Plan  outlines
California's  higher  education  structure  and  articulates  the
University  of  California  (UC)  as  the  state’s  premiere  research
university.

65

the  University  of  California  based  on  a  minimum
grade-point  average  on  a  specified  set  of high  school
courses  (known  as  a-g  courses)  and  defined  scores  on
standardized  admissions  tests  (SAT or ACT).  Students
who  meet  basic  eligibility  are  guaranteed  access  to  a
UC  campus,  although  not  necessarily  one  of  the
applicant’s  choice,  due  to  the  increased  selectivity  of
some  of the  more  competitive  campuses  within  the  UC,
in  particular  Berkeley,  Los  Angeles  and  San  Diego.

Historically,  the  University  of  California  has
engaged  in  affirmative  action  in  order  to  enroll]  a
student  body that  reflects  the  demographic  composition
of  California.  California  is  one  of  the  most  diverse
states  in  the  nation,  where  the  demographics  are
rapidly  changing  as  the  growth  in  the  Latino
population  increases,  and  whites  no  longer  represent
the  majority  of  the  state’s  school-age  population
(Census  Bureau  2000).

The  University  has  worked  to  attain  a  diverse
student  body  through  targeted  outreach  programs  in
K-12,  and  through  the  use  of race/ethnicity  as  a  factor
in  admissions.  Given  significant  racial  disparities  in
the  eligibility  pool  and  on  grades  and  test  scores,  the
most
selective  UC  campuses  employed  a  dual
admissions  process  to  ensure  diverse  enrollment,  one
for
Asians,
for
underrepresented  minority  groups  (Douglass  1999).
The  percentage  of underrepresented  minorities  in the
UC  freshmen  class  overall  grew  from  9.9%  in  1980  to
21.0%  by  1995  (UCOP  Report  2003).

and  = another

whites

and

Changes  in  these  race-conscious  policies,  as  a
result  of  the  UC  Regents’  ban  on  the  use  of  race  in
admissions  in  1995  and  the  passage  of Proposition  209

66

in  1996  have  had  a  significant,  negative  impact  on
minority  applications,  admissions,  and  enrollments.
Specifically,  Proposition  209  prohibited  the  state  from
discriminating
preferential
or
treatment  to  any  individual  or  group  on  the  basis  of
race,  sex,  color,  ethnicity,  or  national  origin  in  the
operation  of public  employment,  public  education,  or
public  contracting.

Although  the  Board  of  Regents  decision  in  1995
and  the  passage  of Proposition  209  in  the  fall  of  1996
did  not  go  into  effect  until  the  entering  class  of  1998,
the  University  of California  witnessed  immediate  drops
in applications  from  underrepresented  groups  (Karabel
1998).  Many  observers  attributed  these  immediate
declines  to  the  “chilling  effect”  resulting  from  the  end
of race-conscious  practices  on  minority  applicants  to
the  University  (Orfield  and  Miller  1998).  In  1995,
21.5%  of UC  applicants  were  from  an  underrepresented
minority  group,  by 1998,  17.5%  of UC  applicants  were
from  an  underrepresented  minority  group.  Minority
apphecant  rates  stabilized  at  about  17%  following  Prop
209  implementation,  and  then  actually  increased  in
subsequent  years,  albeit  not  to  the  pre-209  levels.  The
result  is  that  ten  years  later,  the  representation  of
Latino,  Afmican  American,  and  American  Indian

*  Richard  Atkinson  (President  Emeritus  of  the  University  of
California)  writes,  “|the  Regents  decision  (SP-  1) and  the  passage
of  Proposition  209}  transformed  California  into  a  battleground
fairness,  and
where  opposing  views  of  individual
educational  opportunity  struggled  to  prevail  (and  still  do).  They
also  made  the  University  of California  a case  study  in how  an  elite
public  university.  required  to employ  admissions  policies  that  are
demonstrably  inclusive  and  fair,  responded  to the end  of nearly  30
years  of affirmative  action”  (Atkinson  and  Pelfrey  2004).

merit,

particularly  at  the  UC’s  most  selective  campuses,  has
decreased,  even  as  these  groups  make  up  a  larger
share  of California  high  school  graduates.

minority

15%  (UCOP  Report  2003).  The  drop  at  the  most

1997  and  1998),  the  fraction  of  minorities  in  the  UC
Berkeley  freshmen  class  fell  from  22%  to  12%;  UCLA

effect,

into

By  2002  underrepresented  groups  represented
about  17%  of UC  freshmen  matriculants  (UCOP  Report
2003).  Moreover,  given  the  growth  in the  percentage  of
California  high  school  graduates  who  are  students  of
color,  the  gap  in  UC  enrollment  of underrepresented
groups  widened  considerably.  In  1995,  38.3%  of
California  high  school  graduates  were  from  an
underrepresented  minority  group  and only 21%  of new
UC freshmen  were  from  an  underrepresented  minority
group  (a  difference  of  17.3%).  In  1998  following  the
implementation  of 209,  the  gap  had  widened  to  nearly
24%,  as  underrepresented  groups  constituted  41.6%  of
California  high  school  graduates,  but  only  17.8%  of
new  UC  freshmen  (UCOP  2003).

65

The  University  of  California  and  Governor  Gray
Davis  developed  multiple  strategies  to  address  the
declining  enrollment  of African  American,  Latino  and
Native  American  students  after  the  passage  of
Proposition  209.  The  “Four  Percent  Plan”  (also  called
Eligibility  in  the  Local  Context)  was  one  strategy.
Under  this  plan  high  school  graduates  in  the  top  four
percent  of their  high school  were  guaranteed  admission
to  at  least  one  of  the  University  of  California
campuses.  However,  the  four  percent  plan  has  not
adequately  increased  admission  and  enrollment  of
URM  students  tn the  University  of California.  The  plan
has  been  particularly  limited  in  increasing  URM
admission  and  enrollment  at  the  most  selective  UC
campuses  (Los  Angeles,  Berkeley).  Less  than  three-
fourths  of California  students  in the  top  4 percent  plan
apply  and  are  admitted  to  these  two  most  selective
campuses.  The  first  year  the  4  percent  plan  was  in
effect  (2001),  African  American  freshmen  enrollment  at
Berkeley  fell  to  3.9  percent  (compared  to  7.8  percent  in
1997).  Similarly,  Latino  freshmen  enrollment  at
Berkeley  dropped  to  10.8  percent  (compared  to  14.6
percent  in  1997).

evaluate
at
in  minority
California  public  and  private  institutions  in the  wake
of affirmative  action.  Specifically,  they  investigate  how
minority  enrollments  across  institutions  shifted  as  a
consequence  of Proposition  209.  They also  examine  the
degree  to  which  California’s  community  colleges  and
private
have
compensated  for  the  losses  in  minority  enrollment  at
some  of  the  more  selective  public  institutions.  Their

and

69

elimination  of  affirmative  action  led  to  declines  in
minority  enrollment  at selective  public comprehensive
colleges  in  California.  Yet,  in  addition  to  the  much-
publicized  declines  in  minority  enrollments  at  the
state’s  most  selective  public  institutions,  several  other
considerably  less  selective  public  four-year  institutions
in  Black  and  Latino
also  witnessed  big  losses
enrollments  post-209.

For  African  Americans,  they  find  that  minority
enrollment  in  public  four-year  colleges  declined  by
about  one  percent,  while  African  American  enrollment
at  private  not-for-profit  and  proprietary  institutions
increased  by 1.2%  and  3.5%  respectively.  For  Hispanic
students,  they  find  a  modest  and  non-significant
decline  in  enrollment  at  public  four-year  colleges
accompanied  by a  4.7%  increase  in community  college
matriculation.  The  shares  of  incoming  Hispanic
students  at  private  not-for-profit  and  proprietary
schools  increase  by 2.5%  and  5.9%  respectively.  These
mean  shifts  mask  important  changes  in  minority
enrollment  patterns  across  institutions  of each  type  as
well  as  shifts  in  the  average  quality  of the  institution
under  represented  minority  students  attend.  In  the
absence  of  affirmative  action,  Black  and  Latino
students  clearly  turned  to  the  least  selective  UC
campus—UC  Riverside—and  to some  of the  state's  less
selective  four-year  public  institutions.

Moreover,  the  average  SAT  scores  of  schools
attended  by African  American  and  Hispanic  students
declined  by  over
100  points  (a  half  a  standard
deviation)  as  a  result  of  Proposition  209.  Private
institutions  compensate  for this  decline  in part  in the
case  of African  American  students,  but  not  in the  case

70

of  Hispanic  students.  Overall,  however,  their  results
suggest  that  both  Black  and  Latino  students  increased
their  reliance  on  private  four-vear  and  proprietary  sub-
baccalaureate  institutions  post-209.  In addition,  Latino
students  also  saw  large  increases  in  enrollment  at
community  colleges  post-affirmative  action.

The  recovery  in  minority  representation  at  UT
Austin  was  largely  a  result  of rigorous  race  attentive
recruitment  efforts  (Horn  and  Flores  2003)  and  robust
growth  in  minority  students’  share  of  high  school
graduates  in Texas.  As  Long  documents  (2007,  Figure
2),  the  degree  of  under-representation  of  Blacks,
Hispanics,  and  Native  Americans  among  enrollees
(relative  to  the  minority  share  of  Texas  high  school
graduates)  increased  post-Hopwood  from  20  to  25
percentage  points  at  UT-Austin  and  from  25  to  30
percentage  points  at  Texas  A&M.  The  degree  of
freshman  enrollee  under-representation  of minorities
at UT-Austin  has  remained  virtually  unchanged  since
1997:  24.4%  in  1997  and  23.7%  in  2004.  At  Texas

California  high  schools.  In 2007,  UC  admitted  a  record

total  of  57,318  California  high  school  semors  were

Admissions  offers  to  out-of-state  and  international

2006  (6,143).4  Texas’  flagship  public  university,
University  of  Texas  at  Austin’s  in-state  admits
comprise  about  95%  of  the  freshman  class.  For
example,  in  Fall  2006,  only  5.5  percent  of  entering
freshmen  were  out-of-state  students.®

In  contrast,  the  University  of Michigan  selects  a
considerable  portion  of its  freshman  class  each  year
from  applicants  who  are  not  graduates  of  Michigan
high  schools.  The  following  chart  shows  how  this
practice  affected  the  composition  of  the  African
American  and  Latino  admits  in  2005.  As  the  table
below  shows,  more  than  half of the  African  American
and  more  than  70%  of the  Hispanic  admitted  students
in  2005  (for  whom  the  university  reported  residency
data)  were  not  residents  of the  state.

This  pattern  of  admissions  stems  from  the
university's  explicit  interest  in  enrolling  a  diverse
student  body tnat represents  “Undergraduates  from  all
50  states  and  over  80 foreign  countries;  over  one-third
from  outside  the  state  of Michigan.”6

Michigan's  high  proportion  of  freshman  admits
from  outside  the  state  poses  particular  problems  for
maintaining  or  increasing  diversity  under  Proposal  2's
restrictions.  Moreover,  since  school-based  race-neutral
policies  such  as  Percent  Plans  do not  reach  beyond  the
state  borders,  these  students  cannot  possibly  benefit
from  such  plans.

Additionally,  race-neutral  admissions  policies  are
likely  to  reduce  the  admissions  of underrepresented
minority  out-of-state  applicants,  in  that,  on  average,
admitted  students  from  this  group  admits  score

than  those  admitted  from  non-underrepresented
groups.  The  following  table  shows  that.  although
underrepresented  admits  from  out-of-state  in  2005
scored  somewhat  higher  on  academic  admissions
criteria  than  did  in-state  underrepresented  admits,
they are,  as  a whole,  far less likely to be competitive  for
admission  in a  race-neutral  admissions  environment.
