76

As  a  result,  a  plan  guaranteeing  admission  to
high ranking  students  at each  of the state's  high
schools  would  have  its greatest  effect  on  schools
with  predominantly  white  populations,  yield  a
very  small  number  of URM  students  from  these
schools,  and  would  likely  vield  many  admits
without  sufficient  academic  preparation  to
succeed  at  the  university.

As

noted

above,

Michigan’s

public  _ school
enrollment  is 1.7  million,  with  72%  white  students.  An
additional  20%  of students  are  black,  4%  Latino,  2%
Asian,  and  1%  Native  American.  (2005-2006  data).
From  1990-1991  to  2005-2006,  Michigan  public  school
enrollment  grew  more  than  13%.’  Like  the  rest  of the
country.  Michigan's  school  enrollment  has  also  grown
more  diverse  during  this  time  period  although
Michigan’s  public  school  enrollment  is not  as  racially
diverse  as  the  national  enrollment.§  In  1990,  78%  of
students  were  white  while  only  72%  were  in 2005.  The
percentage  of  both  Latino  and  Asian  students  has
doubled,  and  the  percentage  of black  students  has  also
shghtly  increased.

* The  primary  source  of data  is the  National  Center  for  Education
Statistics  Common  Core  of Data  (CCD).  The  CCD’s  Public  School
Universe  has  collected  data  from  virtually  every  public  school
district  in the nation  since  1987,  using student  enrollment  counts.
to  analyze  student
These  data  provide  the  opportunity
enrollments,  by  race/ethnicity  and  by  poverty  status,  across
districts  and  vears.  The  analyses  here  use  data  from  1990-91
through  2005-06,  the  latest  year  of  CCD  data  that  has  been
released.

®  The  racial  composition  of Michigan’s  public  school  enrollment
approximates  that  of  the  Midwest  as  a  region.  In  2005-06,  in
Midwestern  states,  73%  of students  were  white,  15%  black,  8%
Latino,  3% Asian.  and  1% Native  American  (Orfield  & Lee,  2007).

77

Black  students  remain,  by  a  large  margin,
Michigan's  largest  minority  group  among  public  school
students.  The  Detroit  City  Schools  are  the  largest
school  district  in  Michigan,  with  more  than  133,000
students  in 2005-06.  Slightly  more  than  one-third  of all
black  students  in Michigan  and  one-tenth  of all Latino
students  attended  Detroit  City  Schools.  Only  0.3%  of
all  white  students  in  Michigan  attend  Detroit  City
Schools.

The  racial  concentration  of Michigan  students  is
revealed  by  examining  schools  of  three  different
categories:  predominately  minority  schools  (50-100%
nonwhite  students),  intensely  segregated  minority
schools  (90-100%  nonwhite  students),  and  intensely
segregated  white  schools  (90-100%  white  students).
Conceptually,  90-100%  is  used  to  signify  “one-race”
schools  because  schools  that  are  90%  or  greater
minority  students  have  been  shown  overwhelmingly  to
be  schools  with  high  concentrations  of poor  students,
and  linked  to  important  educational  disadvantages.
(Frankenberg,  Lee,  &  Orfield,  2003;  Orfield  &  Lee,
2006).  Likewise,  both  90-100%  minority  schools  and  90-
100%  white  schools  indicate  only  a  token  presence  of
whites  or  non-whites,  respectively,  in such  schools  (see
Ma  & Kurlaender,  2005).

High  percentages  of black  students  attend  schools
with  large  shares  of students  of color.  In fact,  in 2005,
a  vast
a  majority  of  black  students  attended
predominantly  black  and intensely  segregated  minority
schools.  77.5%  of all black  students  attended  50-100%
minority  schools  in 2005-06,  and 58%  of black  students
attended  intensely  segregated  minority  schools  (90-
100%  minority  students).  To  contextualize  these

of  the  percentage  of  black  students

percentages,  it is important  to  note  that  Michigan  has
consistently  ranked  near  the  top  of all  U.S.  states  in
terms
in
predominantly  and  intensely  segregated  minority
schools;  in 2005-06,  Michigan  was  ranked  as  the  sixth
and third-highest  state,  respectively,  in the percentage
of black  students  in such  schools  (Orfield  & Lee,  2007).
In  2005-06,  approximately  40%  of  black  students
across  the  country  were  in  90-100%  minority  schools.
Yet,  this  national  percentage  is  still  almost  20
percentage  points  lower  than  the  share  of Michigan
black  students  in these  intensely  segregated  minority
schools.

Notably,  among  states  that have  prohibited  the use
of  race  in  admissions,  Michigan  has  the  highest
percentage—by  far---of  black  students  in  racially
isolated  minority  schools  (where  90%  or  more  of the
enrollment  are  students  of color).  This  is particularly
significant  given  the fact,  as  seen  above,  that Michigan
has  the  fewest  students  of color  and  thus  it would  be
expected  that  fewer  students  would  be  likely  to  be  in
schools  with  such  high  concentrations  of students  of
color.  Washington,  whose  demographics  are  the closest
to  Michigan,  has  the  least  black  segregation  on  any  of
the  three  measures.

This  pattern  of  racial  isolation  means  that  the
majority  of  Black  students  in  Michigan  and  a
significant  proportion  of Hispanic  students  are  found  in
a  very  small  number  of  the  state's  high  schools.  In
2005-2006,  only  113 of the state’s  831high  schools  were
comprised  of 90-100%  non-white  students.  In contrast,
60 percent  of Michigan’s  white  students  attend  schools
where  white  students  comprise  90-100%  of the student
body.  Of the  state’s  831  high schools,  nearly  half (406)

80

are  90-100%  white.  An  additional  230  high  schools  are
50-90%  white.

A  Percent  Plan  applied  to  Michigan's  pattern  of
racially  isolated  high  schools  that  would  guarantee
admission  to top-ranked  students  at  each  of the  state's
high  schools  would  disproportionately  favor  white
students  who  are  much  more  broadly  distributed
across  a  large  number  of the  state’s  schools.  This  is
particularly  the  case  because  Black  and  Hispanic
students  are  less  likely  than  whites  to  fall  in  the  top
ranks  of their  class  in  racially  diverse  schools,  even
when  they  are  in  the  majority,  a  Percent  Plan  would
yield  a  small  number  of Black  and  Hispanic  students.
Moreover,  such  a plan would  not  capture  the  Black  and
Latino  students  who  attend  racially  diverse  schools
and  might  not  be  ranked  at  the  top  of their  class  but
are  .onetheless  high-achieving  and  prepared  to
succeed  at  the  University  of Michigan.

the

with

B.3.  The  small  number  of  Michigan  high
predominantly  URM
schools
populations  produce  relatively  few  graduates
with
academic  records  that  would  make
them  competitive  for  admission  at _ the
University  of  Michigan.  This,  in  part,  results
from  the  high  concentration  of poverty  at  racially
isolated  minority  schools.  These  lower  levels  of
academic  performance  result,  in  part,  from  the
differential  educational  opportunity  in Michigan—
specifically  the  ability  to  meet  state  standards  on
assessment  tests,  qualifying  for  a  state  merit  aid
for  college—that  are  related  to  the  racial  and/or
poverty  composition  of  a  student’s  high  school.
Consequently,  race-neutral  “Percent  Plans”  would

51

likelv  result  in the  admission  of underrepresented
minority  students  with  academic  preparedness  far
below  that  required  to  succeed  in  an  “elite”
university.

One  indicator  of  the  lower  levels  of  college
readiness  provided  by schools  attended  by most  of the
state’s  Black  students  is the rates  at  which  students  in
different  schools  earn  Michigan  Merit  awards.’  The
Michigan  Merit  Awards  are  granted  based  on  student
performance  on  the Michigan  Educational  Assessment
Program  (MEAP).  The  MEAP  consists  of a  series  of
questions,  the  purpose  of  which  was  to  evaluate  the
teaching  of  Michigan  students  with  respect
to
curriculum  standards  established  by the State  Board  of
Education  in five content  areas:  mathematics,  reading,
science,  social  studies,  and  writing.

to

earn

Merit

Michigan

Fewer  students  in intensively  segregated  minority
schools  have  opportunities  to learn  sufficient  to enable
them
Scholarships.
Specifically,  in the  high  school  class  of 2006,  less  than
10%  of  students  in  segregated  minority  schools,  on
average,  received  the  Michigan  Merit  Award.  By
contrast,  the  average  percentage  of  students  who
received  the  merit  award  in  majority  white  and
segregated  white  schools  was  more  than  four  times,
39.6%  and  42.2%,  respectively,  the  average  share  of

%  Data  on  students’  performance  on  the  MEAP,  Michigan's
standardized  test,  is  taken  from  the  Michigan  Department  of
Education's  website.  The  dataset  of  Michigan  Merit  Award
Recipients  is  available  from  the  Michigan  Department  of  the
Treasury,  which  administers  the  award.  These  datasets  were
merged  with  the  respective  vear  CEPI  datasets  to  calculate  the
racial  and  poverty  composition  of each  school.

82

Merit  Awardees  in schools  with  0-10%  white  students.
More  than  30%  of  segregated  white  schools  had  a
majority  of students  receiving  the Merit  Award.  At the
same  time,  none  of the  10-50%  white  schools  and  only
one  0-10%  white  school  had  more  than  half  of their
twelfth  graders  receiving  the  Merit  Award.

{United  States  District  Court  for  the  Eastern
District  of Michigan  Docket  No.  2:06-cv-15024}

THE  UNIVERSITY  OF  MICHIGAN
ANN  ARBOR

OFFICE  OF  THE  PRESIDENT

March  5, 1970

To  the  Members  of the  Black  Action  Movement:

The  statement  submitted  by  the  Black  Action
Movement  has  been  reviewed  by  the  Executive
Officers,  and the attached  proposal  will be presented  to
the Regents  for their consideration  during the course  of
their  meetings  on  March  18 and  19.  I should  like to add
some  comments  about  the  proposal.

Black  students  have  placed  special  emphasis  on
the point that  there  should  be sufficient  black  students
enrolled  by  the  academic  vear  1973-74  to  equal  ten
percent  of the  student  population  in Ann  Arbor.  In the
preliminary  conversations  which  have  been  held
between  administrators  and  black  students,  the
administrators  have  suggested  two  reservations  about
the  ten  percent  enrollment  point.  One  has  to  do  with
obtaining  the  financial  resources  to  support  it.  The
other  raises  the  question  of whether  a  viable  modified
admission  standard  can  be devised  which  will  produce
the  required  number  of students  by 1973-74.

Unfortunately,  both  the  black  students  and  the
difficulty  § in
to
administrators
understanding  one  another  on  the  ten  percent  issue.
When  the  administrators  indicate  reservations,  the

seem

have

89

black  students  interpret  this  as  a foot-dragging  or  lack
of commitment.  When  the  black  students  insist  on  an
unequivocal
the
percent
administrators  do  not  reject  the  figure  as  either
undesirable  or  unattainable.  On  the  contrary,  they
simply  do not wish to promise  something  which  present
information  would  indicate  may  not  be possible.

commitment,

ten

For  all  practical  purposes,  funds  to  be  used  in
support  of  additional  black  students  can  be  derived
from  general  funds  appropriated  to  the  University  by
the State  Legislature,  tuition  paid by students,  income
on  investments,  gifts  (individual,  corporate  and
foundation),  and  federal  funds.  If there  is any  single
fact  about  universities  today  which  is  the  subject  of
agreement,  it  is  that  all  of  them  are  very  short  of
funds.  Therefore,  the  only  way  in which  funds  can  be
put into  the  support  of additional  black  students  is to
divert  such  funds  from  other  purposes  and  give  a  very
high  priority  to  the  recruitment  and  support  of black
students.  Such  flexible  funds  as  there  are,  are  now
heavily  committed  to  such  things  as  laboratory
renovations,  land  purchases,  badly  needed  equipment,
library  books,  and  capital  improvement  projects.

Aside  from  the  financial  problem,  there  is  a
problem  of  admissions  criteria.  These  criteria  are
administered  more  flexibly  in the  case  of Opportunity
Award  students  than  for  others,  with  less  reliance  on
academic  averages  or  test  scores.  We  know  from  our
on
present
Opportunity  Award  students  runs  somewhat  more
than  twice  the  average  of all  students,  but  it is  still
tolerable  in terms  of what  is being accomplished.  We do
not  know  how  much  further  deviation  we  can  make  in

experience

attrition

that

rate

the

90

the  criteria  and  still  accomplish  anything.  At  some
point  it  is  clear  that  the  student  is  far  better  off  to
enter  a college  where  the competition  is less severe  and
where  the  course  options  are
less  academically
oriented.

Our  tradition,  if one  can  speak  of traditions  in so
short  a  period,  is one  of working  together  toward  the
achievement  of mutually  desired  ends.  There  are  on
our  staff  a  number  of individuals  on  whom  we  all  rely
for  advice  in  these  matters.  Men  like  John  Chavis,
George  Goodman,  Richard  Huston,  Will  Smith  and
others  have  now  spent  some  time  on  the  problems  of
black  students,  particularly  as  they relate  to admission
and  financial  aid.  With  their  help,  and  with  our  joint
efforts  to  advance,  we  can  agree  on  how  best  to
maximize  our  interests.  Given  the  willingness  of the
University  to  give  a  very  high  priority  to  these
problems,  and  the  dedication  of the  black  students  to
increased  educational  opportunities  for  blacks,  it will
be  tragic  if we  expend  our  energies  in disagreement.

I  shall  be  glad  to  involve  the  above  named
individuals  and  others  in conversations  as  to  how  we
can  best  move  forward.  I have,  in  fact,  asked  Will
Smith  to  deliver  this  statement  to  you  and  discuss  it
with  you.

Sincerely,
R.  W.  Fleming
R.  W.  Fleming

President  Fleming  said  that  the  issue  of the  black
student  demands  had  been  discussed  at  two  previous
meetings.  Subsequent  to  these  meetings,  there  had
been  developed  a document  that  would  be presented  to
the  Regents  by the  executive  officers,  indicating  the
kind  of proposal  the  administration  was  prepared  to
make.  The  Regents  furthermore  had copies of the black
student  document,  as  well  as  the  administration
proposal.  In  addition  to  those  documents,  Vice-
Presidents  Smith  and  Spurr  spoke  to  the  other  issues
in  the  black  student  document  which  had  not  been
covered  in the  original  proposal.

The  problem,  Mr.  Fleming  said,  was  on  the  one
hand  to  make  the  maximum  commitment,  and  on  the
other,  to  do  so  responsibly.  We  were  in  a  period  when
anyone  concerned  with  university  financing  must  know
that  the  single  most  difficult  problem  was  simply  that
of money.  This  was  true  of all  academic  institutions.
Unfortunately,  this  problem  came  at  a  time  when  on

both  a  state  and  national  level  education  was  enjoying
its  least  popularity  in perhaps  many a year.

Regent  Huebner  then  read  the following  statement,

which  was  moved  and  supported:

1. The  Board  of Regents  concurs  in the  suggestion
that  questions  of  admission  and  financial  aids  be
separated  for  program  analysis,  in all  the  schools  and
colleges.

2.

That  the  Board  of Resents  then  concurs  in  the
establishment  of an  admissions  goal which  is designed
to produce  by 1973-74  admissions  aimed  at  10 per cent
enrollment  of  black  students  and  substantially
increased
minority  § and
of
members
disadvantaged  groups.

other

3.  In  reaffirming  the  conviction  of the  importance
of  recruiting  added  numbers,  the  Board  of  Regents
recognizes  that  increased  funds  for  staff  (both
recruiting  and  counseling)  and  for financial  aid  will  be
required.  It therefore  commits  for  1970-71,  a minimum
of  $100,000  for  employment  of  staff,  strategically
placed  to  assure  the  broadest  intensification  of  the
effort.  Moreover,  the  financial  commitment  to  the
program  (for both  staff and  financial  aid  will  be raised
from  its  present  level  of approximately  $1,000,000  to
$3,000,000  by  1973-74.  The  allocation  of the  annual
increments  between  staff  and  financial  aids  can  be
determined  best  on  an  annual  basis.  It may  be  noted
that  $3,000,000  represents  an  amount  greater  than  the
total  present  General  Fund  Budget  for financial  aid.  It
must  also  be  noted  that  in  providing  this  level  of
guaranteed
the  commitment
provides  a  top  priority  of  University  funds,  and  the

financial

support,

93

achievement  will  require  active  support  from  all
operating  units  of the  University.

It is recognized  also that the financial  support  thus
guaranteed  will  require  supplementation,  and  the
recruitment  program  should  include  efforts  to seek out
students  with  veterans’  educational  benefits  so  that
numbers  may  be  increased  while  alleviating  the
financial  drain.  In  addition,  intensified  efforts  will  be
undertaken  to  raise  additional  funds  from  state,
federal  and  gift  sources,  including  the  many  local
foundations  now  being  contacted.  We  invite  the
assistance  and  suggestions  from  the  many  groups
interested  and  benefitted  by the  program.

experimented  with  in such  a  way  that  the  objective  of

studied

be

5. The  total  Opportunity  Award  Program,  including
recruitment,  admissions,  and  financial  aids  will  be
centrally  coordinated  in  the  Office  of Vice  President
Spurr,  working  with  a  committee  consisting  of
students,  faculty,  and  administrators.

6. By separating  admissions  from  financial  aids.  it
is clear  that  admission  of self-supporting  students,  and
the  strategic  allocation  of resources  in individual  cases
based  on  need,  will  advance  the  achievement  of  the
admissions  goal.

94

7.  In  addition  to  the  assertion  of  a  goal  of
admissions,  and  the  commitment  to  increase  total
dollar  support  of  that  program,  the  University  will
provide  General  Fund  support,  in  the  College  of
Literature,  Science,  and  the  Arts  for  1970-71,  in  the
amount  of $170,000,  to  provide  support  for  continued
development  of the  Afro-American  Studies  Program,
the  initial  development  of the  Black  Students  Center,
and  the  funding  of  special  seminar  support.  The
allocation  among  the  three  purposes  will  be  handled
within  the  College  to  assure  the  best  use  of the  funds
for  total  development.  Program  Centers  for  other
groups  may  in  the  future  be  appropriate  in  the
University.

Regent  Cudlip  said  that  he  had  not  spoken  at  the
previous  hearings  because  he wished  to hear  all of the
facts  from  all of the  sources.  He  said  that  he learned  a
great  deal  at  the  hearing  of  yesterday,  and  he  had
continued  to  learn  as  the  discussions  proceeded.  He
said that  the  proposal  presented  by Regent  Huebner  in
his estimation  was  a sound  plan which  did  indeed  give
attention  to  the  seriousness  of the  problem.

Our  country,  he  said,  was  experiencing  severe
strains  because  of problems  that  had  gone  unattended
for  many  years.  He  felt  that  every  segment  of society
must  assume  some  responsibility  to  help  those  who
had  not  had  their  fair  share.  These  objectives  he  felt
were  necessary  to  achieve  harmony  and  unity  in  the
country.  He  said  that  it  was  pointed  out  before  that
there  were  limitations,  because  money  which  was
representative  of resources  was  not  readily  available.
Education,  he  felt,  was  a  vital  part  of everyone’s  life,
and  the  more  educated  people  of all kinds  who  had  the

9d

will  to come  and  conclude  their  education  at  a  place  of
higher  learning  would  indeed  make a contribution  to
society.  Everyone,  he  felt  should  be  brought  into  the
stream  of life.

Regent  Dunn  commented  that  he  wished  to
recognize  the  constructive  comments  brought  by the
Black  Action  Movement  students  to  help  broaden
educational  opportunities  for black  students  and for all
disadvantaged  students.  He  hoped  that  in  the  future,
students  as  well  as  faculty  would  constructively  help
the  University  achieve  its  goals.  He  thought  that  the
movement  assisted  the  Regents  to review  priorities:  he
knew  that the force  of the  movement  would  continue  to
have  the  Regents  do so.

Regent  Dunn  also  stated  that  he felt that  students
and  faculty  could  not  only  help  in  setting  financial
priorities,  but  through  analysis  assist  in  curriculum
and  course  content  development.  In  sum,  he  said,  he
hoped  that  the  other  schools  in Michigan  would  follow
the lead  which  The  University  of Michigan  at this  point
was  prepared  to  take.

Regent  Nederlander  was  particularly  impressed  by
the  presentations  made  over  the  last  two  months  by
members  of  the  Black  Action  Movement  and  black
faculty,  and  the  manner  in  which  the  entire  process
had  been  handled.  He felt  that  it was  the  obligation  of
the  University  to  see  that  blacks  continued  to  remain
and  more  definitely  be brought  into  the  mainstream  of
life.  If that  meant  the  committing  of funds,  then  the
University  must  do so.

He  also  said  that  by approving  the  proposals  read
by  Regent  Huebner  this  would  be  interpreted  as

96

capitulating  to student  demands,  particularly  by some
of the  legislators.  He  felt,  however,  that  the  issue  was
of such  priority  that  the  University  and  others  should
work  on  the  state  level  to  try  to  get  funding  for  these
critical  programs.  He  concluded  by  saying  that  he
would  support  an  admissions  goal  for  1973-74  which
aimed  at  a  ten  per  cent  enrollment  of black  students
and  increasing  the  number  of  other  minority  and
disadvantaged  groups.

Regent  Brown  said  that  the  University  had  been
involved  for  the  last  five  or  six  years  in  a  very
constructive  approach  which  was  identified  as  the
Opportunity  Awards  Program.  The  goals  of  that
program,  he  said,  were  still  valid  and  continued  to
receive  wholehearted  encouragement  from  the Regents.
An earlier  document  prepared  by George  Goodman,  he
went  on,  carried  out  in  some  detail  the  concepts  and
ideas  supported  by that  program.

Regent  Brown  went  on  to say  that  the financial  aid
program  had  increased  from  $66,000  in  1964-653  to
$980,000  for the current  year.  He continued  to  support
the  program,  and  hoped  that  over  the  next  four  or  five
years  significant  progress  could  be made.  Therefore.  he
too  felt that  the  proposal  read  by Regent  Huebner  was
one  which  he  could  accept  and  would  indicate  the
Regents’  dedication  to  improving  the  position  of  the
black  students  as  well  as  that  of other  minority  and
disadvantaged  groups.

Regent  Lindemer  commented  that  the  responsible
and  constructive  approach  taken  by many  members  of
the  student  body,  members  of  the  Black  Action
Movement,  and  black  faculty  was,  in  his  estimation,
good.  He thought  he reflected  the  reaction  of the  other

97

Regents  to  the  favorable  presentation  made  yesterday
by Professor  Marshall  and  in the  manner  in  which  it
was  done.  He hoped  that  in the future  responsible  and
mature  approaches  could  be  taken  to  this  problem,
separating  the true  issues  from  extreme  emotionalism,
and  in  some  instances,  extreme  and  irresponsible
actions.

One  of the problems  which  the  University  had was
the problem  of disassociating  the  merit  in matters  such
as  the presentation  of the Black  Action  Movement  from
the  uses  that  others  sought  to  make  of that  merit  for
their  own  ends.

He  wanted  it clearly  understood,  in order  to  avoid
any  possibility  of  deception.  that  except  where  the
Regents  have  said  “commit,”  they  were  referring  to
goals.  He  said  these  were  good  faith  goals  toward
which  everything  would  be  devoted.

Regent  Dunn  asked  whether  the  goals  that  were

set  were  for the  total  University  community.

Vice-President  Smith  responded  that  indeed  the
intent  was  to  make  the  goals  University-wide,  clearly
including  Flint  and  Dearborn.

Regent  Goebel  said  that  perhaps  many  of  the
comments  he  had  intended  to  make  were  already
made,  but he wanted  it made  clear  that  the  actions  the
Regents  would  take  were  not being taken  as  a result  of
had  a
pressure.
responsibility  to  do  everything  humanly  possible  to
assist  in correcting  social  wrongs,  as  was  also  true  of
the state  and federal  government.  When  the  University
initiated,  several  years  ago,  the  Opportunity  Awards

the  University

He  said

that

98

Program,  this  was  a  clear  indication  that  the
University  was  concerned  about  solving the problem:  it
had  a  distinct  obligation.  This  program  at  the
University  was  one  of  the  first  in  the  country  of  a
rather  large  magnitude.

He  went  on  to  say  that  he  felt  the  University  had
been  moving  in  the  direction  suggested  by the  Black
Action  Movement.  He said that  he was  wholeheartedly
in  favor  of the  University’s  program  of expanding  and
encouraging  the  admission  of  more  minority  and
disadvantaged  groups  to  the  University  community.

President  Fleming  then  made  some  concluding
remarks  wherein  he said that if the goal of ten  per cent
enrollment  of  black  students  was  to  be  achieved,  it
would  require  the  wholehearted  cooperation  of  the
various  schools  and  colleges:  it  was  here  that  the
resources  lay, and thus  the commitment  by the schools
and  colleges  would  be an  essential  factor.

to  that,

In  addition

the  proposal  certainly
contemplated  that  the  University  community  as  a
whole  would  work  with  the  state  legislature  and
federal  sources  to  increase  dojlars  that  could  be
devoted  to  the  program.  One  should  not,  however,
underestimate  the  difficulties  that  would  be  faced  to
bring  this  about.  There  was  no  question,  he  said,  that
the  entire  program  would  necessitate  a  reallocation  of
internal  resources,  and  that  could  only be done  by the
complete  cooperation  of  all  those  involved  in  the
University  community.

Therefore,  the  significant  difference  between  the
proposal  read  by  Regent  Huebner  and  the  previous
statement  was  that  the  ten  per  cent  admissions  goal

99

was  concurred  in for  1973-74,  a commitment  that  was
taken  seriously  and  one  which  the  University  would
seek  to  achieve.

Mr.  Ron  Harris  then  addressed  the  Regents  and
indicated  that  no  clear  statement  was  directed  to  the
Black  Action  Movement’s  request  for  additional
recruiters  for  Chicanos;  to  the  specific  number  of
additional  recruiters  to be hired;  or  to the admission  of
at  least  900  black  students  in  the  school  year  of 1970-
71.

the

that

Vice-President

dollars
Smith  said
allocated  and  committed  which  call  for  a  level  rate  of
$500,000  a  year,  would  be  available  for  both  staff
acquisitions  and  student  aid,  and  the  allocations  would
be  made  through  Vice-President  Spurr’s  coordination
with  the  committee  that  was  proposed.  We  do  not
know,  he  said,  whether  a  sizable  proportion  of  the
initial  allocation  should  go  to  recruiting  staff.  If that
committee  determines  that  there  should  be  in effect  a
front  loading on  staff acquisitions  in order  to give mote
rapid impetus  to the recruiting  efforts  at both  graduate
and  undergraduate  levels,  then  that  decision  can  be
made  in  the  allocation  of the  funds  available  in  next
year’s  budget.

Mr.  Harris  then  asked  about  the  admission  of 900

black  students  for  the  1970-71  period.

Vice-President  Smith  responded  that  the  question
of 900  black  students  for  that  period  would  again  be  a
question  for  the  committee  to consider,  and  to  look  for
the  areas  in  which  such  a  number  could  possibly  be
achieved.

100

Professor  Gloria  Marshall  then  addressed  the
Regents  and  said  that  she wished  it to be clear  that  the
black  faculty  and  Black Action  Movement  persons  were
in  support  of  commitment  rather  than  the  simple
setting  of a goal of ten  per cent  admissions.  She hoped,
therefore,  that  statement  number  2 would  be rewritten
in  order  that  commitment  would  be  the  dominant
thrust  therein.

President  Fleming  observed  that  this  was  the
intention  of Paragraph  2, that  the  University  would
make  every  effort  to  achieve  the  goal of a  ten  per  cent
enrollment.  “We  do not  lightly  undertake  obligations,”
he said,  “and  when  the University  establishes  that as  a
goal  and  says  it  will  try  to  achieve  that  goal  it  will
make  every  effort  to  do  so.”

Mr.  Ron  Harris  suggested  that  a  manner  in which
the  Regents  could  proceed  would  be to  look  at  each  of
the  ten  black  student  demands  and  votre
om  them
individually.

Regent  Lindemer  said  that  the  resolution  which
Regent  Huebner  presented  reflected  the  input  and
thinking  of all of the Regents,  as  well  as  the thinking  of
the  black  students  themselves.  And  in  the  process  of
working  out  a  solution,  nobody  got everything  that  he
or  she  wanted,  including  Regents  or  other  members  of
the  University  community,  or  including  those  who  had
written  in direct  opposition  to the  demanas  laid  before
the  Regents.

With  respect  to  the  tuition  waiver,  Regent
Lindemer  said  that  in the cold  light of reason  it was  an
unworkable  concept.

101

Vice-President  Smith  said  he  would  like  to  make
another  comment,  and  that  was  that  the  total  dollar
figures  expressed  in  the  resolution  were  by no  means
the only monies  that  would  be devoted  to the program.

An  unidentified

speaker  from  the  audience
suggested  at  that  point  that  the  Regents  should  vote
the  motion  down  because  there  really  were  slight
changes  in this  proposal  from  that  of the previous  one.

On  vote  of the  resolution  stated  above  by Regent

Huebner,  the  motion  passed  unanimously.

All  of the  above  actions  were  by unanimous  vote

except  where  indicated.

Expenses  of the  Regents  of the  University  reported

during  the  month  of February  1970  were  $771.70.

The  Regents  adjourned  to  meet  on  April  16 and  17.

HERBERT  W.  HILDEBRANDT,  Secretary

having been  called  as  a witness  herein,  and after

Q Professor  Orfield,  can  you  spell  your  name  for  the

A Gary  Orfield,  O-R-F-1-E-L-D.

Q Give  us  your  basic  geographical  data,  where  and
when  you  were  born?

A I was  born  in  1941,  in Minneapolis.

Q Did  you  grow  up  there?

AI  did.

Q Tell  us  your  educational  history  if you  would?

Alm  a  graduate  of public  schools  in Minneapolis,  and
of the  University  of Minnesota  where  I| graduated  in
political  science.  I went  to  the  University  of Chicago
and  received  my  masters,  Ph.  D.  in  political  science,
and  then  began  teaching  and  a  research  career.

teaching

and  research  career,  it  would  be  very  irritating  and

initially.

University

of  Virginia

A Well,  I basically  taught  at five  research  universities,
Princeton
the
University,  University  of  Illinois,  the  University  of
Chicago,  and  Harvard  University.  I’ve  also  worked  at
research  centers  including  Brookings  Institution.  [ve
done  brief  stints  with  the  government,  the  U.S.  Civil

Q Can  you  tell  us  vour  main  current  areas  of research?

A  I  research  particularly  on  issues  of  educational
equality  at this  time.  During  this year,  we  will  publish
three  edited  volumes  on  educational  and  equality
issues.  One  will  be  on  an  issue  on  school  reform.  It’s
called  “Hard  Work  for Good  Schools.”  It’s  about  Title  I
programs  in the federal  government.  The  second  one  is
on  testing.  It’s  called,  “Raising  Standards  or  Raising
Barriers.”
of  testing  and
accomplishments  of testing  on  active  --  completion  of

Its  about  the  effect

And  we  are  publishing  a  book  called,  “Our

Diversity

[Page  83]
Challenge,”  which  is  abou.  --  research  on  impacts  of

Within  the  last  couple  of weeks,  our  project  ran  a

States.  This  Friday,  we’re  running  a conference  at the
University  of  Texas  with  research  centers  on  the
impact  of  the  changes  in  testing  and  in  college
admissions  tests  at  the  Texas  Institution.

We  are  doing  a  study  now  about  the  Florida  Plan.
We've  been  doing  interviews  by  phone,  and  we'll  be
doing  interviews  in the  field  on  that.

Q What  you  men  by the  “Flor  Plan”  if you  would?

admit  the wop  twenty  percent  of the graduating  class  of
each  high  school  in Florida.

Q@  And  that  was  instituted  as  a  replacement  for

A By Governor  Bush,  yes,  sir.

Q Have  you  testified  as  an  expert  witness  before?

A Yes.

Q In  what  kind  of cases?

education.  Those

cases.

are  the  major  areas  in which  I've  testified.  And  | think

think  those  are  some  of our  major  funders.

THE  COURT:  I have  read  it and  IJ don’t  believe
the plaintiff has any  objection,  nor  the defense  to allow
him  to  testify  as  an  expert.

We  didn’t  get  really  good  national  data  on

Rights  Act,  and  a  collection  of data  that  followed  that
Act.  From  the period  when  we  begin to closely,  we  went
from  almost  complete  apartheid  in the  South  in  1954,
almost  one  hundred  percent  of  black  students  and
teachers  were  in completely

segregated  institutions,  to  a  situation  whereby  1970,

South  became  the  most  integrated  part  of the  United
States.

Throughout  all  of the  data  that  we  have  the  most
intense  segregation  that  existed  in  the  country  since
1970,  has  been  in  the  major  industrial  states  of the

A  Yes.  Well  this  is  a  chart  that  shows  the  most
segregated  states  in  the  United  States  for  black
students  in  1998-99,  which  is  the  most  recent  data
that’s  available  from  the federal  government  and  what
will  be  in  our  forthcoming  report  measured  three
different  ways.  And  it basically  here  shows  that  in the
state  of  Michigan  only  seventeen  percent  to  the
students  in the  state  or  black  students  are  in majority
white  schools.  In  other  words,  eighty-three  percent  of
the  black  students  in  the  state  of  Michigan  are  in
predominantly  minority  schools,  in  schools  that  we
would  call  segregated  schools.

On  the  measure  of  what  we  call  extreme
segregation  which  is  ninety  to  one  hundred  percent
minority  schools,  Michigan  ranks  number  one.  Sixty-
four  percent  of all the black  students  in the state  are  in
extremely  segregated  schools.  These  are  the  ones  that
vou  might  call  hyper-segregated  schools  where  there  is
little  or  no  contact  with  students  of other  racial  groups.

In terms  of the  third  measure  that  we  use  which  is
call  the  Exposure  Index  which  shows  that  typical

composition  of  a  class  --  of  a  schoo]  attended  by
students  in the states  which  involves  computing  every

forth,  it indicates  the typical  black  student  in Michigan
is in  a  school  that  is about  eighty  percent  non-white.
And  Michigan  ranks  second  in  the  country  on  this
measure.  So  Michigan  ranks  between  first  and  third

A  Well,  for  me  basically  if  you  were  to  compare

Basically  there’s  no  place  in  the  South  that  is
anywhere  close  to  the  segregation  level  of Michigan.
There's  no  place  where  there’s  less  contact  between
blacks  and  whites  in  any  of the  areas  where  there  is
apartheid  in the country  until  a generation  ago. A little
girl was  never  led  into  a

[Page  91]
school  in the  Michigan  area.

I just  read  a  report  from  the  National  Bureau  of
Economic  Research  yesterday  that  showed  that  in
metropolitan  Detroit  segregation  is the  highest  of any
metropolitan  area  in the  country  for  schools.  And  the
typical  black  student  in  metropolitan  Detroit  is  in  a
ninety-three  percent  minority  school.  It’s  the  most
segregated  urban  community  we  have  in  educational
terms  in the  country.

The  intense  segregation  is  kind  of  a  measure  of
really almost  complete  isolation  because  many  of those
schools  who  are  nearly  almost  one  hundred  percent
minority  is  almost  like  the  school  that  Erika  talked
about  where  there  are  no  students  of other  races,  and
there’s  no  way  for  students  to  understand  what
students  of other  races  experience  until  they  take  a
field  trip or  something  like  that  as  she  described  in her
concert  competition.

a  city in every  dimension  of educational  opportunity.

Q What  is the educational  consequences  of that  kind  of
segregation?

A  Well.  there  are  many  educational  consequences
because  basically  the  schools  that  say  separate  but
equal  is  the  most  extensive  social  experiment  in  the
United  States  history.  We've  tried  it in  thousands  of
places for many  generations.  It never  worked  anywhere
as  far  as  |  can  tell.  Nobody's  been  able  to  me  a
comparable  example.  There  never  was  a  separate  but
equal  school  system.  That's  because  of  many  things.
It’s because  the poverty  levels  in segregated  schools  are
much  higher.  Almost  the only intensely  improvishered
schools  that  we  have  in the  metropolitan  areas  are  for
black  and  Latino  children.  They  are  also because  there
are  many  fewer  minorities  in  teacher  training.  There
are  many  fewer  teachers  who  choose  to  go  work  in
schools  of this  sort.  Most  teachers  who  start  in schools
that  are  segregated  leave  faster.  The curriculum  that  is
offered  is  more  limited.  The  probability  that  the
teacher  will  be  trained  in  their  field  is  much  more

117

limited.  The  level  of competition  is less.  The  respect  for
the  institution  on  the  outside  world  is  less.  The
connections  to  colleges  are  less.  There  are  more
children  with  health  problems  because  minority
children  are  much  more  likely  to  live  in  rental]  than
ownership  housing.  The  population  is  much  more
unstable.  Many  segregated  schools  have  a  vast
turnover  of students  every  year

[Page  93}
and  there's  tremendous  educational  instability  as  far
as  students  go  and  faculty  go.  It’s  a  different  world  in
every  respect.  You  heard  some  illustrations  of that.  But
basically  we  are  not  a society  that’s  learned  how  to run
separate  but  equal  schools.  We  run  separate  schools
that  are  always  systematically  unequal.  And  basically
if you  --  sometimes  when  I was  doing  research  on  this
issue  in Chicago  a  suburban  teacher  would  call  me  and
say,  you  know,  we've  read  your  study,  we  decided  to
actually  go in and  see  what  the  same  exact  class  looked
in  a  counter-part  school  in  Chicago.  And  they  would
come  back  and  everyone  would  just  be  stunned.  The
kids  didn't  have  books.  They  were  only  half  way
through  the  subject.  The  teacher  didn’t  know  the
subject.  There  were  no  facilities.  And  the  whole  level  of
competition,  expectation  was  so  different  that  it was
like  a  different  planet,  a  different  society.

Q How  does  educational  segregation  relate  to  housing
segregation?

A  It’s  directly  related  to  housing  segregation  unless
there  is  some  kind  of  de-segregation  plan  that
overcomes  the  housing  segregation.  And  Detroit  has  --
is  one  of  the  most  intensely  segregated  housing
markets  in  the  United  States.  It’s  one  of those  that
have  been  designated  as  hyper-segregation  in  our
research.  Doug  Massey  and  Nancy  Denton  and  other
scholars  who have  looked  at housing  segregation  across
the  country.  So  there’s  intense  housing  segregation,
and  very  strong  boundaries  that  separate  schools  from

122 ce

different  racial  groups  because  even  the  working  class
suburbs  of Detroit  have  had  tremendous  residential
segregation.

A Yes.

Q If you  could  tell  us  a  little  bit  about  this  chart,  and
what  we  should  draw  from  it.  Well,  we  looked  at  all  of
the  schools  in  the  United  States  in  which  data  was
reported  to  the  federal  government,  many  tens  of

123

thousands  of  schools.  And  we  looked  at  schools  that
had  different  percentages  of  African-American  and
Latino  students,  and  different  percentages  of poverty
to  see  whether  or  not  these  two  things  were  related
fairly  strongly.

And  what  we  saw  is that  if vou  look  at  the  schools
that  had  zero  to ten  percent  black  and  Latino  students,
which  are  almost  half the  schools  in the  United  States
at this  period,  only  7.8  percent  of those  --  7.7  percent  of
those

{Page  99}
schools  had  more  than  half poor  kids  in them,  only one
out  of fourteen  schools.

If you  look  at  the  schools  who  are  ninety  to  one
hundred  percent  black  and  Latino  and  those  are  the
schools  that  have  about  three  quarters  of  black
students  in Michigan,  for example,  those  schools  have
eighty-seven  percent  concentration  of poverty.  In other
words,  intensely  segregated  minority  schools  have  very
high  levels  of poverty,  and  those  very  high  levels,  of
poverty  are  linked  in  many  forms  of educational  and
equality.  And  poor  white  children  are  much  less  hkely
to  end  up  in impoverished  schools  than  poor  minority
children  because  they're  just  not  that  concentrated
residentially.

Q What's  the  measure  of poverty  here?

A Well,  this  is the  measure  of Free  Lunch.  It’s  the  only
data  that  exists  on  poverty  in  schools  at  the  national
level.  Free  Lunch,  of course,  is  set  by federal  statute
and  eligibility.  It’s  not  the  same  as  the  census  poverty
standards.  It’s  a  little  higher.  It’s  very  low  income.

124

Q I'm  sorry,  it’s  a  little  higher  meaning  that  you  can
have a little  more  income  --

A A little  more  income  than  the  poverty  standard,  but
these  are  families  whose  income  level  is not  adequate
to  provide  --  they  don't  have  enough  money  to  pay  for
lunch  for  their  children.

{Page  100]
Q Does  this  chart  mean  that  going  to  a  school  that’s
largely  black  and  Latino  means  going  to a  poor  school?
Does  it mean  that  poor  and  black  are  co-extensive?

A  It  means  that  if  you  go  to  a  school  that’s
overwhelming  black  and  Latino,  nine  times  out  of ten
it’s  going  to  have  concentrated  poverty.  If you  goto  a
school  that’s  overwhelming  white,  hardly  ever,  once  out
of fourteen.

Now,  there’s  many  kinds  of schools  that  aren’t  in
either  of these  categories.  But at these  extremes  there’s
a  strong  relationship,  a  serving  relationship.

And  when  we  looked  at  metropolitan  Chicago  we
looked  at eighteen  hundred  elementary  schools,  and  we
found  none  that  were  white  with  heavy  concentrated
poverty,  and virtually  all with  the African-American,  in
densely  segregated  schools.

Q Here's  what  I’m  wondering,  Professor  Orfield,  if the
level  of correlation  is  this  high  in  the  context  of the
affirmative  action  debate,  why  couldn’t  we  just
substitute  poverty  measures  for  race  in  admissions-
decision  making?

A Well,  there  are  several  reasons.  This  has  been  tried
in  a  number  of places  and  we  have a lot  of evidence
about  what  actually  happened.  But  at  the  extreme,
these  relationships  are  very  dramatic,  but you  can  still

are

Q How  do the  social  backgrounds  of those  middleclass
Latino  and  black  students  you  were  describing  differ
from  those  who  bear  white  counter-parts  in  white
suburbs?

A This  is  one  of the  issues  that  the  College  Board  is
looking  at  in  a  study  right  now  of the  High  Unequal
Education  Attainment  of Middleclass  Children  in this
country.  There  are  --  many  suburban  communities
around  the  country  are  collaborating  because  we  have
the  disturbing  differences  between  black  so-called

127

middle  class  achievement  and  white  middleclass
achievement.

When  vou  look  under  this  data  there’s  many
differences  between  middle  class  black  and  white  in
this

[Page  103}
country  on  an  average.  Middleclass  black  typically  do
not  come  from  a  family  with  the  same  kind  of
educational  history.  They don’t  come  from  a  family the
same  kind  of financial  resources.  In  this  country,  the
average  black  family  has  only  about  one  tenth  the
wealth  of the  average  white  family,  for  example.  The
wealth  gaps  are  much  bigger  than  the  income  gaps.
They're  much  less  likely  to  be  homeowners.  They're
much  less  likely  to live  in a stable  community.  They’re
much  more  likely  to  have  a  single  parent.  Most
middleclass  families  have  a  network  of  middleclass
relatives  that  support  them  through  trouble.  Most
middleclass  black  families  and  Latino  families  have
work  with  low  income,  and  families  that  they  have  to
support  through  trouble.  Most  middleclass  black  and
Latino  areas  are  near  poor  black  and  Latino  areas.
That’s  why  the  housing  market  works.  So  those  kids
are  exposed  to  many  more  negative  peer  group
influences.

There’s  a  wonderful  set  of  studies  done  by  a
psychologist  at  Northwestern  who  grew  up  in  a
community  like  this,  who wrote  this  book  called  “Black
Picket  Fences,”  who  explains  that  being  middleclass  is
not  the  same  across  racial  lines.
Families  are  less
middleclass.  They  have  less  middleclass  network,
resources,  respect,  connections.  Their  middleclass  is
much  more  vulnerable.  Their  ability  to  isolate  their

128

children  from  lower  class  and  negative  influences  is
much  less.  And  as  you  noticed,  for  example,  in
testimony  we

{Page  104]
just  heard,  Erika  was  telling  us  how  she  had  to  cut
herself  off from  her  community  to  make  it. This  is the
experience  of  many  of  our  middleclass  students  at
Harvard  and  other  universities.  They  have  been  in
situations  that  have  so  many  negative  influences  that
they  or  their  parents  had  to  decide  to  cut  themselves
off  almost  completely  from  their  setting  in  order  to
have  a  chance  to  make  it in the  educational  process.

There's  a  difference.  There’s  a  very  dramatic
difference  even  if vou  are  so-called  middleclass.  And
theres  a  danger  of  not  remaining  middleclass  and
being  exposed  to  non-middle  influences.  It’s  much
greater  for  minority  students.

There's  also  preferential  treatment  in schools.  I've
had  professional  colleagues  at all the great  universities
that  I’ve  been  at  who  are  African-American  or  Latino
and  almost  always  they  tell  me  that  one  or  more  of
their  children  have  been  placed  in a  lower  track  when
they  first  go  to  their  school.  And  they  are  professional
Ph.  D.  from  the  greater  universities  in  the  country.
And  they  go  and  they  raise  hell  with  the  principal  and
they  get  the  kid  put  back  in the  right  track,  but,  they
are  presumptively  put  in  the  wrong  track,  given  the
wrong  advice  because  of  their  skin  color.  They  are
children  of  the  most  educated  people  in  the  United
States,  but  they  are  seen  as  black  or  Latino  and  they
are  assumed  to  be  better  off  in  a  lower  track  course
until  somebody  tells  them

129

[Page  105]
that  their  parents  are  Harvard,  and  then  the  school
says,  I'm  so  sorry,  Doctor,  we  didn’t  know.  We  thought
this  child  was  black.

Q Professor  Orfield,  you  were  saying  I think,  you  were

Q If you  could  develop  that  please?

A As  the  housing  works  typically  after  a community
goes  through  racial  change,  it  then  gets  exposed  to
lower  income  families  pretty  rapidly  so  oftentimes  if

130

you  look  at  a  typical  pattern  in a  community  that’s  on
the  frontier  of racial  change

[Page  106]
the initial  families  that  move  into the communities  will
be  middleclass  people  who  are  seeking  middleclass
schools  and  often  integration.  But  after  they  come  in,
oftentimes  the  housing  is no  longer  shown  to  whites,
the  housing  market  shrinks,  and  within  a  few  years
they  begin  to  break  it up  into  rental  housing  and  show
it  to  poor  people,  and  it  begins  to  be  targeted  with
Section  Aid  and  other  kinds  subsidized  housing.  And
you  get  more  families  who  are  connected  to  basically
non-middleclass  backgrounds.

And  those  schools,  even  middleclass  schools  that
serve  minority  families  typically  have  substantially
higher  numbers  of low  income  children.  Many  of the
teachers  that  are  in  suburban  schools  are  completely
unprepared  to  teach  minority  kids.  They  have  no

of

now

Q What’s  the  fundamental  cause  of the  dynamics  that
you’re  describing?

A  Well,  there’s  an  interaction  between  residential
segregation,  school  segregation,  belief  in  the  society,
special  structure  to  perpetuate  inequality.  There’s  a
whole  system  of ghetto  station  and  inequality  that  has
it’s roots  in the  earl  20th  century,  never  been  broken  in
our  largest  metropolitan  areas.  That’s  the  patterns
that  have  really  not  changed  significantly  in one  anda
half century,  in these  large

[Page  108]
Industrial  centers  of hyper-segregation.  They  are  less
extreme  in  some  places  in  the  west  and  some  multi-
racial  metropolitan  areas.

Q Could  I summarize  those  causes  that  you  just  said,
they  apply  safely  with  the  word  racism?

A | usually  don’t  use  that  word  to  define  it.  It doesn’t
advance  the  debate  very  much.  But  I think  --  what  I
hke  to  talk  about  is  discrete  elements  of  racial
inequality.  There  is a system  of residential  segregation.
And  there’s  lots  of  innocent  victims  in  it.  Both  the
whites  would  like  to  remain  in  an  intergraded  place,
are  not  offered  the option  because  of the way  --  no  pure
whites  are  shown  into  the  community.  The  minorities
who  would  lke  to  live  in  a  intergraded  place  have
almost  no  opportunity  because  extreme  residential
segregation  and  so  forth.  And  there’s  lots  of innocent
bystanders  who  contribute  to  this  process  without
being  explicitly  racist  just  by having  belief  and  fear
that  they  wish  weren’t  true,  and  that  they  fear  are
true.

There  are  a lot of other institutions  that perpetuate
just by not  doing anything  to change  it. In other  words,
it’s  a deeply-rooted  set  of social  structure  and  practices
and  beliefs  that  have  a  very  strong  tendency  to
perpetuate  and  spread  itself  unless  there  is  explicit
intervention  to  stop  it or  to  create  a  different  option,
and those  are  difficult  to do. And they can  only be done
when  you

[Page  109]
decide  to  do them  consciously.

133

Q How  long  as  we  as  a  nation  been  engaged  in  policy
measures  like  that  to  intervene  in  those  structures,
and  in those  dynamics  of racial  inequality.

A Well,  in terms  of school  segregation  in my judgment
we  intervened  in the period  1965  and  1973,  and  mostly
in the  South.  We  really  never  did  desegregate  our  big
metropolitan  areas  in  the  North.  And  we  have
abandoned  making  any  significant  effort  to do that  by
the  middle  1970s  an  early  1980s.  And  now  in  1990s  we
are  dissolving  what  we  have  a  desegregation  plan  in
much  of the  country.  Even  where  we  had  a  plan  ora
like  you  had  the
consent  decree  or  something  --
Milliken  case  here  in Detroit,  it was  dissolved.  In our
book,  “Dismantling  Desegregation”  we  actually  at  the
effects  of  that  and  found  it  was  dissolved  without
achieving  any  of its  educational  goals.

THE  COURT:  That  was  the  Detroit  school

system?

THE  WITNESS:  No,  it  in  Grand  Rapids.  The
Milliken  II  case,  it was  decided  educational  remedies
which  we,  in our  judgment,  were  never  implemented
significantly  to  make  a  difference  for  kids  in Detroit.

THE  COURT:  So  it was  both  resolved  as  well  as

implementation.

THE  WITNESS:  Yes.

Q Professor  Orfield.  when  we  broke  you  were  talking
about  how  black  and  Latino  students  in  law  school  all
had
in
had_
contact’
with  their  white
contradistinction
classmates.

and  contrast

substantial

interracial

What  does  that  mean,  why  is that?

A Well,  if you  look  at  the  report  that  we  did  from  the
Michigan  and  Harvard  service,  it basically  shows

137

[Page  120]
that  for African  Americans  almost  all of them  had  had
significant  interracial  contact  either  residentially  or  in
school  or  both.  And  the  same  was  true  for  Hispanics,
but  half of whites  had  not.

I think  the  reason  is that  there’s  almost  never  an
adequate  preparation  given  for  all the  skills  you  need
to succeed  in a place  like competitive  law  school  in the
segregated  educational  environment.

And basically  for minority  students  who  go to elite
white  institutions  in this  society,  they  have  to  make  a
transition  to a  middle  and upper  class  institution  that’s
overwhelmingly  white  simultaneously.

So,  they  have  to  make a social  class  and a racial
transition  almost  instantly  and  fluently,  and to be able
to  perform  very  highly  in  that  new  setting  very  fast.
Because  there’s  very  few  support  systems  in  elite
colleges  and  universities  or  of professional  schools.

And  so  if  they  don’t  have  that  preparation  on,
which  is  usually  both  ability  to  understand  and
interpret  that  situation  and  the  academic  skills  that
you  need  to make  it, they have  to be really  remarkable,
or  they  don’t  have  a

[Page  121]
reasonable  chance.

Also  students  who  are  in  segregated  background
typically  don't  even  hear  about  a  lot of the  options  and
are  not  recruited  and  are  not  in  networks  where  they
know  people  have  been  to those  institutions,  don’t  have

138

all  the  kinds  of contacts  and  networks  that  lead  you
into  different  kinds  of choices  in your  life.

I've  had  a  good  many  African  American,  Latino
undergraduate  students  and  very,  very  talented  ones
who  never  thought  about  a  graduate  school  until  I
talked  to  them  about  it.

Nobody  had  ever  mentioned  it  to  them  in  their
lives  even  though  they have  been  in a  great  university.
And  no  one  in their  family  ever  had  contact  with  that
possibility.

So,  you  have  to  be in a  place  where  you  hear  about
the  possibilities,  and  where  there  are  real  connections
and  where  you  can  get  what  you  need.

And  what  you  need  to survive  is both  academic  and
an  understanding  of the  setting  that  you're  going  into.
And  those  are  very,  very  hard  to  get  in  isolated
situations.

of  resources.

And  they didn't  have  the connections,  so  they didn't
suggest  their  students  went  into  those  networks.  And
they didn’t  know  anybody  to call to explain  if a student
had  talent  that  didn’t  show  up  on  his  records,  or  how
would  he get.  Like  the  suburban  school  would  have,  for
example.

So,  there's  all  kinds  of  things  that  exist  in  vour
typical  middle  class  and upper  middle  class  school  both
in  terms
of
competition,  knowledge  of

of  resources,

connections,

levels

[Page  124]
options  and  so  forth.  They  just  don’t  exist  in  most
segregated  schools.

Most  of the  students  who  come  out  of those  schools
who  really  make  it are  incredibly  self  driven,  or  they
know  somebody  who  is  mentoring  them  very,  very
strongly.  Or  they  would  never  make  a  connection  like
that.

Q  Let's  change  the  subject  a  little  bit.  We've  been
speaking  mostly  about  black  students  up  to  now?

A Right.

Q  How  do  these  same  kinds  of  questions  come  out
when  we're  looking  at  the  Latino  student  population?

AI  think  that’s  a really good question,  since  the  Latino
student  population  is just exploding  in the country.  It's
gone  up  by orders  of magnitudes  since  the  ‘60s.

141

Latino  students  are  even  more  segregated  then
black  students  in  all  of the  United  States  today,  and
they  have  been  throughout  the  last  several  years.

And  they tend  to be isolated  in big cities  and  metro
areas  of relatively  small  number  of states.  The  nearest
one  here  is metropolitan  Chicago,  of course.

And  they  tend  to  be  in very  inferior

[Page  125]
schools,  and  they  also  tend  to  have  to  deal  with
additional  problem,  particularly  for recent  immigrants
of language,  and  linguistic  isolation  in the  residential
communities.  As  well  as  it’s  an  isolation  by race  and
class.  Those  are  really,  really  hard  problems.

In  our  college  admissions  processes,  we  don't
consider  fluency  of two  languages  to  be  an  asset,  we
only  measure  English  ability.

And  any  of us  who  would  try  to  get  admitted  to
competitive  schools  in Spain  would  probably  not  do too
well,  no  matter  how  intelligent  we  were.

So,  you  have  to  think  about  this  as  another  very
large great  disadvantaged  population.  Latino  students
are  much  more  likely  not  even  to make  it through  high
school,  and  they’re  less  hkely  to  go  to college  than  the
black  students,  if they  do  graduate  from  high  school.

And  they’re  very  often  very  poorly  prepared  for
making  the  kind  of transition  that  we  talked  about
between  two  different  worlds.

142

A  The  Native  American  student  population  is  very

and  it  has  not  been  studied  very  much  and  doesn't
appear  in  large  enough  numbers  in  most  of  our
national  surveys  to  describe  in much  detail.

And,  of  course,  represents  hundreds  of different
kinds  of  communities  within  the  Native  American
population.  But  their  general  problems  are  similar.

A Yes.  I did work  when  I was  in college  at reservations

experiencing  extremely  high  poverty  concentrations.
And  very  inadequate  schools,
lots  of  prejudice,
isolation.

through  12  attend  segregated  schools?

[Page  127]

A About  70  percent.

Q What  are  the  implications  of what  vou've  testified  to
about  educational  segregation  and  inner  quality?  Well,
two  things,  we'll  take  them  one  at  a  time.

First,  access  to college,  and  second  achievement  in

college?

A  Well,  if you  think  about  access  to  college,  you're
thinking  about  a  number  of different  things.  First  of
all,  you're  thinking  about  whether  you  acquired  the
precollegiate  skills  that  vou  absolutely  have  to  have  to
survive  in college.

Which  are  basically  skills  about  research,  writing
analysis,  basic  mathematical  computational  skills  and
so  forth.  And  increasingly  fairly  high  level  of  math
preparation.

Those  skills  are  very  hard  to  acquire  in a lot  of
segregated  schools,  and  they  are  fairly  normal  in
middle  class  schools.  There’s  a default  expectation  that
students  in  middle  class,  upper  class  suburbs  will
receive  those  and  that  they  will  be  taught  by people
who  actually  know  the  subject,  and*they  will  be  at  a
level  that's  appropriate

[Page  128]
preparation  for college.

144

That  does  not  exist  in  a  lot  of central  segregated
schools.  There's  not  the level  of competition,  there's  not
the  level  of training  by the  teachers.  And  the  classes
are  not  operated  at a level  that  actually  perhaps  vou  to
meet  the  minimum  requirements  of  the  collegiate
environment.

When  I was  a faculty  member  at the  University  of
Chicago,  we  would  routinely--my  Admissions  Office
would  routinely  reject  valedictorians  from  Chicago  high
school,  most  Chicago  high  schools  because  of  the
experience  that  they  could  not  survive  for  a  single
quarter  on  our  campus.

Even  if they  were  the  very,  very  best  student  in
their  school.  there  was  no  way  in the  world  they could
have  acquired  the  skills,  you  need  to  be  a  minimally
adequate  student  in  our  college.  No  matter  how
intelligent  they were  as  a person,  because  preparation
did  not  exist  in their  community.

So  we  would--I  didn’t  reject  them,  but  our
Admissions  Office  would  reject  them  all  the  time.  I
once  had  a  student  who  was  the  best  student  in many
years  in a  segregated  inner  city  school  in

[Page  129]
Washington,  D.C.  who  came  to  the  University  of
Chicago  and  she  was  my  advisee.

And  she  had  never  gotten  less  than  an  A plus  in
her  life  and  she  had  been  leader  of every  activity,  and
she  could  not  survive  the  first  semester  of our  classes.
And  she  had  a  mental  breakdown.

145

And  nothing  about  it was  her  lack  of intelligence
ability  or  an  education,  it was  simply  that  she  was  ina
completely  inadequate  preparation.

Now,  in logical preparation  you  need to understand
what  you  need  to  do to  get  ready  for college.  We  did  a
survey  of  several  thousand  students  in  Indiana,  we
found  that  most  students  whose  parents  hadn’t  been  to
college  really  didn’t  know  what  courses  they needed  to
take  to  get ready  for college,  and  they often  didn't  take
them.

Even  though  they  planned  to  inspire  to  go  to
college,  we  found  the same  thing in senior  surveys  that
were  done  in  Chicago.  Many  students-believed  they
were  ready,  when  they  weren't  ready  in  any  way  and
nobody  had  ever  told  them  differently.

Many  students  in  isolated  communities  have  no
way  of knowing  what  they need,  and  the counselors  in
big city  schools  are  typically

[Page  130]
overwhelmed
of  non-academic
counseling.  First  of  all,  community  problems  and
reporting  recuirements  and  so  forth.  They  have  almost
no  time  to  work  with  students.

kinds

with

all

The  students  when  we  would  survey  them  would
say,  they  have  almost  no  information  about  college
from  their  own  communities  or  from  their  parents,
because  often  times  nobody  there  has  been  to  college.
And  there  is no  network.

The  colleges  I’ve  taught  and  I teach  in my graduate
seminars  on  college  access  issues,  classes  that  included

146

many  admissions  officers  from  colleges  around  the
country,  or  people  who  have  been  admissions  officers.

They  routinely  report  that  they  do  not  recruit  in
most  central  city  high  schools  at  all,  because  there’s
nobody  there  that they could  possibly  get admitted  who
could  make  it on  the  campus.

I have  no  idea  whether  that  pattern  is true  here,
but  it is  in  much  of the  country.  And  the  reason  they
don't  recruit  isn’t  because  they  don’t  want  to,  it’s  that
they know  by and  large  that  there's  very  few  students
there  who  have  been  given  the  tools  they  need  to
survive.

[Page  131]

And  most  competitive  colleges  don’t  have  anything
to  really  helps  students  who  have  lacked  basic  tools.
It's  a  very  difficult,  difficult  requirement  at that  stage.

So,  the  network  isn’t  there,  the  knowledge  isn’t
there,  the  preparation  isn't  there.  And  the  college
doesn't  really  reach  out  very  effectively  into  most  of
these  particular  kinds  of schools.

So.  if a  student  gets  to  a  selective  college  from  an
institution  hke  that,  usually  there’s  an  individual
mentor  or  program  or  something  that's  identified  that
student  and just grabs them  and  giving them  the skills
and  motivated  them  and  filled  out  their  forms  and
done  all kind  of things  that  wouldn't  normally  happen.
It almost  never  happens  by accident.

There  was  this study done on  four children  growing
up  in Chicago  in Woodlawn,  a community  south  of the
University  of Chicago,  that  there  was  an  experiment  to

147

help  these  kids  in their  earliest  years  in  school,  it was
pretty  massive.

And  20  years  later  they  tried  to  see  who  got  to
college,  and  they  found  that  nobody  from  the  whole
experiment  had  gotten  to college  out  of

{Page  132]
that community  without  someone  actually  reaching  out
and  bringing  them  to  college.  Nobody  had  gotten  there
by themselves.

So,  you  know,  you  have--it’s  not  only  a  lack  of a
network,  it’s  really  just  nonexistent  connections
between  many  of  these  communities  and  what  we
need,  particularly,  to  get  into  a  competitive  college.

A  Well,  I have  a  special  interest  in  magnet  schools,
magnet  from  out  of desegregation  plans  and  I have
studied  them  in  Chicago  and  all  other  places.

Magnet  schools  give you  a chance,  but  even  though
it may  look  like  a  very  elite  school  inside  the  city,  it

really  looks  like  a  very  average  or  low  average  school  in

So,  my  own  children  went  to  a  school

[Page  133]
that  was  a  magnet  school  in  Chicago  Public  Schools
which  was  predominately  African  American,  and  had
some  wonderful  teachers  and  programs in  it.

And  that  school  was  recruited,  the  colleges  do
recruit  from  schools  like that  pretty  intensively.  There
are  students  who  do make  it, but  they’re  not  nearly  as
well  prepared  as  they  should  be.

Masically  that  school  as  best  I  could  tell,  was
equivalent  to  a  lower  level  of suburban  school.  That
school  had  the  only debate  team  that  was  left  out  of 65
Chicago  high  schools  at  that  time.

When  the  debate  team  went  off  to  a  suburban
school,  they  would  see  paid  staff  person  working  with
them,  they would  see  a library,  and they see  kids  going
to  debate  camp,  they  see  this  and  that  and  the  other
thing.  And  this  school  had  none  of those  things  there.

They  had  a  volunteer,  they had  no  materials,  they
had  no  room,  they  didn’t  have  a  media  center  to
support  them,  they  had  nothing.  And  they  were  the
only ones  in the  city who  could  even  amount  a  debate
team  at  that  point.

So you  have  these  inequalities  even

Q Id  like  to  shift  gears  here  and  talk  about--George,  if
you  could  put  up  Proposed  198.

THE  COURT:  Can  I just  ask  a  question.  Magnet

schools,  tell  me  a  little  more  history.

A Judge,  the  magnet  schools  really  are  a  result  of the
desegregation  plan that  started  in the  1970s  primarily,
and  they  started  out  in the  big scale  in Cincinnati  and
Milwaukee.

Congress  then  passed  an  amendment  to  the
Emergency  School  Aid  Act  to  provide  the  funding  to
create  magnet  schools  around  the  country.  That  was
part  of the  segregation  plan.  And  hundreds  of them
were  created.

[Page  136]

THE  COURT:  Tell  me  what  role  they  play  in  the

desegregation  plans?

after

faced

the  Milliken

A  In  the  desegregation  plans  the  problem  that  the
courts
decision  which
prevented  suburban  schools  from  being included  in the
any
desegregation
desegregation  in predominately  minority  central  cities
and  to  hold  any  middle  class  residence  of any  race.

was  how  to  achieve

plans,

151

The  solution  that  was  invented  in Milwaukee  and
Cincinnati  was  to  try  to  create  magnet  schools  that
would  be  integrated  and  offer  a  specifically  advanced
curriculum,  so  that  people  would  choose  to  go  to  an
integrated  school  in order  to  get  a  better  education.

THE  COURT:  So, part  of it was  education,  part  of
it was  to keep the diversity  from  moving  out  so  that,  at
least,  they had  some  that  they considered  to be a better
educational  school?

A Exactly.  And  the tool of it was  to create  something  so
attractive  that  it  would  be  diversed  and  it  would  be
reasonably  stable.

And  the  other  advantage  they  had  was  there  were
new  programs  so  they  could  create  new  faculties  and
start  new.  And  they could  also  offer

[Page  137]
ideas  that  werent  really appropriate  for everybody  ina
particular  neighborhood,  but would  be exciting  to some
kids  from  all  over  the  city.

So  it  offered  a  chance  for  a  major  educational

innovation  when  it’s  done  the  right  way.

THE  COURT:  But  part  of the  reason  was  to try  to
attract  those  persons  what  may  be  moving  out  and
leaving?

A Yes,  that  was  the  basic  reason.

THE  COURT:  Okay.

BY  MS.  MASSIE:

Q In  other  words,  it  was  a  substitute  for  mandatory
bussing  programs  in  the  like.  or  a  component  of
desegregation  that  was  non-mandatory?

A  It  was  non-mandatory,  and  it  did  involve  a  major
educational  experience  when  it was  done  right.  Now,
we  have  what I call little  magnets  which  had  an  actual
major  alternative  name  plate  magnet,  which  just
appeared  they  were  magnets,  but  didn’t  really  have
anything  really  strong.

THE  COURT:  Was  it done  right  anywhere?

A Yes,  it was  done  right  many  places.

THE  COURT:  Give  me  an  example.  Not  so  much

here,  but  why  did  it work  there  and  why  was

[Page  138]
it done  right  there  as  opposed  to  other  places  when  it
wasn't  done  right.

A  Well,  for  example  though,  Judge,  that  many  cities
that  are  created  racial  and  performing  arts  magnets.
They  offer  chances  for  kids  to  do  professional  level
preparation  in  the  fine  arts  and  visual  arts  and
performing  arts,  that  didn't  exist  anyplace  before.

Or science  and  math  magnets  that  are  similar,  that
really  offer  very  good  competitive  training  for college,
that  didn’t  exist  in the  school  district  before.  And  they
really  do have  good  staffs,  and they really  do do the job.

THE  COURT:  Did  it accomplish  anything  in terms

of diversity?

153

A Yes,  I think  it  helped  a  lot  of students  who  would
have  been  lost  otherwise.  It tended  to  stratify  kids  on
social  class  and  income  levels,  because  for  kids  who
had  knowledge  and  connections  figured  out  how  to get
into  them.  But  it did  hold  a  lot of middle  class  kids  in
the  central  city  schools.

THE  COURT:  So certain  cities  it did  work?

A Yes.  And  in  certain  schools  and  certain  cities  it was
quite  successful.

THE  COURT:  Okay.

Q And  the  broad  question  I want  to  pose  to  you,  and  I
think  there  will  be  a  number  of different  components
for  our  discussion  of that,  is  why  just  becoming  less

Put  it a  different  way,  why  do we  need  affirmative

A If you're  going  to  look  at  the  University  of Michigan,
for example.

Q And  let  me  interrupt  you  very  rudely,  I apologize.
Tell  us  what  the  data  is behind  this  chart,  it looks  like
it was  supplied  by the  LSAC,  what  is that,  if you  can
just  say  what  the  chart  represents  graphically?

155

A  Yes.  The  Law  School  Admissions  Council  is  the
organization  that  sponsors  the  law  school  admissions
test  and  kind  of  oversees  its  use.  And  it’s  the
institution  that’s  responsible  for the major  assessment
of entering  law  students  through  standardized  testing.

And  it sets  rules  and  policies  under

[Page  154]
which  those  tests  are  supposed  to  be used,  which  most
of the  colleges  don’t  follow.

But  basically  the  law  school  admissions  test  gives
you  a  score,  and  the  law  schools  tend  to  look  for
students  along a certain  point on  those  continuum.  And
tend  to use  those  tests  as  one  of their  major  criteria  for
admissions.

And  they tend  to be evaluated  in part,  particularly
by  influential  evaluation  like  U.S.  News  who  will
report  about  how  high  an  average  test  score  is on  this
test.  And  that  effects  who  applies  to  them  and  how
they are  viewed  in the  profession  and  so  forth.  So this
is a  very  important  tool.

The  Law  School  Admissions  council  say  it  should
never  been  used  as  the only way  to select  a  student  for
a law  school,  but sometimes  it is and sometimes  there’s
absolute  cut  points  and  so  forth.

The  law  school  admissions  test

like  other
standardized  tests  is  not  a  reliable  predictor  of  an
individual  student’s  performance.  It’s  a  moderately
good predictor  of a group  of students  with  a similar  test
score  for  the  first  part  of  their  law  school  career  in
terms  of what  their  grades  are.

156

[Page  155]

Law  school  admissions  test  has  nothing  whatever
to  do  with  measuring  whether  you  will  you  be  a  good
lawyer.  There  is no  evidence  to show  that  it’s linked  to
that.

Now,  here  if we  look  at  this  distribution  of scores,
we'll  see  the  University  of Michigan  is selecting  around
this  kind  of average  score  for  its overall  population  in
the  168  or  69  levels  as  I understand.

So, if the  population  that  has  almost  no  minorities
in  the  distribution  of the  test  scores  is  13,  an  almost
invisible  line  there.  And  basically  if it  were  to  lower
that  average  test  score,  for  the  moment  we're  just
considering  this  as  the  thing that  they're  admitting  on,
they're  obviously  admitting  on  multiple  dimensions.

But  if it  were  just  test  scores,  if they  were  to  go
down  by ten  points,  they would  expand  the  number  of
students  who  would  be eligible  to get in there  by many
times.

And  most  of the  students  who  would  be included  by
lowering  the  standard,  huge  majority  of them  would  be
whites.  Because  as  you  go down  the next  several  levels
in  test  scores,  the  line  of  increase  in  white  eligible
students  increases  much

[Page  156]
more  rapidly  then  the  line  of eligible  black  or  Asian  or
Mexican  American  students.

It's just  a tremendously  high  increase.  So you'll  be
getting  a  huge  number  of  students  that  would  be
eligible,  the  vast  majority  of them  would  be white.

DECLARATION  OF  BOB  LAIKD

Background.

1.

lam  a  citizen  and  resident  of Berkeley,  California.

2.1  graduated  from  the  University  of California,  Santa
Barbara  in  1962  with  a  bachelor’s  degree  in  Physical
Education.  | completed  a  Master  of  Arts  degree  in
English  at  UCSB  in  1965.  |  also  hold  California
General  Secondary  and  California  Community  College
teaching  credentials.

3.  | worked  in the  Admissions  and  Enrollment  Cluster
of  the  University  of  California  at  Berkeley  (UC
Berkeley)  continuously  from  my  hire  in  1977  until  my
retirement  in  November  1999.

4.  In  July  1993,  UC  Berkeley  appointed  me  as  the
Interim  Director  of  Undergraduate  Admission.  In
March  1994,  the  university  appointed  me  as  the
Director  of Undergraduate  Admission.  I served  in that
position  until  my  retirement  in November  1999.

5.  As  Director  of Undergraduate  Admission,  | either
reviewed  or  supervised  the  review  of  over  150,000
applications  to  UC  Berkeley.

forces,  and  other  bodies  that  studied  or  discussed
all  nine  undergraduate

on  several  occasions.
