Summary.

11.  On  July  20,  1995,  the  Regents  of the  University  of
California  approved,  by  a  vote  of  14  to  10,  Ward
Connerly’s  Special  Policy  1 (SP-l)  which  eliminated  the
consideration  of race,  ethnicity,  and  national  origin  in
admissions  at  the  University  of  California.
See
attached  Exhibit  2.

12.  After  that  vote,  Connerly,  and  Pete  Wilson.  the
Governor  who  had  appointed  him,  led  the  statewide
campaign  for  Proposition  209  which  placed  the
substance  of SP-1  in the  Constitution  where  it could  be
not  changed  by a future  vote  of the  Regents.

13.  The  California  electorate  approved  Proposition  209
in  the  November  2006  elections.

14.  For  a  variety  of legal  reasons,  SP-]  and  Proposition
209  did  not  go  into  effect  at  the  graduate  and
professional  level  until  the  class  that  entered  in  fall
1997  and  at  the  undergraduate  level  until  the  class
that  entered  in  fall  1998.

15.  In the  Regents’  debate  leading  up  to  the  adoption  of
SP-1  in  July  1995,  I,  along  with  the  admissions
directors
at  other  UC  campuses,  provided  oral
testimony  and  submitted  numerous  reports,  computer
simulations  and  other  data  to  the  Regents.  In

166

summary,  the  admissions  officers  advised  the  Regents
that  ending  affirmative  action  would  inevitably  result
in  a  sharp  and  immediate  drop  in  the  number  of
Afmican  American,
Latino  and  Native
Chicano,
American  students  who  were  admitted  to  Berkeley,
UCLA  and  the  other  selective  campuses  of  the  UC
system.

16.  In  fall  1997,  when  SP-]  and  Proposition  209  went
into  effect  at  the  graduate  and  professional  schools,  our
recommendations  and  predictions  were  confirmed
when  there  was  a  sharp  drop  in  the  admission  of
Latino,  and  Native
African  American,  Chicano,
American  students  at  the  selective  law,  medical  and
graduate  schools  throughout  the  UC system.  That  drop
has  continued  for  the  ten  years  that  Proposition  209
has  been  in effect.

17.  Our  recommendations  and  predictions  were  again
confirmed  in fall  1998,  when  SP-l  and  Proposition  209
went  into  effect  at  the  undergraduate  level.  At
Berkeley,  there  was  a  55  percent  drop  in the  number  of
underrepresented  minorities  admitted.  There  were  also
sharp  drops  at  UCLA  and  other  selective  campuses  in
the  UC  system.  That  drop has also continued  for the  10
vears  that  Proposition  209  has been  in effect,  especially
when  the  data  is interpreted,  as  it must  be against  the
background  of the  massive  growth  of the  Latino  and
Chicano  population  of California.  The  attached  Exhibit
4  1s  a  copy  of  the  official  UC  statistics  documenting
these  facts  at  the  undergraduate  level,  including  in
particular  at  UCLA  and  Berkeley.

18.  Both  before  and  after  the  date  when  SP-l  and
Proposition  209  went  into  effect,  the admissions  offices
at  UC  Berkeley,  UCLA  and  other  campuses  attempted

167

to develop  systems  that  would  allow  us  to  admit  larger
numbers  of  underrepresented  minority  students.
Despite  our  efforts,  we  could  not  devise  such  a  system
because  of  the  gaping  educational  inequalities  in
California  and  the country  and  because  of the  inherent
biases  and  inequities  in  grade  point  averages,  test
scores,  and  other  criteria  that  are  the  main  standards
that  are  available  to  use  in evaluating  applicants.

20.  I  concur  with  Mr.  Spencer's  testimony  that
Proposal  2  will  inevitably  result  in  a  drop  in  the
number  of  African  American,  Latino,  and  Native
American  students  who  are  admitted  to  the  University
of  Michigan.  I  would  add  that  that  drop  will  be

21.  There  are,  of  course,  demographic  differences
between  Michigan  and  California.
In  particular,
Michigan  has  a  far  larger  percentage  of its  population
that  is African  American  and  far  smaller  percentages
of its  population  that  are  Chicano,  Latino,  or  Asian.
These  demographic  differences  would  have  some  effect,
but  because  the  pattern  of  unequal  education  and
unequal  test  results  1s  common  to  African  American,
Latino

168

22.  In fact,  because  Michigan’s  high  schools  appear  to
be  far  more  segregated  and  the  pool  of  minority
students  from  integrated  schools  thus  far smaller  than
in  California,  the  long-term  effects  of  Proposal  2  in
Michigan  could,  if anything,  be worse  than  the  effects
of Proposition  209  in  California.  As  is set  forth  below,
at  Berkeley  and  at  every  other  selective  university  of
which  I am  aware,  the  underrepresented  minority
students  who  in general  had the best education  and  the
best  chances  of success  in the  university  were  students
who  attended  integrated  schools.

23.  I have  set  forth  below  the  facts  that  make  clear
that  Mr.  Connerly  and  anyone  else  who  was  familiar
with  the  admissions  process  knew  before  they  were
ever  adopted  that  Proposition  209  and  Proposal  2
inevitably  drive  down  the  number  of underrepresented
minorities  who  would  be  admitted  at  any  selective
university  for  the  time  during  which  those  proposals
remained  in effect.

Background--the  University  of California.

24.  In  order  to  explain  the  intent  and  effect  of
Proposition  209  in  California,  it is necessary  to  begin
by setting  forth  a  few  basic  facts  about  the  University
of California  and  about  the  students  who  apply  to  and
attend  the  UC.

25.  Other  than  the  California  State  University  system,
UC  is  the  largest  public  university  in  the  country.  It
has  nine  undergraduate  campuses  located
now
throughout  the  State.  In addition,  the  Hastings  College
of Law  and  the  Medica!  Center  at  UC  San  Francisco
are  part  of the  UC  system.  The  UC  is governed  by an
appointed  Board  of Regents.

169

26.  UC  is  governed  by  the  Master  Plan,  which  was
enacted  into law by the California  Legislature  in 1960.
Under  the  Master  Plan,  the  UC  is  the  leading
undergraduate,  graduate  and  research  university  in
the  state.  The  Master  Plan  also  established  a  state
university  system  and  a  system  of community  colleges.

27.  The  Master  Plan  requires  the  UC  to  admit  90
percent  of its  students  from  California  and  to  accept
the  top  12.5  percent  of high-school  students  in the  state
into  the  UC  system  as  a  whole.

28.  The  Regents  have  at  various  times  established
differing  standards  for determining  the  12.5  percent  of
students  who  are  “UC  eligible.”  In  general,  however,
those  standards  have  included  graduation  from  high
school,  a  specified  grade  point  average,  the completion
of certain  course  work,  and  certain  minimum  scores  on
standardized  tests.  After  Proposition  209  passed,  the
Regents  also  established  a  program  where  the  top  four
percent  of the  class  at  any  particular  high  school  was
considered  UC  eligible.

29.  Except  for  a  small  category  of  admissions  by
exception?--which  may  not  exceed  six  percent  of  the
entering  freshman  class  and  four  percent  of  the
entering  transfer  class--an  individual  UC  campus  may
only accept  persons  who  are  eligible  under  the  Regents’
criteria.

-_—-

“ “Admissions  by exception”  are  frequently  used  for  athletes,  for
applicants  who  have  had  a  non-traditional  educational  experience,
for  some  disabled  applicants,  and  for  students  with  outstanding
talent  in  the  performing  arts.  Prior  to  the  passage  of SP-]  and
Proposition  209,  admission  by exception  had  also  been  used  for
some  African  American.  Latino,  and  Native  American  students.

170

30.  Beyond  those  basic  standards,  each  UC  campus
decides  which  students  it  will  accept.  Because  the
campuses  differ  widely  in the  opportunities  that  they
afford,  there  is a great  difference  in the applicant  pools
at  each  campus  and  in  the  selectivity  «*  their
admission  systems.

UC.

They

professional

are  _ world-famous

31.  The  University  of California  at  Los Angeles  (UCLA)
and  UC  Berkeley  are  the  flagship  campuses  of  the
entire
research
universities  with  numerous  professors  who  have
received  Nobel  Prizes  and  other  recognition  for  their
advanced  scholarship.  Their  graduates  have  vastly
increased  chances  of  being  admitted  to  the  most
selective
in
and
Cahfornia  and  in  the  Nation.  Both  proudly  claim  that
they  have  produced  a  disproportionate  share  of  the
political,  economic,  and  professional  leaders  of  the
State.  Berkeley  and  UCLA  are  by far the  most  selective
undergraduate  campuses  in the  UC  system.  Since  the
early  1970s,  admission  to  Berkeley  and  UCLA  has
become  extremely  competitive,  with  the  two  campuses
currently  admitting  roughly  25%  of  their  freshman
applicants.

graduate

schools

32.  In  addition,  as  California’s  population  has  grown,
the  admission  standards  at other  campuses,  including
in  particular  UC  San  Diego  and  UC  Santa  Barbara,
have  become  increasingly  selective  because  these
campuses  are  perceived  as  offering  opportunities
beyond  those  on  other  UC  campuses.

SP-l_  and  Proposition  209  intentionally  aimed  at  a
sharp  drop  in  the  admission  of  African  American,
Chicano,  Latino,  and  Native  American  students  at
Berkelev,  UCLA  and other  selective  campuses.

171

33.  I have  read  Ward  Connerly’s  deposition  testimony
where  he  claimed  that  he  did  not  know  that
Proposition  209  would  result  in  a  sharp  drop  in  the
admission  of underrepresented  minority  students  at
Berkeley,  UCLA  and  other  selective  campuses.  Based
on  the  information  that  the  admissions  officers
provided  to the  Regents,  including  Mr.  Connerly,  that
claim  is absolutely  untrue.  He  and  the  other  Regents
knew  that  it was  essentially  undisputed  that  SP-]  and
Proposition  209  would  result  in a  dramatic  drop  in the
admission  of African Americans,  Chicanos,  Latinos  and
Native  Americans  at  the  most  selective  campuses.

34.  Because  Mr.  Connerly  targeted  the  policies  of UC
Berkeley  throughout  his campaigns  against  affirmative
action,  I played  a  special  role  in providing  the  Regents
with  information  that  made  clear  what  the  effects  of
SP-]  and  Proposition  209  would  be.

35.  In repeated  and  highly-publicized  meetings  of the
Board  of Regents  during  1994  and  1995,  I personally
heard  Connerly  repeatedly  focus  on  the  difference  in
GPAs  and  standardized  test  scores
of  students
admitted  from  different  racial  groups  as  the  primary
evidence  of what  he and  Governor  Wilson  called  “racial
preferences.”

36.  I, along  with  other  admissions  officers,  submitted
voluminous  material  to  the  Board  of  Regents  that
established  that  students  who were  white  or  from  most
Asian  backgrounds?  in fact had  significant  advantages

3 South  East  Asian  refugees  and  some  other  Asian  groups  suffer
disadvantages  similar  to  those  for  underrepresented  minorities.
Most  Asian  groups  who  are  allowed  into  the  US,  however,  have

172

on  those  criteria  over  applicants  who  were  African
American,  Chicano,  Latino,  or  Native  American.

37.  To  begin  with,  we  advised  the  Regents  that  the
educational  disparities  in California,  like  those  in the
Nation,  were  so  great  that  the  percentage  of  high-
school  graduates  who  were  even  eligible  to apply to the
UC  was  far  higher  among  whites  and  Asians  than  it
was  among  African  American,  Chicano,  Latino,  or

38.  We  further  informed  the  Regents  that  even  among
those  graduates  who  were  UC  eligible,  white  students
and  students  from  most  Asian  backgrounds  had,  in
general,  significant  advantages  over  African-American,
Chicano,  Latino,  and  Native  American  students.

39.  On  grade  point  averages,  for example,  the  Regents
had  decided  that  high-school  students  who  took  AP  or
honors  courses  should  receive  an  extra  grade-point
point on  that  course  when  the grade-point  average  was
calculated.  Although  the  Regents  policy  capped  the

high  levels  of educational  achievement  both  before  and  after  they
emigrate  here.

173

number  of additional  honors  grade-points  at eight,  this
cap  did  not  mitigate  the  differences  in  curricular

disadvantaged  high  schools.  Predominantly  white  and
Asian  high schools  were  far more  likely to have  a large
selection  of honors  and  AP  classes,  and  students  from
those  schools  had  a  distinct  advantage  on  GPA  over
students  who  went  to schools  that  were  predominantly

40. Although  the  UC system  does  not  require  letters  of
recommendations,  counselors  in predominantly  white
and  Asian  schools  were  very  knowledgeable  about  the
UC  admissions  process  and  would  often  make  very
effective  phone  calls  or  write  compelling  letters  of
support  for  students  from  their  schools  who  had
unusual  circumstances  or  who  were  appealing  denials
of  admission.  These  counselors  and  teachers  could
provide  far  more  knowledgeable  and  detailed  letters
than  schools  that  were  predominantly  composed  of
underrepresented  minorities  because  their  schools  had
far  better  teacher  and  counselor  to  student  ratios.
Many disadvantaged  high schools  in California  have  no
counselors  whatsoever.

41.  Above  all,  however,  white  students  and  students
from  most  Asian  backgrounds  had significantly  higher
mean  scores  on  the  standardized  tests  than  students
from  African  American,  Latino,  or  Native  American
backgrounds.

42.  In  part,  this  was  due  to  the  fact  that  white
Asian
applicants
backgrounds  had on  average  far higher  family  incomes

applicants

from

most

and

174

and  higher  parental  education  levels,  two  variables
with  which  test  scores  are  highly  correlated.

43.  The  advantage,  however,  was  far greater  than  this.
According  to UC  and other  studies,  we  knew  that  even
with  income  held  constant,  white  students  and
students  from  most  Asian  backgrounds  had  much
higher  mean  scores  on  the  standardized  tests  African
American,  Latino,  or  Native  American  students.

44.  In  fact,  we  knew  that  white  students  from  the
poorest  income  brackets  had  mean  test  scores  that
were  above  those  of African  American  and  Chicano
students  from  the  highest  income  brackets.

45.  As  the  admissions  director  at  Berkeley,  I advised
the  Regents  that  the  Berkeley  Admissions  Office  had
long  recognized  that  it  could  not  apply  the  existing
criteria  mechanically  if it was  to identify  talented  and
promising  students  across  racial  lines.  As I advised  the
Regents,  our
office  evaluated  scores  and  other
educational  criteria  in the  context  of the  background
from  which  the particular  student  came.  We recognized
that  a  given  score,  for  example,  meant  very  different
things  for a student  who  had  had every  advantage  and
one  who  had  struggle  to  overcome  disadvantages,  of
which  the  effects  of racial  discrimination  were  by far
the  largest.

46.  As  I  and  other  admissions  officers  advised  the
Regents,  if  we  could  not  evaluate  the  educational
qualifications  that  we  were  using  in light of the  race  of
an  applicant,  we  could  not  identify  many  of the  most
promising  and  talented  African  American,  Chicano,
Latino  and  Native  American  students.

175

47.  As  far  as  I can  recall,  every  study  provided  to  the
Regents  during  the  debate  on  SP-|  made  clear  that
eliminating  affirmative  action,  including  eliminating
the  consideration  of race  in  the  admissions  process,
would  inevitably  mean  a  sharp  drop  in the  admission
of underrepresented  minorities  at Berkeley,  UCLA  and
every  other  selective  campus.

48.  The  UC  Berkeley  Admissions  Office  even  ran
careful  computer  simulations  of the  results  that  would
occur  if  race  was  not  considered  in  the  admissions
process.  Those  simulations  demonstrated  that  there
would  be  a  devastating  drop  in the  number  of African
American,  Latino,  and  Native  American  students  at
Berkeley,  UCLA  and  every  other  selective  campus  in
the  UC  system.

49.  When  the  Board  of Regents  adopted  SP-!  on  July
20,  1995,  it knew  that  there  would  be  a  sharp  drop  in
the  admission  of African  American,  Chicano,  Latino,
and  Native  American  students  at  the  selective
graduate  and undergraduate  schools  in the  University
of California.

50.  Similarly,  when  Connerly  and  Governor  Wilson,
who  was  then  still  running  for  President,  announced
their  support  for Proposition  209,  they knew  that there
would  be  a  sharp  drop  in  the  admission  of  African
American,  Chicano,  Latino,  and  Native  American
students  at the  selective  graduate  and  undergraduate
schools  in the  University  of California.

After  the  adoption  of  SP-l  and  Proposition  209,  the
admissions  offices  could  not  stop  the drastic  decline  in
the  admission  of underrepresented  minorities.

176

51.  Before  SP-l  and  Proposition  209  went  into  effect,
the  Berkeley  admissions  office  and  many  other
admissions  offices  attempted  to establish  policies  that
would  reduce  the  drastic  drop  in minority  admissions
that  we  knew  would  occur.

52.  In  November  1997,  Berkeley  adopted  a  policy
statement  that  provided  for  a  flexible  review  of every
candidate
of
disadvantage  except  race.  A true  copy  of that  policy  is
attached  as  Exhibit  4.

every

53.  However,  because  race  was  bv far the  source  of the
largest  degree  of  inequality  in  education  and  in
educational  measures,  we  knew  that  our  policies  could
not  halt  the  decline  in  admissions  that  we  had
predicted.  The  contrast  between  the  last  Berkeley
freshman  class  with  affirmative  action  and  the  first
that  entered  after  Proposition  209  took  effect  starkly
illustrates  the  magnitude  of that  decline.

in  the  application  process  or  Asian  American  students  who

178

56.  In  a  single  year,  there  was  a  drop  of  932  African
American,  Chicano,  and Native  American  students  who
were  offered  admissions.  That  is, we  offered  admission
to  5d  percent  fewer  students  in these  groups  than  we
had  one  vear  before.

57.  In  the  years  after  1998,  the  admissions  office,
under  my  direction  and that of my successor,  continued
to  search  for  ways  to  increase  the  numbers  of African
American,  Chicano  and  Native  American  students
whom  we  could  admit.  But  even  after  SP-l  was
rescinded  by the  Regents  in 2001,  that  was  impossible
because  Proposition  209  remained  in  the  California
Constitution.  I have  attached  as  Exhibit  5 a copy  of the
official  statistics  on  new  freshmen  registrants  from  at
UC  Berkeley  from  fall  1996  through  fal]  2007.

58.  After  the  class  that  entered  in  fall  1998,  we,  for
example,  continued  to  consider  economic  and  social
disadvantage  in evaluating  an  applicant's  performance
on  educational  criteria.  But  as  our  studies  had  shown,
at every  level  of social  and economic  status,  candidates
who  were  underrepresented  minorities  had  adjusted
GPAs  and,  especially,  scores,  that  were  significantly
Jower  on  average  than  white  and Asian  applicants  from
the  same  social  and  economic  background.

59.  Moreover,  we  found,  as  almost  all universities  had
found,  that  African  American,  Chicano,  Latino  and
Native  American  students  who  attended  integrated,
middle  class  or  upper-middle  class  schools  were  the
best  prospects  for  admission  and  for  graduation  from
schools  lke  Berkeley.  For  a  variety  of reasons,  these

6

179

students  suffered  educational  disadvantages  relative  to
their  white  and  Asian  high-school  class  mates,
including,  above  all,  on  standardized  tests.

60.  With  affirmative  action,  we  could  admit  these
highly-promising  minority  students.  If,  however,  we
considered  social  or  economic  disadvantage  without
considering  race,  we  could  not  admit  them--and  we  lost
many  of  the  best  minority  students  to  private
institutions  or  to far less  selective  UC  campuses  where
they  would
the
opportunities  that  they  could  have  received  at  UCLA,
Berkeley,  or  similar  universities.

the  education

receive

not

or

61.  Beyond  these  specific  effects  of SP-]  and  Proposition
209,  the Regents  repeatedly  threatened  to audit  us  and
private  parties  occasionally  threatened  to  sue  us  for
alleged violations  of Proposition  209.  These  threats  put
a chill  over  a  decision  to  admit  any  African  American,
Chicano,  Latino  or  Native  American  student  who  was
not  among  the  very  few  such  students  with  relatively
high  test  scores  and  a  large  number  of AP  and  honors
classes.  No  such  chill  affected  decisions  to  admit  any
other  student  or  group  of students.

62.  Finally,  Proposition  209  harmed  Berkeley  and
UCLA  on  both  the  application  and the yield ends  of the
admission  process.

63. As fewer  underrepresented  minority  students  were
admitted,  both  campuses  had  to  fear  that  fewer  would
apply.  Especially  after  the  California  Supreme  Court
held  that  Proposition  209  banned  targeted  recruiting,
there  was  the  fear  that  the  whole  process  of building
up  minority  admissions--which  had  taken  years  to
accomplish--would  begin  to  unravel.

180

64.  Similarly,  at  the  other  end  of  the  admissions
process,  both  campuses  had  to  fear  that  fewer
underrepresented  minority  students  would  accept  the
offers  of  admission  that  the  campuses  were  able  to
make  in favor  of offers  from  other  institutions  that had
more  African  American,  Chicano,  Latino,  or  Native
American  students.

The  expected  effects  of Proposal  2 in Michigan.

65.  As  a  person  who  has  spent  most  of my  professional
life  participating  in or  writing  about  the  admission  of
minority  students  at  selective  universities,  I can  say
without  hesitation  that  racial  differences  between
applicants  are  by far  the  largest  differences  that  any
admissions  department  at any  selective  university  will
face.

66.  Michigan  is,  by  any  standard,
a_  selective
university,  and  particularly  selective  for the  one  third
of its  class  that  is taken  from  outside  the  State.

67.  The  Michigan  admissions  policies  that  I  have
reviewed  are  essentially  like  those  that  we  devised  at
Berkeley  after  the  passage  of  Proposition  209.  They
take  account  of  every  level  of  disadvantage  and
inequality  except  the  one  that  is most  crucial--race.

68.  | am  less  familiar  with  the  pool  of applicants  to
Michigan  than  I am  with  the  pool  of  applicants  to
Berkeley  and  UCLA.  Nevertheless,  the  problem  of
fewer  honors  and  AP  classes  for  underrepresented
minorities,  the  inequalities  in  secondary  education,
and,  especially,
in
disparities
large
standardized  test  scores  are  national  phenomena.

racial

the

181

69.  In  fact,  because  the  State  of Michigan  has  a  larger
percentage  of students  in  racially  segregated  schools
and  far fewer  African  American  and  other  students  in
integrated  schools,  it will be more  difficult  for Michigan
to  admit  underrepresented  minority  students  from
Michigan  than  it was  for us  to admit  underrepresented
minority  students  from  California.

70.  It  is  my  opinion  that  the  number  of  African
American,  Latino  and  Native  American  students  will
drop  substantially  in  future  years  at  Michigan.  It  is
further  my  opinion  that  the  admissions  office  can
somewhat  lessen  that  fall,  but  that  it can  not  stop  it as
long as  Proposal  2 remains  in effect.

71.  Finally,  it is  my  opinion  that  Ward  Connerly  and
others  who know  the realities  of admissions,  knew  that
SP-1,  Proposition  209,  Proposal  2  and  all  similar
proposals  will  result  in  a  drastic  reduction  in  African
American,  Chicano  and  Native  American  students  at
any  selective  undergraduate  institution  and  at  any
selective  graduate  or  professional  school.

I declare  under  penalties  of  perjury  that  the
foregoing  is true  and  correct.

Bob  Laird
Berkeley,  California.
January  5, 2008

6.  I am  very  familiar  with  the  SAT,  ACT,  LSAT,  and
MCAT  and with  the various  statistical  claims  made  for
their  validity  in the  admissions  process.

183

7.  Because  of  my  knowledge  about  the  role  of
admissions  test  scores  in influencing  the racial  makeup
of student  bodies  the U.S.  Department  of Justice’s  Civil
Rights  Division  engaged  me  as  a  paid  expert  in  its
intervention  in the  Mississippi  higher  education  case
(Ayers).  I have  also  served  as  an  expert  in other  cases
including  the  challenge  to  MEAP  scholarships  in
Michigan,  a  challenge  to  the  Massachusetts  teacher
licensing  examination,  a  challenge  to the  scoring  on  a
nationally  administered  teacher  licensing  examination.

matter,

Background  on  the  adoption  of standardized  tests.

8.  Most  major  public  universities  did  not  require
applicants  to  submit  SAT  or  the  ACT  scores  until  the
“Baby  Boom”  generation  entered  college  in  the  early
1960s.  Before  that  date,  only  the  Ivy League  colleges
and  a  few  private  and  public  institutions  used  such
tests  for  admissions  decisions.

9.  At  the  time  when  many  public  universities  first
adopted  the  SAT  or  the  ACT,  it was  generally  known
that  there  were  differences  in  scores  associated  with
the  race,  national  origin,  family  income,  gender,  and
similar  factors.  In  addition,  it  was  known  that  the
initial  author  of the  SAT  tests  and  many  of its  early
advocates
in  racial
superiority  and  eugenics.  But  the  test  manufacturers
claimed  to have  corrected  the  bias  in the tests  and  had
dissociated  themselves  from  the  eugenics  and  racial
superiority  views  of the  early  advocates  of such  tests.
Until  1980,  the  manufacturers  did  not  release  data  on
the  differences  in  scores  associated  with  the  race,
national  origin,  family  income,  gender,  and  similar

had  openly  stated

beliefs

184

factors.  The  scale  of the  differences  in  scores  based  on
race  and  income  was  thus  not  known  before  1980.

10.  Beginning  in  1980,  however,  the  Educational
Testing  Service  (ETS),  which  manufactures  the  SAT,
and  the  College  Board,  which  sponsors  the  test,
released  such  data  publicly.

11.  From  the  time  of the  release  of that  data  forward,
there  has  been  no  question  that  there  are  very  large
differences  in the  scores  on  the  SAT,  ACT  and  similar
tests  that  are  associated  with  race,  national  origin,
and,  to  a  lesser  extent,  family  income.

Racial  and  national  gaps  in  test  scores

12.  The  attached  Exhibit  1  is  a  statement  of  the
College  Board's  own  data  on  the  way  in which  average
SAT  scores  in 2007  varied  by the  race  of the  applicant
and  by the  income  of his  or  her  family.

13.  The  attached  Exhibit  2 is a  statement  of the  ACT,
Incorporated’s  own  data  on  the  way  in  which  average
ACT  scores  in 2007  varied  by the  race  of the  applicant
and  by the  income  of his  or  her  family.

14.  The  attached  Exhibit  3 is a  statement  of the  Law
School  Admissions  Council  own  data  on  the  way  in
which  average  Law  School  Admissions  Test  (LSAT)
scores  in  2007  varied  by the  race  of the  applicant  and
by the  income  of his  or  her  family.

15.  The  attached  Exhibit  4  is  a  statement  of  the
Association  of American  Medical  Colleges’  own  data  on
the  way  in which  average  Medical  College  Admissions

185

Test  (MCA)  scores  in  2007  varied  by the  race  of  the
applicant  and  by the  income  of his or  her  family.

16.  As  can  be  seen  from  Exhibits  1,  2,  3,  and  4,  race
affects  the  average  variation  in  test  scores  far  more
than  income  or  any  other  factor.

17. Moreover,  there  is voluminous  research  that  shows
that  even  when  white  and  minority  test  takers  come
from  the  same  income  groups,  there  is a still  a  very
great  discrepancy  in the  average  test  scores  based  on
the  race  of the  applicant.

18.  In fact,  even  racial  minorities  who  have  the  same
educational  accomplishments  as  their  white  peers
score  far lower  on  standardized  tests.  For example,  ina
study  of applicants  to  the  University  of California  at
Berkeley  Law  School  (Boalt  Hall),  Mr.  David  White
demonstrated  that among  applicants  who had attended
the  same  elite  undergraduate  institution  who  had  the
same  undergraduate  grade  point  average,  Native
Americans  scored  on  average  4.0  points  lower  on  the
LSAT  test,  Hispanics  scored  6.8  points  lower.  and
African  Americans  scored  9.2  points  lower.

19.  The  University  of California  has  published  similar
data.  UC  and  the  SAT:  Predictive  Validity  and
Differential  Impact  of  the  SAT  I and  SATII  at  the
University  of  California  (Saul  Geiser  with  Roger
Studley,  University  of  California,
of  the
President,  2001).

Office

20.  In  the  last  twenty  vears,  there  have  been  some
variations  and  some  improvements  in  the  average
score  gaps  by race  set  forth  in  Exhibits  1 through  4.
But  there  has  been  at  all  times  a  significant  gap  by

186

race  in  average  scores  on  the  SAT,  the  LSAT,  the
MCAT,  and  the  ACT.

21.  There  are  many  explanations  offered  for  the
differences  in test  scores--but  there  is no  dispute  as  to
the  fact  of those  differences.

What  test  scores  claim  to  predict

selective

admitted

22.  There  is also  no  dispute  that  in the ranges  of scores
or
to
of  students
universities,  the  differences  in  test  scores  between
white  students  and  African-American,  Hispanic  and
American  Indian  students  say little  or  nothing  about  a
student’s  chance  of success  in college  or,  still  less,  in
his  or  her  later  professional  life.

colleges

23.  The  companies  that  manufacture  the  SAT,  the
ACT,  the  LSAT,  and  the  MCAT  have  long  ago
abandoned  any  claim  that  these  tests  measure
“intelligence,”  “aptitude,”  or  any  similar  quality.  In
fact.  the  College  Board  and  ETS  have  given  up  the
names  “Scholastic  Aptitude  Test,”  and  “Scholastic
Assessment  Test,”  both  of which  were  prior  names  for
the  “SAT.”  The  test-makers  have  retained  the  name
SAT,  but  now  assign  no  meaning  to  those  initials.

24.  The  only claim  made  by the  test  manufacturers  is
that  there  is  some  level  of  correlation  between  test
results  and  the  average  grades  during  the  first  year  of
college,  law  school,  or  medical  school.

25.  The  explained  variation  refers  to  the  portion  of a
statistica]  outcome  (in this  case  first  year  grades)  that
is  accounted  for  by a  particular  predictor  (e.g.  high-
school  grades  or  test  scores)  or  set  of predictors  (grades

187

and test  scores  combined  by a  specified  formula).  Thus,
the  figures  set  forth  below  indicate  that,  on  average.
the  test  scores  alone  account  for less  than  a  quarter  of
the  difference  among  students’  first  year  college
grades.

a

of

first

such

small

portion

26.  The  reason  that  admissions  test  scores  (either
alone  or  in  conjunction  with  high-school  grades)
explain
year
undergraduate  grades  (generally  less  than  a  third,  no
matter  how  precise  the  predictors  are)  and  even
smaller  percentages  of more  meaningful  outcomes  such
as  graduation  rates  or  four-year  grades  (typically  one-
fifth  to  one  quarter)  is  that  the  predictors  do  not
include  many  factors  that  are  significant  for academic
in
performance.
overcoming
creativity,
openness  to  new  ideas,  willingness  to  seek  help,  etc.
Nor do they account  for the  fact  that  the  individuals  in
question  are  teenagers,  who  are  still  developing
intellectually,  personally  and  socially.

These
obstacles,

self-discipline,

perseverance’

include:

27.  There  is  some  dispute  over  how  much  predictive
power  the  test  results,  standing  alone,  have  as  to  an
applicant’s  likely  first-year  grades.  Even  the  College
Board  and  ETS,  however,  claim  that  the  SAT  only has
the ability to predict  22 percent  of the variation  in first
vear  grades  of  college  freshmen.  Other  researchers
have  published  data  that  puts  the  figure  much  lower.

28.  The  ACT  claims  to  predict  about  20  percent  of the
variation  in  first-year  undergraduate  grades.  The
LSAT  claims  to  predict  13  percent  of the  variation  in
first-year  law  school  grades.  The  MCAT  exams  make
similar  claims.

188

29.  Every  college  and  graduate  school,  however,
considers  scores  on  tests  in  conjunction  with  an
assessment  of  high-school  or  college  records.  The
question  is therefore  not  how  much  predictive  power  as
to  first-year  grades  that  the  tests  have  standing  by
themselves,  but  how  much  they  add  to  the  prediction
from  high-school  or  college  grades.

30.  The  College  Board  and  ETS  now  claim  that  the
SAT  adds  eight  percent  to the  predictive  power  of high-
school  grades  alone  in  forecasting  first  year  college
grades.  ACT  Inc.  makes  a  similar  claim  about  their
test.  The  Law  School  Admissions  Council  now  claims
that  the  LSAT  adds  about  15  percent  to  the  power  of
college  grades  alone  in predicting  first year  grades.  The
Association  of American  Medical  Colleges  now  claims
that the MCAT  adds  21 percent  to the predictive  power
of college  grades  alone  in forecasting  grades  in the first
two  years  of medical  school

a  “neutral”

31.  Even  assuming  that  these  claims  are  true,
requiring
on
standardized  admissions  tests  would  have  little  or  no
effect  on  improving  the  graduation  rates  of  those
minorities  who  are  admitted.

application

of  scores

32.  First,  the tests  make  no  claim  to predict  graduation
rates,  so  there  is no  evidence  that  raising  the test  score
requirements  would  change  the  average  graduation
rate  at  all.

33.  Second,  the  graduation  rate  from  selective  colleges
and  law  schools  is already  very  high  for  all  students--
including  minorities.

189

34.  Third,  James  Crouse  and  Dale  Trusheim  have
documented  statistically  that  requiring  a  “neutral”
application  of  the  test  scores  would  not  assist  in
identifying  the particular  minority  students  who would
fail  to  graduate  if  they  were  admitted.  See  James
Crouse  and  Dale  Trusheim,  The  Case Against  the SAT,
Chapter  Five,  pp  89-121.  Since  that  time,  no  one  has
disputed  their  findings  in a  published  work.

35.  The  factors  that  primarily  determine  a  minority
student’s  success  in college  that  are  not  measured  by
test  results.  For  example,  the  tests  do  not  claim  to
family
account
responsibilities  that.may  affect  a  student’s  chance  of
success.  Nor  can  they  measure  factors  such  as  work
habits,  motivation,  peer  and  family  support,  and  other
factors  that  affect  graduation  rates.

di§ifficulties

financial

for

or

36.  Recognizing  the  facts  set  forth  above,  many  private
liberal  arts  and  non-selective  public  colleges  have
dropped  the  requirement  for an  SAT  or  ACT  test.  The
granting
number  of  accredited,
colleges  that  do  not  require  many  or  all  applicants  to
submit  test  scores  before  admissions  decisions  are
made  is now  in excess  of 760.

bachelor-degree

37.  However,  the  tests  continue  to  be  required  by all
selective  public  universities.  Moreover,  the  LSAT  is
required  for  accreditation  at  law  schools  by  the
American  Bar  Association.

38.  Finally,  there  are  strong  political  pressures  for
continued  use  of the  tests.

39.  In  universities  where  they  are  still  used,  it  is
undisputed  and  well  known  that  requiring  a  “neutral”

190

application  of test  scores  between  white  applicants  and
African-American,  Hispanic  and  Native  American
applicants  would  exclude  large  numbers  of  minority
students  with  no  increase  in  the  success  rate  of the
much  diminished  number  of those  minority  students
who  were  still  admitted.

I  declare  under  penalties  of  perjury  that  the
foregoing  is true  and  correct.

Robert  A. Schaeffer
Sanibel,  Florida
January  7, 2008

THEODORE  SPENCER,

was  thereupon  called  as  a  witness  herein,  and  after
having  first been  duly sworn  to testify  to the truth,  the
whole  truth  and  nothing  but  the  truth,  was  examined
and  testified  as  follows:

EXAMINATION

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  Okay.  Can  you  state  your  full  name  for the  record.

A.  Theodore  Spencer.

216

MR.  ROSENBAUM:  Mr.  Sauer,  can  you  hear

okay?

MR.  NIEHOFF:

This  is  J.D.  --  how  do  you

pronounce  your  last  name?

MR.  TALIAFERRO:  Taliaferro.

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  Mr.  Spencer,  how  are  you?

A.  Fine.

Q.  How  are  you  doing  today?

[Page  6]
A.  I'm  all  mght.

Q.
Now,  you're  under  oath,  so  | want  to  ask  vou,
recognizing  the  fact  that  you’re  under  oath,  who  is
going  to  win  the  Michigan-Ohio  State  game?

MR.  NIEHOFF:  Objection.  Calls  for speculation.

THE  WITNESS:  I am  certain  that  Michigan  will

win  this  year.

217

THE  WITNESS:  But  more  than  that.  we  owe  Ohio

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

A.  Yes.

And  you  discussed  the  procedures  with  your

Q.
counsel?

A.  Yes,  I have.

how  we re  going  to  be  proceeding?

A.  Not  at  this  point.

Q.  At  any  time  you  need  a  break  --

THE  WITNESS:  No,  I'm  just  clearing  my  throat.

[Page  7]
BY MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  Any time  you  need  a  break,  just  let  me  know;  we'll

Are  you  saying  yes?

218

A.  Yes.  I'm  saying  ves.  Sorry.

Q.  Mr.  Spencer,  what's  vour  present  position?

A.  Iam  the  Vice  Provost  and  Executive  Director  of
Admissions  at  the  University  of Michigan.  Associate
Vice  Provost.

Q.  I was  negligent  in requesting  a  vitae.  Do you  have
a  resume  or  vitae?

A.  Ido.

Q.  Is it about  a  thousand  pages  long?

A.  Not  quite.

MR.  ROSENBAUM:  Can  we  get  that  at  some

point?

MR.  NIEHOFF:  Not  a  problem.  Mark,  I should
say  that  just  as  a  matter  of  practice,  for  my  own
convenience,  if parties  ask  for  documents  during  the
deposition.  | ask  to  send  me  just  a  reminder  e-mail  or

they've  asked  for,  as  well.  You  don’t  need  to  wait  for a

[Page  8]
notes.  so  I ask  that  people  send  me a list  of whatever
they're  asking  for.

MR.  ROSENBAUM:  Okay.  That's  fine.

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  And  recognizing  the  fact,  Mr.  Spencer,  that  we  re
going  to  get  a  copy  of your  vitae,  you  don't  have  to  go
into  excruciating  detail.  but can  you just briefly  sketch
out  for  us  your  education  background?

A.  Yes.  lL attended  Melrose  High  School  in Memphis,
Tennessee.  Graduated  from  there  in  1959.  Attended
Tennessee  State  University  in  Nashville,  Tennessee.
Graduated  from  there  around  1963.  Not around,  but in
1963.  And  I was  commissioned  as  a  lieutenant  in the
United  States  Air  Force  and  completed  my  master’s
degree  while  on  active  duty  from  Pepperdine  around
1981.

Q.  And  the  master’s  was  in  --

A.  Sociology.  The  BS  was  in  political  science.

Q.  Okay.  And  can  you  briefly  sketch  out  for  us  your
employment  background?

A.  Well,  as  I stated,  my  first  real job was  as  an  officer
in  the  United  States  Air  Force.  And  I was  on  active
|
duty  for  about  4  1/2  years.  Got  out  for  a  period.
worked  for  IBM  in  the  city  of  Detroit,  and  then
returned  to  the  military  after  about  three  years,  and

[Page  9]
went  on  active  duty,  and  spent  a  great  deal  of time  at
the Air  Force  Academy  in Colorado  Springs  from  about
1976  until  1989,  when  I arrived  at  the  University  of
Michigan.

Q.  What  was  your  rank?

A.  Lieutenant  Colonel.

Q.  So  you  came  from  the  Air  Force  Academy  to  the
University  of Michigan?

A.  I did.

Q.  And  what  was  vour  --  when  was  that?

A.

1989.

Q.  What  was  your  first  position  with  the  University  of
Michigan?

1 was  the  associate  director  for  marketing  and

A.
recruiting.

and  responsibilities  entail?

A.
Pretty  much  identifving  students,  prospective

Michigan,  encouraging  them  to enroll  at the University
of Michigan.  And  then  again  making  certain  that I --
we  had  a  presence  in all of the  state  high  schools.  And
because  were  a  national  university.  and  most  of the

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  Mr.  Spencer,  you  said  one  of your  charges  was  to
assure  that  the  University  of Michigan  had  a  presence
in  state  high  schools  throughout  Michigan;  is  that
nght?

A.  That's  correct.

So  that’s  nothing  new.

[Page  11]
Q.
That  is,  Michigan  has
always  attempted  to  reach  out  to  students  in  high
schools  throughout  the  state  of Michigan;  is that  right?

A.  That  is correct.

Q.  And  that  would  include  inner-city  schools?

A.  It certainly  would.

i) i) bo

And  that  would  include  inner-city  schools  in

@.
Detroit.

A.  Yes.  it would.

Q.  And  have  vou  made  that  a  matter  of particular
attention  to  --  to  you.  to  make  sure  that  Michigan  was
well-represented  and  well-understood  in  those  high
schools?

A.  Ihave.

MR.  NIEHOFF:  You  mean  --  just  so  the  witness
understands  the  question.  you  mean  has  he  made  it a
matter  to  him  personally?

MR.  ROSENBAUM:  Yes.

THE  WITNESS:  Yes.  The  answer  is yes.

BY  MR.  ROSENBAUM:

Q.  And  that  goes  all  the  way  back  to  1989?

A.  It does.

Q.  And  can  you  describe  for  us.  please,  what  that
means,  having  a  presence  in high  schools?

Well,  one  of the  things  it  means  is  that  we  —  we

A.
established  an  office  in the  city of Detroit.  And

[Page  12]
what  we  try to do is again  to identify  voung  people  that
might be interested  in the  University  of Michigan,  or  in
some  cases  identifying  students  who  have  the  potential
to  be  students  at  the  University  of Michigan,  and  to

design  programs  and  invite  them  and  their  families  to
the  events  that  the  University  of Michigan  sponsors.

Q.  Soas  far as  vou  know,  there  are  not  pockets  of high
schools  in Michigan  that the university  has  not  already
reached  out  to  and  made  its  welcoming  message
known;  is that  correct?

Q.  All  right.  Now,  help  me  understand  some  of the
changes  that  your  office  began  to  design  and
implement.
One  of  the  areas  was  with  respect  to
initial  evaluation  of the  application;  is that  nght?

A.  That  is true.

Q.  And  help  me  understand  what  happened  there.
What  were  the  changes  that  took  place?

240

A.  Well,  the  primary  change  was  that  we  went  froma
single  evaluation  to  a  multiple  evaluation.  And  what
that  in summary  really means  is that every  application
is reviewed  at  least  twice.  And  so  the  readers  that  we
hired,  the  16,  18 readers  that  we  hired,  will  review  an
application  and  then  they  will  write  remarks  and
evaluation  of those  --  of that  particular  file.

And  then  it  moves  to  the  counselor  who  is
responsible  for that  particular  territory  or  high  school.
And  then  they  do  what  we  call  a  blind  read;  in other
words,  they  have  no  idea  what  the  first  person  wrote.
And  then  they  make  their  evaluation  of the  student,
and  then  they  will  put  their  evaluation  on  --  in  the
computer.

The  third  read  then  comes  about  in  the  event

[Page  29]
that  there  are  significant  gaps  between  reader  No.  1
and  reader  No.  2.
And  that  third  read  is  then
generally  done  by  people  who  are  part  of  the
management  staff,  our  assistant  directors  and  our
associate  directors  and  myself.  I should  also  add  that
the  third  read  may  also  include  faculty  members,  both
at  LS&A  and  engineering,  who  will  also  look  at  those
records  In  some  cases.

Q.
And  the  readers  you're  talking  about  that  you
hired,  those  included  former  educators;  is that  right?

A.  There  was  a  great  pool  of folks  who  acquired  the
positions.  Many  were  former  educators.  Some  were
people  who  worked  at the  University  of Michigan,  who
retired.  We had civic  leaders  who  were  also  involved  in
that,  who  had  recently  retired.  So it was  a  very  broad

241

group  of folks  who  felt  as  though  --  and  did  do  a  very
good job working  in the  office.

Q.  And  what  criteria  did  you  apply  in  deciding  who
should  be hired  to be readers?  How  did you  decide  who
to  choose?

A.  Well,  we  did  want  to  look  at  some  people  who  had
had  some  background  in  education,  either  at  the  K
or  had  some

involvement  with  students  and  parents  in  programs,

training  entails?

this

Q.  Tell  me  what  your  understanding  of that  word  is.

Well,  after  the  decision  in  Grutter,  with  the  law
A.
school,  we  had  a  great  road  map  of  what  a  holistic
review  process  should  look  like.  And  so  we  really
began  with  that  document  and  that  process  of loo)xing
at  many,  many  different  factors  in  reviewing  the

250

student’s  application  and  doing  it in a  multiple  review
process.

And  so  holistic  means  that  the  decisions  are  not
just  based  on  grades  and  test  scores,  it means  that  it’s
not  just  based  on  perhaps  being  an  alum  or  any  one
particular  factor,  race  or  anything  else,  but  rather  on
the  composite  review  of all  the  information  that  the
student  provides,  and  then  to  come  up  with  a
recommendation  to  whether  or  not  we  think  this
student  would  be  a  good  fit for  the  university.

ADMISSION  OF  FRESHMAN
A
A.  DEFINITION

OF

FRESHMAN
APPLICANT:  FTIAC  (first  time  at  any
college).

A  freshman  is  defined  as  an  entering
student  who  has  never  attended  any  college
following  high  school  graduation.
This
definition  includes  students  enrolling  in the
fall  term  who  take  college  classes  as  guest
students  who  enter  with  advanced  standing
by earning  college  credit  though  Advanced
Placement  Examinations  or  courses  taken
at  a  college  prior  to  high  school  graduation.

All  other  students  will  be  designated  as
transfer  students  (Tvpe  4) with  Level  (1,  2,
3,  4)  being  determined  by  the  about  of
transferable  credit  projected  to  the  term  of
entry.  e.g.  Level!  1.  1-24  credits.  This  will
include  those  freshman  level  students  who
attend  college  in  the  fall  and  apply  for
admission  to  LSA  for  the  winter  semester.
They  will  be  coded  as  transfer  students
(Type  4) at the  freshman  level  (Level  1) and
be
to _ transfer
according
guidelines  for  Winter  2007.

ACT/SAT  scores.

(1)All  freshman  applicants  are  required  to
have  their  ACT/SAT  scores  sent  directly
from  the  testing  agencies  (composite
ACT/total  SAT).

(2)  Beginning  summer  2006,  freshman
applicants  will  be  required  to  submit  the
results  from  the  new  SAT  with  the
writing  section  or  the  ACT  with  optional
writing  test  for admissions  purposes.

(3)  The  highest  set  of scores  will  be  used  to
make  the  admission  decision.
For  new
SAT,  the  total  of  the  critical  reading
(formerly  verbal)  and  math  sections  will
be  used.

(4)  A  report  of applicants  with  new  scores
that  reflect  a  higher  test  range  will  be
run  weekly  after  the  receipt  of  Fall  test

a.  Honors  Consideration.

The

Honors

The  Honors
Program  reviews  the  files  of  students
admitted  to  LSA,  using  a  number  of
queries  on  the  database  to  find  potential
candidates.
Program
searches  for  students  whose  admission
files  strongly  indicate  that  they  are  well
suited
challenging
academic  program.  They  are  looking  for
students  who  want  to  engage  in  active
inquiry  and  who  are  eager  for  a  vigorous
intellectual  community.  They  no  longer
publish  threshold  figures,  although  they
use  their  former  thresholds  as  one  of our
queries.  Instead,  we  have  published  on
our  website  the  median  ranges  for  SAT

especially

they  welcome  requests  for  review  from
students  who  wish  to be considered.  Thev
also  welcome  recommendations  from  high
school  counselors  and  QUA  counselors
and  leaders.  They  no  longer  promise  that
a  student  will  hear  within  four  weeks  of
admission  to  LSA:  they  do,  however,
commit  to  informing  students  of  their
decision  in  time  for  them  to  matriculate
by May  Ist.

Counselors  will  update  the  CSP  Checklist  for

update  to  the  CSP  must  be  marked  up  on  the
application  folder,  the  Checklist  created  and
values  entered.  The  CSP  admit  letter  must  be
ordered.

Summer  Bridge  Program:
The  LSA  or  ARC  Review  Committee  must
approve  all  admissions  to  the  Summer  Bridge
Program.

If admission  to  the  Summer  Bridge  Program  is
approved,  the  term  of entry  must  be changed  to
Summer  prior  to  the  final  EVALUATION
RECOMMENDATION  being  entered  on  the
database.

The

In early March  each  school  or  college  will  produce
a  roster,  which  includes  all  admitted  students
who  completed  preferred  admission  question  on
the  application.
The  applications  of  these
students  can  be  viewed  through  imaging.  The
schools  or  colleges  send  letters  in April  inviting
the  applicants  they  wish  to  include  in  their
Preferred  Admission  group.  Ross  Business  School
will  manage  their  sophomore  level  preferred
admission  program  on a rolling basis  beginning  in
November  2005.  To  learn  more  about  Preferred
Admission,  refer  to  the  LSA  leaflet  on  the  LSA
web  page:  http://www.LSA.umich.edu/saa/

D.

DUAL  ADMISSION

1.

GENERAL  POLICY

However,  after

Students  may  select  one  school  or college  at the
the
time  of  application.
completion  of one  academic  year,  it is possible
to  enroll  in  a  second  academic  career  and
complete  degree  requirements  for  both  school
and  college  as  an  internal  process.  This  is an
internal  process  between  the  units  involved.

EXCEPTION

School  of Music  and  LSA or  Residential  College
(RC),  (or  Engineering).
a.
Students  may  apply  to both  the  School  of
Music  and  LSA  or  RC, (or Engineering)  for the
same  semester.  Their  credentials  will  be
evaluated  on  the  qualifications  specific  to each
academic  unit.  By permitting  this  dual  degree
opportunity.  the  School  of Music  is in  a  better
position  to  recruit  top  musicians  who  are  also
solid  academic  students.

b.
Territory  counselors  will  review  the
applications  for  LSA  or  RC  (or  Engineering).
Review  of the  School  of Music  segment  of the
dual  application  is  the  responsibility  of  the
counselors  assigned  to  the  School  of  Music.
Communication  to the students  regarding  LSA
or  RC  (or  Engineering)  will  be  independent  of
communication  regarding  music.

c.
The  applications  of students  who  commit
to be dually enrolled  in two  units  at the  time  of
orientation  will  be  re-coded  to  reflect  dual
enrollment  status.

Let  me  paint  the  current  picture  for  you  and  explain
why  I am  so  worried,  and  in  fact  angry,  about  the

284

current  climate  we  are  operating  in  as  we  work  to
produce  graduates  prepared  for  a  diverse  world.

We  know  that  affirmative  action  works,  and  the
Supreme  Court  provided  us  with  moderate  guidelines
that  we  are  following.  We  know  that creating  a diverse
student  body  makes  for  a  stronger  educational
experience.  We  have  an  impressive  and  growing  body
of  social  science  research  that  demonstrates  the
positive  educational  outcomes  linked  with  diverse  class
environments.

Students  learn  better  in a diverse  class.  They are  more
analytical,  and  more  engaged.  These  students  are  more
open  to  perspectives  that  differ  from  their  own  and
they  are  better  prepared  to  become  active  plavers  in
our  society.

Most  people  recognize  the  value  of having  students  of
different  races  and  ethnic  origins  live  and  learn
together.  I wish  we  lived  in  a  society  free  of  social
inequities.
fully  integrated  neighborhoods,
churches  and  schools.  But  we  all know  there  is so  much
more  to  do  before  we  can  say  we  have  succeeded.

with

just  one

of  the  many
Affirmative  action  is  one
different  tools  we  can  use  to  help  make a difference  in
society.  We  defended  its  use  all  the  way  to  the  high
court  because  we  believe  it  helps  us  achieve  a  rich
student  body  that  mirrors  society,  and  a  majority  of
justices  agreed  with  us.  What  disturbs  me  is that  we  -
and  by we,  | mean  higher  education  -  we  are  acting  as
if we  did  not  prevail  before  the  highest  court  in  the
land!

285

We  are  on  fair  legal  ground  yet we  are  running  scared.
and  that  is  a  senseless,  dangerous  mode  of behavior
that  threatens  the  progress  we  have  made  on  our
campuses.  Colleges  and universities  across  the country
are  succumbing  to  a  chilling  effect  that  is paralyzing
scholarships,  admissions  and  programs  for  minority
students,  for  fear  of being  challenged  in court.

We  must  pay  attention  to race.  We must  pay  attention
to  ethnicity.  We  must  pay  attention  to  socioeconomic
class.  If we  look  away,  the  future  is  bleak,  and  one
need  only  visit  the  state  of  California  to  see  what
happens  when  we  go  down a road  that  ignores  these
factors.

A  few  weeks  ago,  our  campus  hosted  Dr.  Richard
Atkinson,
the  president  emeritus  of  the  great
University  of California  system.  Dick  was  president  in
1995  when  the  UC  Board  of  Regents  voted  to  ban
affirmative  action  in  admissions,  an  action  that  was
followed  by a  statewide  referendum  that  applied  that
same  ban  to  all  public  bodies  in  California.  Out  went
any  consideration  of  race,  gender,  ethnicity  and  the
like  in public  employment.

In banning  affirmative  action,  the UC  Board  of Regents
also  directed  UC  administrators  to  achieve  a  diverse
student  body.  Dick  will  tell you  that  affirmative  action
had  been  a  critical
tool  to  UC,  and  without  it
administrators  found  themselves  challenged  to  admit
underrepresented  minority  students,  particularly  at
UC-Berkeley,.  their  most  selective  campus.

Many,  many  steps  were  taken  to  recast  the  methods
UC  used  to  admit  minority  students.  Faculty  and
administrators  strengthened  their  outreach  to  high

286

schools.  They  put more  emphasis  on  achievement  tests
rather  than  aptitude  tests  such  as  the  SAT.  They
intensified  reviews  of  admissions  applications,  and
asked  students  to share  more  about  the obstacles  they
faced  in preparing  for college.  UC  opened  the  doors  to
the  top-performing  four  percent  of  students  from  all
California  high  schools,  and  took  in  more  transfer
students  from  California  community  colleges.

It  has  been  a  massive,  lengthy  undertaking  by
thoughtful  leaders  like  Dick  Atkinson,  and  you  know
what?  (Pause)

It hasn't  worked.  It simply  hasn’t  worked.  The  state  of
California
a  state  that  is  the  most  diverse  in  this
country  and which  represents  the fifth-largest  economy
is  educating  fewer  and  fewer
in  the  world  -
underrepresented  minority  students  at a time  when  its
citizenry  is growing  more  and  more  diverse.

Here  are  the  numbers:  In  1995,  before  the  ban  on
affirmative  action,  underrepresented  minorities  made
up  38  percent  of California  high  school  graduates  and
21  percent  of UC’s  entering  freshman  class.  Last  fall,
underrepresented  minority  students  represented  45
percent  of high school  graduates,  but  had  fallen  to just
19 percent  of incoming  freshmen.  And the statistics  are
even  more  grim  at  the  most  selective  campuses  of
Berkeley  and  UCLA.

The  gap  is widening.  not  closing,  and  it is particularly
troubling  when  you  consider  that  African  Americans
and  Latinos  make  up  more  and  more  of the  public  high
school  graduates  in California.

As  a  matter  of  comparison,  last  year's  combined
freshman  class  at  UCLA  and  UC-Berkeley  was  2.9
percent  African  American,  where  at Michigan  we  saw
5.7  percent  black  students  in our  incoming  class.  That
was  a  drop  from  the  previous  year’s  7.3  percent
a
decline  I attribute  to the lawsuits  and  one  we  expect  to
fully  rebound  from  this  fall.  Just  this  week  we
announced  that  the number  of applications  we  received
increased  from  every  minority  group,  and  our  paid
enrollment  deposits  are  up 20.1  percent  among  African
American  students  and  15.3  percent  among  Hispanic
students.

Our increase  comes  because  we  redoubled  our  outreach
efforts  this  year,  and  we  asked  our  entire  University
family  to  help  us  extend  a  warm  welcome  to  minority
families  in  the  face  of any  anxiety  they  were  feeling
because  of the  lawsuits.  We  invited  faculty,  students
and  alumni  to join us  at  recruiting  events.  They  came
by the dozens,  and  attendance  went  through  the roof.  I
visited  churches  and  community  centers
across
Michigan.  And  we  launched  a  Spanish  version  of the
University’s  website.

I'm  digressing  a  bit  from  California.  Here  is one  more
sobering  figure  that  Dick  Atkinson  provided.  Of that
incoming  freshman  class  at  UC-Berkeley  and  UCLA-
schools  that,  like  Michigan,  are  highly  selective  --  a
combined  total  of  83  African-American  men  were
enrolled.  Eighty-three  black  men  starting  college  at two
of our  country’s  leading  universities.  And  half of them
were  there  because  they were  on  athletic  scholarships.

That  number  alone  should  make  every  leader  in  this
country  stand  up  and  demand  change.  Because  if our
public  universities  -  particularly  selective  schools  like

and  talented

(pause)

Just  as  we  looked  to  our  friends  throughout  higher
education  for  support  before  the  Supreme  Court,  I am
once  again  asking  for your  leadership  in speaking  out
about  the  need  for  greater  diversity  on  our  college
campuses.

If we  look  at  the  University  of  Michigan  and  at  the
California  system.  we  know  that  there  is  one  major
difference:  we  both  have  the  will,  but  Michigan  also
has  the  tool  of affirmative  action.  We  have  the  ability
to  consider  a  student’s  whole  experience,  his  or  her
background,  race  and  ethnicity,  as  individual  factors
among  many  others.  We  know  it is critically  important
to  do the  kind  of outreach  we  did  this  year,  to create  a
welcoming  climate,  and  to  lower  perceived  barriers.
But  we  also  know  that  those  efforts  are  not  enough  on
their  own.  We  need  affirmative  action  in our  toolbox.

289

1  learned  an  important  lesson  on  the  steps  of  the
Supreme  Court:  The  power  of our  voice  matters.  Our
persistence  and  our  unwillingness  to  accept  anything
less  than  broad  diversity  on  our  campuses  send  a
strong  message  in this country.  Since  our  court  victory,
higher  education  has  sometimes  tread  too  cautiously
when  what  we  must  do  is lead  decisively,  and  I want
you  to  stand  alongside  us.

There  are  three  ways  you  can  join  us.

Our  greatest  challenge  is a  statewide  ballot  initiative
in  2006  to  end  affirmative  action  in  Michigan.  It  is
grossly  misnamed  the Michigan  Civil  Rights  Initiative
and I believe  the  political  debate  surrounding  it  will
divide  our  communities,  when  our  efforts  should  be
focused  on  constructive  ways  to  build  broad  diversity
within  the  moderate  guidelines  outlined  by  the
Supreme  Court.

I encourage  you  to  speak  out  against  this  initiative.
Why  should  a  college  president  from  Maryland  or
Louisiana  or  Wisconsin  stand  on  the  soapbox  about  a
ballot  question  in Michigan?  Because  I guarantee  you,
regardless  of whether  this  flawed  initiative  passes  or
fails  here.  its organizers  will  move  on  to another  state,
perhaps  your  state,  and  we  cannot  allow  the  scenario
unfolding  in California  to  be  repeated  elsewhere.

I feel  strongly  about  working  hard  to educate  Michigan
citizens  about  the  serious  implications  of such  a ballot
decision.  | want  people  to hear  Dick  Atkinson  when  he
says:  “Any  state  tempted  to  emulate  the  example  of
California  should  think  long and  hard  about  it.”

290

A  second  way  you  can  join  us  is  to  provide  greater
access  for low-income  students.  In particular,  we  must
find  ways  to  support  our  neediest  students  if we  hope
to  achieve  true  economic  diversity  on  our  campuses,
We  cannot  afford  to  leave  these  talented  young  people
behind,  and  data  show  us  that  qualified  students  from
low-income  families  are  sometimes  turned  away
because  the  barrier  is just tuo  steep.

At  Michigan,  we  have  always  guaranteed  our  in-state
students  that  if they  qualify  to  attend  our  university,
we  will  not  turn  them  away  because  of an  inability  to
pay.  We are  deepening  that  commitment  by increasing
our  financial  aid  to  them  and  reducing  the  amount  of
debt  they  take  on.

We  call this  program  M-PACT,  and  we  are  launching  it
with  $9 million  from  private  gifts.  In our  first  year.  we
will  help  2,900  students  and  their  families.  We  then
want  to  grow  the  program  with  a  major  fundraising
initiative  of at  least  $60  million  to  endow  M-PACT  for
both  in-state  and  out-of-state  students.

Lastly,  vou  can  help  by supporting  our  new  initiative,
the  Center  for  Institutional  Diversity.

With  the  national  spotlight  from  the  Supreme  Court
cases  and  the  University’s  long track  record  of diversity
initiatives,  many  people  around  the  country  are
looking  to  Michigan  and  saying,  “What  now?  What

ov”

next.

This  Center  is one  answer  to  that  question.  It  is  the
work  of many,  many  people,  with  Lester  Monts  as  its
driving  force.  | want  to thank  him  for his leadership  in
helping  this  university  bring  our  aspirations  to  life.

291

We will  launch  the  Center  for Institutional  Diversity  in
the  fall  of 2006.  With  financial  support  from  the  Ford
Foundation  and  input  from  intellectual  leaders  from
across  the  country,  we  are  framing  this  Center  to
develop  methods  and  best  practices  for  creating,
exploring,  and  embracing  diversity.

We  will  alwavs  need  quality  research,  and  our  Center
will  encourage  and  support  that.  Yet  we  must  move
bevond  research  and  find  new,  innovative  ways  to
address  the  disparities  we  see  in health  care,  upward
mobility,  K-12  education.  and  higher  education.

(pause)

In  preparing  to  argue  before  the  Supreme  Court,  the
University  of Michigan  received  tremendous  support
from  corporations,  the  military,  and  colleges  and
universities  across  this  country.  We  cannot  do  this
work  alone.  We  looked  to  you  in the  past  to  speak  out
for  diversity,  and  you  responded  admirably.

In ruling  in the  University’s  favor,  the  Supreme  Court
said,  “The  path  to  leadership  must  be  visibly  open  to
talented  and  qualified  individuals  of  every  race  and
ethnicity.”

Please  remain  by our  side,  remain  vigilant  and  vocal.
and  together  we  will  continue  to  forge  that  path  for
generations  to  come.

Thank  you.

All  of the  evaluation  ratings  are  to  be  used  only  as

regarding  the  decision  whether  to  admit  a  particular
applicant.  The  evaluation  ratings  are  as  follows:
Outstanding;  Excellent;  Good; Average/Fair;  and  Below
Average/Poor.  Also,  reviewers  may  assign  a  plus  or
minus  to  an  evaluation  rating.  Where  the  applicant's

GENERAL  DESCRIPTION
RATINGS  AND  RECOMMENDED  DECISIONS:

OF  EVALUATION

Description  Of Evaluation  Ratings

Reviewers  are  asked  to  exercise  their  professional
judgment  and  experience  and  rate  each  applicant  with
respect  to  Secondary  School  Academic  Performance,
Educational  Environment,  and  Counselor  and  Teacher
Recommendation,  and  to then  provide  an  overall  rating
for  the  application,  based  on  the  following  scale:

Outstanding
All  of  the  applicant's  materials  exemplify  superior
and/or  exceptional  characteristics  that  contribute  the
specific  evaluation  categories.

Excellent
The  applicant’s  materials  illustrate  extremely  strong,
but  not  exceptional,  characteristics.  The  reviewer;  may
have  a  reservation,  but  there  are  enough  redeeming
features  to  compensate  for,  or  outweigh,  reservation.

Good
The  applicant's  materials  demonstrate  competitive
average  characteristics  in  most  of  the  criteria,  they
may  be  particularly  strong  in one  or  more  areas.  The
reviewer  may  have  reservations  about  the  applicant’s
academic  competitiveness.

295

Average/Fair
While  the applicant’s  materials  are  competitive  in each
of the  criteria,  the  reviewer  has  substantial  concerns
about  the  overall  strength  of the  application  and  may
have  reservations  about  the  applicants’  academic
competitiveness.  The  applicant  does  not  stand  out.

Below  Average/Poor
In  the  applicant's  materials,  the  reviewer  detects
serious  deficiencies  in most  of the  evaluation  criteria  in
comparison  to other  applicants.  In addition,  several  of
the evaluation  criteria  may  not  be met  or  may  not  have
been  addressed  in the  applicant's  materials.

Recommended  Decisions

After  conducting  a  comprehensive,  holistic  and
individualized  review  of  an  application,  reviewers
make  an  admissions  decision  recommendation  based
on  the  composite  evaluation  rating  and  comments.
Reviewer  may  assign  a  plus  or  minus  to  any  of  the
following  decision  recommendations.

I.  ACADEMIC  ACHIEVEMENT,  QUALITY,  AND
POTENTIAL

Secondary  Schoo!  Academic  Performance

This  area  will  combine  recalculated  academic  grade
point average,  strength  and  quality  of curriculum,  test
scores,  and  class  rank  (if provided)  into one  categorv  of
academic  achievement.  After  reviewing  all application
materials,  the  reviewers  can  give  the  best  assessment
of the  applicant's  overall  academic  accomplishments.
Before  rating  the  applicant's  academic  achievement,
reviewers  should  consider  many  factors.  Because  it is
impossible  to list all of the  sources  a reviewer  may  find
important  in  making  the  academic  assessment.
reviewers  will  not  be  limited  to  those  cited  in  these
guidelines.

Transcript

What  kind  of grades  has  the  applicant  received  over
the  high  school  years?  Is there
an  upward  or
downward  one?  What  kind  of  program  does  the
applicant  plan  to  take  in  the  senior  year  -
Is  it
challenging  or  weak?  If  available.  where  does  the
applicant  rank?

atrend

and  quality  of the  curriculum.  Given  the wide  disparity
in  high  school  course  selection  and  offerings,  it  is

Reviewers  will  also  have  the  flexibility  to  give  an
outstanding  rating  to  applicants  who  took  college-level
courses  in academic  subjects  and  received  excellent  to
outstanding  grades.  Reviewers  may  exercise  their
judgment  when  giving  below  average,  average,  good,

Test  Scores

SAT/ACT/SAT  Subject  Tests/AP/IB/A  Levels

consider  many  factors

We  will  accept  either  the  SAT  or  the  ACT,  but  we
require  at  least  one  test  score  that  includes  a  writing
assessment.  Reviewers  will  consider  the  highest
composite  score,  no  matter  when  the  test  was  taken
(other  than  Engineering.  which  will  add  additional
consideration  to  the  highest  math  sub  score).  Reviewer
should
when  assessing
standardized  test  scores.  Are  there  subscores  that  are
significantly  higher  or  lower?  How  strong  are  the  SAT
Subject Tests/AP/IB/A  level  scores,  if provided?  Do they
show  special  proficiencies  in  specific  subject  areas?
How  do  the  scores  fit  the  score  ranges  on  the  U of M
freshman  profile?  Do  the  SAT/ACT  scores  correlate
with  the  applicant's  GPA?  Is one  significantly  higher
than  the other?  Does  the  student  demonstrate  English
proficiency  through  the  TOEFL/MELAB/IELTS?

299

Educational  Environment

High  School/College  Profiles

What  can  the  reviewer  learn  from  the  high  school
profile  that  might  assist  them?  How  might  the  high
school  profile  help determine  which  applicant  might be
the best  fit for the  college  to  which  he/she  is applying?
Does  the  high  school  profile  provide  information  that
might  better  help  assess  the  applicant's  educational
opportunities  and  accomplishments?  Does  the  school
provide  a  grade  distribution?  By  considering  the
composite  strength  of the  high school's  grading  system.
course  offerings,  and  the  percentage  of  applicants
attending  post-secondary  schools,  the  admissions
committee  can  better  assess  the  strength  of the  school’s
academic  environment.

Reviewers  should  also  evaluate  the  quality  of  the
school  GPA  by  considering  whether  applicants  from
that  high  school  generally  achieve  a  B+  average  or
higher  once  enrolled  at  the  University  of Michigan.

There  is  no  single  indicator  of  an  academically
disadvantaged  or  economically  disadvantaged  region.
What  is  the  socioeconomic  status  of  the  geographic
area  from  which  the  applicant  is applving,  e.g.  rural  or
urban,  high  cost-of-living,  depressed  local  economy?
Other  factors  to consider  are  whether  the high school  is
in  the  state  of  Michigan,  whether  the  school  is
underrepresented  at  the  University  of Michigan  Ann
Arbor  campus,  and  whether  the  school  is academically
disadvantaged.
consider
whether  a  school  is part  of a  cluster  of schools  that  are
underrepresented  in the  undergraduate  class,  because
students  from  such  schools  may  also  have  experiences

Evaluative  Measures

In  determining  the  applicant’s  academic  potential,
reviewers  should  use  the  high  school  counselor  and
teacher  recommendations  to  note  academic  awards  or
recognition  received.  evidence  of Intellectual  Curiosity,
and  in-depth  commitment  to particular  academic  areas
of  interest.  In  addition  to  the  recommendations,
reviewers  should  also  examine  the  applicant's  short-
answer  questions  and  essay  to  assess  the  applicant’s
written  communication  skills  such  as  writing  quality
content  style and originality/risk  taking.  as  well  as  any
other  evidence  of  academic  potential.  Discretionary
consideration  may  be  awarded  for  an  outstanding
essay.

Alumni  Relationships

The  University  of Michigan  values  the  relationship  it
has  with  current  and  former  students.  These  students
are  part  of  the  Michigan  community;  they  provide
to  the  larger  University
services  and  support
community.  As  such,  reviewers  should  take  into

304

applicants

consideration
direct
relationship,  or  stepfamily  relationship,  with  someone
who  has  attended  the  University  of  Michigan-Ann
Arbor  as  a  degree-seeking  student.

have

who

a_e

Strongest  consideration  should  be  given  to  applicants
whose  parents  or  stepparents  attended  the  University
of  Michigan-Ann  Arbor,  followed  by  applicants  who
have  a  grandparent,  sibling.  or  spouse,  who  has
attended  or  is attending  the  University.

Consideration  is  not  cumulative.  In  other  words,
additional  consideration  should  not  be  given  to
applicants  who  have  multiple  generations  of  family
who  have  attended  Michigan.

To  recognize  the  continuing  service  and  support
provided  to the  University,  consideration  will  be given
for  certain  alumni  relationships:

Legacy
The  applicant,  whose  parent  or  stepparent
attended  UM-Ann  Arbor  as  a  degree-seeking  student
should  be  awarded  discretionary  consideration.
Or
Other  Alumni  Relationships  -  The  applicant,  whose
grandparents,  siblings,  or  spouse  attended  UM-Ann
Arbor  as  degree-seeking  students,  should  be  awarded
discretionary  consideration.  Consideration  cannot  be
awarded  for  both categories.

Scholarship  Athletes

In anticipation  of their  contributions  to  the  University
and  in  recognition  of  the  traditions  and  national
prominence  of  Michigan  Intercollegiate  athletics,
applicants  being officially  recruited  and  considered  for

305

athletic  scholarships  will  be  carefully  reviewed,  in
accordance  with  the  Universities  academic  standards
and  NCAA  guidelines  and  regulations.

QUESTION  PRESENTED

Whether  a  state  violates  the  Equal  Protection
Clause  by amending  its  constitution  to  prohibit  race-
and  sex-based  discrimination  or  preferential  treatment
in public-university  admissions  decisions.

i
