STATEMENT

Respondent  Eric  Russell  concurs  in  Peti-
tioner’s  statement  of the  case.  He  provides  this  addi-
tional  statement  setting  forth  his  involvement  and
interest  as  a  defendant-intervenor  in this  litigation.

Eric  Russell  moved  to  intervene  in this  case  on
December  18,  2006,  five  days  before  Proposal  2 was
to  take  effect,  in  order  to  vindicate  his  right  to  equal
protection  under  this  constitutional  amendment.’
At  that  time,  Russell  had  been  granted  admission  to
Wayne  State  University  Law  School  and  was  an  ap-
plicant  to  the  University  of Michigan  School  of Law.
During  the  course  of this  litigation,  Russell  attended
and  graduated  from  Wayne  State  University  Law
School.  Beginning  in  his  last  semester  of law  school
and  continuing  to  the  present  time,  Russell  has  been
continuously  employed  as  an  adjunct  faculty  member
at  Oakland  Community  College,  a  lecturer  at  Oak-
In  addition,  Russell  re-
land  University,  or  both.
turned  to  Wayne  State  University  as  a  student  in
2011,  where  he  is  currently  pursuing  a  Master  of
The  University  of
Science  degree  in  accounting.
Michigan,  Wayne  State  University,  Oakland  Univer-
sity,  and  Oakland  Community  College  are  all  public
institutions  subject  to  Proposal  2.  Accordingly,  Rus-
sell  has  had  a  direct  and  substantial  interest  in  the
validity  of Proposal  2 throughout  this  litigation.

In  addition,  Russell  has  played  a  leading  role
in  this  litigation  from  the  outset.  He  alone  sought
and  received  from  the  Sixth  Circuit  an  emergency
stay  of the  district  court’s  preliminary  injunction  de-
laying  implementation  of  Proposal  2—an  injunction

1 Intervention  was  granted  on  December  27.

2

See  Pet.  App.  224a-254a.

that  had  been  entered  with  the  consent  of all  of the
other  parties  to  the  litigation,  including  Michigan’s
Attorney  General.
He
alone  took  factual  discovery  and  presented  expert
opinion  evidence  rebutting  the  factual  evidence  and
expert  opinions  offered  by  the  plaintiffs.  He  alone
advanced  numerous  arguments  and  points  relied  on
by the  district  court  in  upholding  the  constitutional-
ity  of  Proposal  2.  And  he  alone  argued  that  even  if
Proposal  2  were  subject  to  strict  scrutiny,  it  would
meet  that  standard.  In  addition,  Russell  submitted
briefs  supporting  Proposal  2 to  the  Sixth  Circuit  both
at  the  panel  and  en  banc  stages,  and  his  counsel  pre-
sented  oral  argument  supporting  Proposal  2  before
the  en  banc  court.’

2  Because  he was  a  party  in the  court  below,  Russell  be-
lieves  that  he  is entitled  to  file  a brief  as  a  respondent  support-
ing  petitioner  in  this  Court.
Furthermore,  this  Court  has
treated  Russell  as  a  party,  directing  that  he  respond  (or  waive
his  right  to  respond)  to  the  petition  for  a  writ  of certiorari  and
serving  him  with  notices  of  its  orders  in  this  case.  Neverthe-
less,  the  district  court  did  ultimately  dismiss  Russell  from  this
case  on  the  theory  that  he  no  longer  satisfied  the  requirements
for  intervention,  and  the  Sixth  Circuit  affirmed  that  dismissal.
See  Pet.  App.  48a-50a.  While  Russell  believes  his  dismissal  was
erroneous,  he did  not  seek  this  Court’s  review  of that  issue.

In  any  event,  Russell  notes  that  this  brief  satisfies  the
filing  deadline  and  word  count  limitations  that  would  apply  to
an  amicus  brief.  And  he  certifies  that  letters  from  all  parties
(including  Russell)  consenting  to  amicus  briefs  are  on  file  with
this  Court,  that  no  counsel  for  any  party  other  than  Russell  au-
thored  this  brief  in  whole  or  in  part,  and  that  no  person  or  en-
tity  other  than  Russell,  his  counsel,  and  their  firms  made  a
monetary  contribution  to  the  preparation  or  submission  of this
brief.

3

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

Under  the  political  process  doctrine  set  forth
in  this  Court’s  decisions  in  Hunter  v.  Erickson,  393
U.S.  385  (1969),  and  Washington  v.  Seattle  School
District  No.  1, 458  U.S.  457  (1982),  States  are  prohib-
ited  from  reallocating  decisionmaking  authority  in  a
manner  that  places  a  comparative  structural  burden
on  the  achievement  of minority  interests.  This  doc-
trine  should  be  overruled.

l.

The  political  process  doctrine  invites
absurd  results,  as  well  illustrated  by the  decision  be-
low,  which  held  that  Proposal  2,  a  state  constitu-
tional  provision  prohibiting  discrimination  or  prefer-
ential  treatment  based  on  race,  violates  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
Not  only  does  the  decision  below  suggest  that  any
governmental  policy  relating  to  the  treatment  of ra-
cial  minorities  is  off-limits  to  higher  levels  of  gov-
ernment,  it  would  appear  to  protect  even  patently
unconstitutional  policies.  While  it may  be  possible  to
read  this  Court’s  political  process  decisions  more
narrowly  than  did  the  Sixth  Circuit,  the  political
process  doctrine  plainly  lends  itself  to  such  anoma-
lous  results.

2.

The  political  process  doctrine  is  incon-
sistent  with  fundamental  equal  protection  principles.

a.)  | The  political  process  doctrine  ap-
plies  strict  scrutiny  to  facially  neutral  laws  even  in
the  absence  of any  proof  of discriminatory  intent.  It
thus  conflicts  with  “the  basic  equal  protection  princi-
ple  that  the  invidious  quality  of  a  [facially  neutral]
law  claimed  to  be  racially  discriminatory  must  ulti-
mately  be  traced  to  a  racially  discriminatory  pur-

4

pose.”
(1976).

Washington  v.  Davis,  426  U.S.  229,  240

b.)  | The  political  process  doctrine  fo-
cuses  on  protecting  the  interests  and  goals  of particu-
lar  groups.  It thus  conflicts  with  “the  basic  principle
that  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the
Constitution  protect  persons,  not  groups,”  Adarand
Constructors,  Inc.  v.  Pena,  515  U.S.  200,  227  (1995),
as  well  as  “the  cardinal  rule  that  our  Constitution
protects  each  citizen  as  an  individual,  not  as  a  mem-
ber  of a  group,”  Metro  Broad.,  Inc.  v.  FCC,  497  U.S.
547,  636  (1990)  (Kennedy,  J.,  dissenting),  overruled
by Adarand  Constructors,  515  U.S.  at  227.

c.)

The  political  process  doctrine  as-
cribes  to  the  groups  it  seeks  to  protect  collective  in-
terests  and  goals  that  it presumes  individuals  share
simply  by  virtue  of  their  race.
It  thus  reflects  “the
demeaning  notion  that  members  of the  defined  racial
groups  ascribe  to  certain  ‘minority  views’  that  must
be  different  from  those  of other  citizens,  the  precise
use  of  race  as  a  proxy  the  Constitution  prohibits.”
Miller  v.  Johnson,  515  U.S.  900,  914  (1995)  (citation
and  internal  quotation  marks  omitted).

d.)

The  political  process  doctrine  pro-
tects  only  minority  groups  and  assumes  that  the  ma-
jority  needs  no  protection  against  discrimination.
But  “[t]he  guarantee  of equal  protection  cannot  mean
one  thing  when  applied  to  one  individual  and  some-
thing  else  when  applied  to  a  person  of another  color.
If both  are  not  accorded  the  same  protection,  it is not
equal.”  Regents  of Univ.  of Cal.  v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.
265.  289-90  (1978)  (opinion  of Powell,  J.);  accord  City
of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.,  488  U.S.  469,  494
(1989).

5

e.)

process

doctrine
The  political
rests  on  “the  conception  of a  Nation  divided  into  ra-
cial  blocs,”  Metro  Broad.,  497  U.S.  at  603  (O’Connor,
J.,  dissenting),  in  which  the  “majority”  and  the  “mi-
nority”  are  engaged  in a  zero-sum  competition  to  pro-
tect  their  conflicting  “interests”  and  achieve  their  di-
Such  a  conception  of  the  Nation:
vergent  “goals.”
“may  balkanize  us  into  competing  racial  factions;  it
threatens  to  carry  us  further  from  the  goal  of  a  po-
litical  system  in  which  race  no  longer  matters—a
goal  that  the  Fourteenth  and  Fifteenth  Amendments
embody,  and  to  which  the  Nation  continues  to  as-
pire.”  Miller,  515  U.S.  at  912.

3.

The  restrictions  imposed  by the  political
process  doctrine  on  reallocations  of  state  decision-
making  authority  cannot  be  reconciled  with  the
“wide  leeway”  afforded  States  “when  experimenting
with  the  appropriate  allocation  of  state  legislative
Holt  Civic  Club  v.  City  of Tuscaloosa,  439
power.”
U.S.  60,  71  (1978).
As  this  Court  has  explained,
“(t]he  principle  is  well  settled  that  local  governmen-
tal  units  are  created  as  convenient  agencies  for  exer-
cising  such  of the  governmental  powers  of the  State
as  may  be  entrusted  to  them  in  its  absolute  discre-
tion.  Whether  and  how  to  use  that  discretion  is  a
question  central  to  state  self-government.”
City  of
Columbus  v.  Ours  Garage  &  Wrecker  Serv.,  Inc.,  536
U.S.  424,  437  (2002)  (citation  and  internal  quotation
marks  omitted).

4.

The  political  process  doctrine  is  malle-
able  and  unpredictable  in  practice,  as  demonstrated
by the  numerous  opinions  from  the  en  banc  proceed-
ings  in  this  case,  the  previous  opinion  from  the  Sixth
Circuit  vacating  a  preliminary  injunction  of Proposal
2, and  the  opinions  from  the  Ninth  Circuit  and  Cali-

6

fornia  Supreme  Court  rejecting  closely  analogous  po-
litical  process  claims.  See  Coalition  for Econ.  Equity
v.  Wilson,  122  F.3d  692  (9th  Cir.  1997);  Coral
Constr.,  Inc.  v.  City  & County  of San  Francisco,  235
P.3d  947  (Cal.  2010).  Indeed,  the  open-ended  formu-
lations  used  to  determine  whether  reallocations  of
decisionmaking  authority  have  a  “racial  focus”  and
are  thus  subject  to  the  political  process  doctrine  may
simply  hcense—or  even  require—judges  to  deter-
mine,  by  their  own  lights,  whether  policies  burdened
by  state  reallocations  of  decisionmaking  authority
are  or  are  not  “racially  beneficial.”  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.,
458  U.S.  at  474,  483.

5.

to  vindicate

Finally,  the  political  process  doctrine  is
unnecessary
the  Equa!  Protection
Clause,  as  underscored  by  the  fact  that  this  Court
has  expressly  relied  on  the  political  process  doctrine
to  invalidate  a  law  only  twice  in  the  forty-four  years
since  the  doctrine  was  invented.  In  one  of those  in-
stances,  moreover,  this  Court  likely  would  have
reached  the  same  result  applying  conventional  equal
protection  analysis.  To  be  sure,  this  Court’s  decision
in  Seattle  School  District  likely  would  have  come  out
differently  but  for  application  of the  political  process
doctrine.  But  that  decision  was  not  a  necessary—or
even
Fourteenth
the
Amendment,  and  it should  be overruled.

faithful—application

of

ARGUMENT

In  2006,  the  People  of Michigan  adopted  Pro-
posal  2,  an  initiative  that  amended  their  Constitu-
tion  to  provide  that  the  State  “shall  not  discriminate
against,  or  grant  preferential  treatment  to,  any  indi-
vidual  or  group  on  the  basis  of race,  sex,  color,  eth-

7

nicity,  or  national  origin  in  the  operation  of  public
employment,  public  education,  or  public  contracting.”
MICH.  CONST.  art.  I,  § 26(2).
Sitting  en  banc,  the
Sixth  Circuit  concluded,  paradoxically,  that  this
guarantee  of equal  treatment  violates  the  Fourteenth
Amendment's  mandate  that  “[m]o  State  shall...
deny  to  any  person  within  its  jurisdiction  the  equal
protection  of the  laws.”  U.S.  CONST.  amend.  XIV.  In
reaching  this  startling  conclusion,  the  Sixth  Circuit
relied  on  the  “political  process  doctrine”  (also  known
as  the  “political  structure  doctrine”)  set  forth  in  this
Court’s  decisions  in  Hunter  and  Seattle  School  Dis-
trict.  These  cases  held  that  the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment  prohibits  States  from  reallocating  “decision-
making  authority”  in  a  manner  that  places  a  “com-
.  on  the  political
parative  structural  burden  ..
achievement  of  minority  interests.”
Seattle  Sch.
Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  474  n.17.

However  well-intentioned  the  cases  establish-
ing the  political  process  doctrine  may  have  been,  that
doctrine  invites  absurd  results,  as  well  illustrated  by
Further,  that  doctrine  can  be
the  decision  below.
reconciled  neither  with  fundamental  equal  protection
principles  nor  with  the  constitutional  prerogative  of
the  States  to  allocate  their  decisionmaking  authority
as  they  see  fit.  Finally,  the  political  process  doctrine
has  proved  malleable  and  unprincipled  in  practice
and  is unnecessary  to  vindicate  the  Equal  Protection
Clause.
For  all  of these  reasons,  it  should  be  over-
ruled.  See  Pet.  App.  92a-99a  (Griffin,  J.,  dissenting);
Coral  Constr.,  235  P.3d  at  966-67  (Corrigan,  J.,  con-
curring).

8

I.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Invites
Absurd  Results.

In  the  case  below,  the  Sixth  Circuit  held  that
by  prohibiting  public  universities  in  Michigan  from
employing  admissions  policies
that  discriminate
against  or  grant  preferential  treatment  to  any  indi-
vidual  based  on  race,  the  People  of that  State  denied
Plaintiffs  the  equal  protection  of the  laws.  It did  so,
moreover,  “in  the  face  of  the  core  equal  protection
principle  of  nondiscrimination—a  principle  consis-
tent  with  the  choice  of the  People  of Michigan,”  “de-
spite  the  absence  of  any  precedent  suggesting  that
states  must  employ  racial]  preferences  in  university
admissions,”  and  even  though  such  preferences
themselves  are  “presumptively  invalid”  under  the
Equal  Protection  Clause.  Pet.  App.  56a  (Gibbons,  J.,
dissenting).

As  well  expressed  by Judge  O’Scannilain  in  re-
jecting  an  analogous  constitutional  claim,  “[i]f merely
stating  this  alleged  equal  protection  violation  does
not  suffice  to  refute  it, the  central  tenet  of the  Equal
Protection  Clause  teeters  on  the  brink  of  incoher-
ence.”
Coalition  for  Econ.  Equity,  122  F.3d  at  702;
see  also  id.  at  709  (“The  Fourteenth  Amendment,  lest
we  lose  sight  of the  forest  for  the  trees,  does  not  re-
quire  what  it barely  permits.”).  Indeed,  it  should  be
axiomatic  “that  a  State  does  not  deny  equal  treat-
Pet.  App.  82a  (Sutton,  J.,
ment  by  mandating  it.”
dissenting).

Invoking  the  political  process  doctrine,  the
Sixth  Circuit  nevertheless  held  that  Proposal  2’s  ban
on  racial  preferences  is  unconstitutional  because  it
“removed  the  authority  to  institute  any  such  policy
from  Michigan’s  universities  and  lodged  it  at  the
most  remote  level  of  Michigan’s  government,  the

9

state  constitution,”  and  thus  made  it  more  difficult
for  racial  minorities  (which  it  presumed  supported
such  policies)  to  obtain  racial  preferences  through
the  political  process.  Jd.  at  36a-37a.  But  the  propo-
sition  that  any  governmental  policy  relating  to  the
treatment  of  racial  minorities  is  off-limits  to  higher
levels  of government  is  completely  unsustainable  as
a  generally  applicable  rule  of law.  Under  this  view,
the  State  Legislature  is  subordinate  to  the  agency
and  the  municipality,  the  Governor  is subordinate  to
the  bureaucrat,  and  the  University  Board  of Trustees
is subordinate  to  the  admissions  committee.  And  the
People  themselves  are  subordinate  to  all  government
bodies  and  officials.  More  important,  racial  prefer-
ences  become  a  one-way  ratchet.
No  intermediate
government  body  or  official  may  ever  ban  them  in
the  first  place  because  that  would  make  it more  diffi-
cult  for  minorities  to  lobby  for  such  benefits  from
lower  level  government  entities.  And  once  some  gov-
ernmental  body  had  adopted  racial]  preferences,  the
decision  may  never  be  rescinded  by  a  higher  level  of
government.

Further,  precisely  the  same  reasoning  could  be
applied  to  a  state  court  decision  invalidating  racial
preferences  in  admissions  under  a  state  constitu-
tion’s  general  equal  protection  guarantee.
Cf.  Lou-
istana  Associated  Gen.  Contractors,  Inc.  v.  State,  669
So.2d  1185,  1196-98  (La.  1996)  (interpreting  state
equal  protection  guarantee  to  prohibit  all  race-based
distinctions).  For  as
Judge  Sutton  explained,  such  a
decision

would  have  precisely  the  same  effect  as
Proposal  2, establishing  that  the  Consti-
tution  bars  racial  preferences  and  plac-
ing  the  onus  on  proponents  of  racial

10

to

the

alter

Constitu-
preferences
tion.  ..
.  If Proposal  2 violates  the  politi-
cal-process  doctrine,  so  too  would  a  de-
cision  by  the  Michigan  Supreme  Court
that  comes  to  the  same  end  through...
interpretation  of  [Michigan’s  general]
equal-protection  guarantee.

Pet.  App.  89a.

Indeed,  given  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  emphasis  on
the  fact  that  Proposal  2 imposes  a  greater  hurdle  to
those  championing  racial  preferences  than  on  those
“advocating  consideration  of  nonracial  factors  in  ad-
missions  decisions,”  id.  at  37a,  the  decision  below
might  well  prohibit  a  State’s  courts  from  interpreting
that  State’s  equal  protection  guarantee  to  mirror  its
federal  analog  in  requiring  strict  scrutiny  for  prefer-
ences  based  on  race,  but  not  for  preferences  based  on
other  considerations.
After  all,  such  an  interpreta-
tion  would  “impose  special  burdens  on  some  policies
that  might  benefit  racial  minorities  in  the  admis-
sions  process  without  imposing  the  same  burdens  on
policies  that  benefit  groups  categorized  along  non-
suspect  lines.”  Jd.  at  90a  (Sutton,  J.,  dissenting).  It
goes  without  saying,  however,  that  “[i]t  would  be
paradoxical  to  conclude  that  by  adopting  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment,  the
voters  of the  State  thereby  had  violated  it.”  Craw-
ford  v.  Board  of Educ.,  458  U.S.  527,  535  (1982).4

3  Indeed,  were  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  of the  Four-
teenth  Amendment  not  the  source  of the  political  process  doc-
trine,  that  Amendment  might  itself  violate  the  political  process
doctrine,  for  as  Judge  O’Scannlain  has  observed,  “[t]he  Equal
Protection  Clause,  parked  at  our  most  ‘distant  and  remote’  level
(Continued  on  following  page)

11

unconstitutional

Despite  its  protestations  to  the  contrary,  see
Pet.  App.  44a  n.9,  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  rationale  would
require  invalidation  of Proposal  2 even  as  applied  to
admissions
patently
policies—a  rigid  racial  quota,  for  example—that  a
court  would  be  duty-bound  to  strike  down  under  the
Equal  Protection  Clause,  see,  e.g.,  Gratz  v.  Bollinger,
539  U.S.  244  (2003);  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265.  Indeed,
the  Sixth  Circuit  expressly  rejected  any  sort  of “out-
come-based  limitation  on  a process-based  right.”  Pet.
App.  41a.  Instead,  the  court  below  held  that

race-based

the  Hunter/Seattle  doctrine  works  to
prevent  the  placement  of special  proce-
dural  obstacles  on  minority  objectives,
whatever  those  objectives  may  be.  .
.
What  matters  is  whether  racial  minori-
ties  are  forced  to  surmount  procedural
hurdles  in reaching  their  objectives  over
which  other  groups  do  not  have  to  leap.
If  they  are,  the  disparate  procedural
treatment  violates  the  Equal  Protection
objective
Clause,
sought.

regardless

of  the

.

of government,  singles  out  racia]  preferences  for  severe  politi-
cal  burdens—it  prohibits  them  in  all  but  the  most  compelling
circumstances.”  Coalition  for Econ.  Equity,  122  F.3d  at  708;  see
also  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  483  (faulting  the  People  of
Washington  for  “lodging  decisionmaking  authority  over  [race-
conscious  busing]  at  a  new  and  remote  level]  of  government”);
Pet.  App.  36a  (faulting  the  People  of Michigan  for  banning  ra-
cial  discrimination  and  preferences  “at  the  highest  level”);  id.  at
83a  (Sutton,  J.,  dissenting)  (“There  is  nothing  unusual  about
placing  an  equal-protection  guarantee  in a  constitution.  That  is
where  individual-liberty  guarantees  often  go,  and  that  after  all
is where  the  national  framers  placed  the  federal  counterpart.”).

12

Id.  (emphases  added);  see  also,  e.g.,  id.  at  40a  (“the
language  of Hunter  and  Seattle  encompasses  any  leg-
islation  in  the  interest  of racial  minorities  ..
.  .”);  id.
at  2la  n.2  (“This  holds  true  for  enactments  that
make  it more  difficult  for  minorities  to  use  the  politi-
cal  process  to  obtain  any  sort  of legislation  that  is in
their  interest  ....”).  According  to  the  Sixth  Circuit,
then,  the  political  process  doctrine  would  protect
even  racial  preferences  that  are  themselves  unconsti-
tutional.  This  proposition  obviously  cannot  be  right.

While  it may  be  possible  to  read  Seattle  School
District  and  Hunter  more  narrowly  than  did  the
Sixth  Circuit  and  thus  to  avoid  some  of these  anoma-
lies,  there  can  be  no  question  that  those  cases,  and
the  political  process  doctrine  they  enunciate,  “lend
themselves  to  arguments  distinctly  at  odds  with  the
high  court’s  own  approach  to  racial  classifications  in
...  later  cases.”  Coral  Constr.,  235  P.3d  at  967  (Cor-
rigan,  J.,  concurring).  Indeed,  in  Seattle  School  Dis-
trict,  this  Court  itself  declined  to  consider  the  consti-
tutional  “propriety  of  race-conscious  student  assign-
ments  for  the  purpose  of achieving  integration,  even
absent  a  finding  of prior  de jure  segregation,”  Seattle
Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  472  n.15,  even  while  holding
that  the  political  process  doctrine  barred  the  People
of  Washington  from  amending  their  constitution  to
prohibit  such  assignments,  see  id.  at  462-63.  Signifi-
cantly,  this  Court  later  held  that  a  subsequent  itera-
tion  of  the  very  desegregation  plan  that  it  had  pro-
tected  under  the  political  process  doctrine  in  Seattle
School  District  itself  violated  the  Equal  Protection
Clause.  See  Parents  Involved  in  Cmty.  Sch.  v.  Seattle
Sch.  Dist.  No.  1,  551  U.S.  701  (2007);  see  also  id.  at
856  (Breyer,  J.,  dissenting)  (stating  that  Seattle
School  District  “involve[{d]  the  original  Seattle  Plan,

13

a  more  heavily  race-conscious  predecessor  of the  very
plan  now  before  us”).

The  Equal  Protection  Clause  is  assuredly  not
at  war  with  itself,  and  any  doctrine  that  suggests
that  the  States  may  not  prohibit  race-based  policies
that  themselves  violate  the  Fourteenth  Amendment
(either  outright  or  at  least  presumptively)  cannot  be
sound.  And,  as  demonstrated  next,  the  doctrinal  dif-
ficulties  posed  by the  political  process  doctrine  are  no
less  serious  than  the  practical  absurdities,  such  as
the  decision  below,  that  it is prone  to engender.

II.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Is Incon-
sistent  with  Fundamental  Equal  Protec-
tion  Principles.

Under  the  political  process  doctrine,  state  laws
are  subject  to  strict  scrutiny  if they  reallocate  deei-
sionmaking  authority  in  a  way  that  makes  it  "™  »re
difficult  for  certain  racial  and  religious  minorities
[than  for  other  members  of  the  community]  to
achieve  legislation  that  is  in  their  interest.”  Seattle
Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  470  (internal  quotation  marks
omitted;  alterations  in  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.);  see  also  id.
at  467  (doctrine  applies  to  “a  political  structure  that
treats  all  individuals  as  equals,  yet  more  subtly  dis-
torts  governmental  processes  in  such  a  way  as  to
place  special]  burdens  on  the  ability  of  minority
groups  to  achieve  beneficial  legislation”  (citation  and
internal  quotation  marks  omitted));  Hunter,  393  U.S.
at  393  (State  may  not  “disadvantage  any  particular
group  by making  it  more  difficult  to  enact  legislation
in  its  behalf’);  Pet.  App.  22a  (doctrine  applies  to  laws
that  “reallocat[e]  political  power  or  reorders  the  deci-
sionmaking  process  in  a  way  that  places  special  bur-

14

dens  on  a  minority  group’s  ability  to  achieve  its  goals
through  that  process”).  As  demonstrated  below,  this
doctrinal  framework  is  fundamentally  inconsistent
with  core  equal  protection  principles  repeatedly  reaf-
firmed  in this  Court’s  cases.‘

A.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Im-
poses  Liability  Absent  Discrimina-
tory  Treatment  or  Intent.

As  an  initial  matter,  the  political  process  doc-
trine  applies  strict  scrutiny  to  facially  neutral  laws
even  in  the  absence  of  any  proof  of  discriminatory
intent.
See,  e.g.,  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  485
(holding  that  “legislation  of  the  kind  challenged  in
.  falls  into  an  inherently  suspect  cate-
Hunter  ..
gory’);  Pet.  App.  23a  n.3  (similar);  id.  at  96a  (Griffin,
J.,  dissenting)  (“The  laws  at  issue  in  Hunter  and  Se-
attle  were  both  facially  neutral.”).

4  See  aiso  Pet.  App.  95a  (Griffin,  J., dissenting)  (“the  ‘po-
litical  structure’  doctrine  is  an  anomaly  incompatible  with  the
Equal  Protection  Clause”);  Coral  Constr.,  235  P.3d  at  966  (Cor-
rigan,  J., concurring)  (noting  “the  difficulty  of squaring  the  ‘po-
hitical  structure  doctrine’  with  modern  equal  protection  juris-
prudence”  and  expressing  “perplex[ity]  by  the  persistence  of
this  anachronistic  feature  of federal  constitutional  law”)  (inter-
nal  quotation  mark  omitted);  David  J.  Barron,  The  Promise  of
Cooley's  Cities:  Traces  of Local  Constitutionalism,  147  U.  PA.  L.
REV.  487,  561  (1999)  (describing  cases  establishing  the  political
process  doctrine  as  “jurisprudes*tial  enigmas  that  seem  to  lack
a
any  coherent  relationship  to  constitutional  doctrine  as
whole”);  Daniel  P.  Tokaji,  First  Amendment  Equal  Protection:
On  Discretion,  Inequality,  and  Participation,  101  MICH.  L. REv.
2409,  2476  (2003)  (“It  is  therefore  easy  to  view  these  cases  as
constitutional  oddballs,  difficult  or  impossible  to  explain  in light
of accepted  equal  protection  principles.”).

15

In  effect,  then,  under  the  political  process  doc-
trine  a  law  may  violate  the  Equal  Protection  Clause
based  solely  upon  a  presumed  disparate  effect  on  ra-
In  this  case,  for  example,  it  cannot
cial  minorities.
be  disputed  that  Proposal  2  is  facially  neutral—far
from  drawing  a  race-based  classification,  it prohibits
all  race-based  discrimination  or  preferences—and
the  Sixth  Circuit  did  not  disturb  the  District  Court’s
finding  that  Plaintiffs  had  failed  to  establish  a  dis-
criminatory  purpose.  See  id.  at  78a  (Gibbons,  J.,  dis-
senting).  The  political  process  doctrine  thus  conflicts
with  the  bedrock  “equal  protection  principle  that  the
invidious  quality  of  a  [facially  neutral]  law  claimed
to  be  racially  discriminatory  must  ultimately  be
traced  to  a  racially  discriminatory  purpose.”  Wash-
ington,  426  U.S.  at  240;  see  also  Personnel  Adm’r  v.
Feeney,  442  U.S.  256,  272  (1979)  (“even  if a  neutral
law  has  a  disproportionately  adverse  effect  upon  a
racial  minority,  it  is  unconstitutional  under  the
Equal  Protection  Clause  only  if that  impact  can  be
traced  to  a  discriminatory  purpose”).
In  short,  as
aptly  stated  by Judge  Griffin  in  his  dissent  from  the
decision  below,  the  political  process  doctrine  “re-
places  actual  evidence  of racial  motivation  with  a  ju-
dicial  presumption  and,  hence,  is  an  aberration  in-
consistent  with  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.”
Pet.
App.  96a.

B.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Pro-
tects  Groups  Rather  than  Individu-
als.

More  important  still,  the  political  process  doc-
trine—with  its  focus  on  the  “interest”  and  “goais”  of
“particular  groups,”  supra  at  13-14—“seems  to  per-
ceive  the  right  to  equal  protection  as  a  group  mght

16

rather  than  an  individual  mnght,”  Coalition  for Econ.
Equity,  122  F.3d  at  705  n.12.  It  thus  conflicts  with
“the  basic  principle  that  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth
Amendments  to  the  Constitution  protect  persons,  not
groups,  Adarand  Constructors,  515  U.S.  at  227,  as
well  as  “the  cardinal  rule  that  our  Constitution  pro-
tects  each  citizen  as  an  individual,  not  as  a  member
of a  group,”  Metro  Broad.,  497  U.S.  at  636  (Kennedy,
J.,  dissenting).
In  the  words  of  Justice  Powell  in
Bakke,  “it  is  the  individual  who  is entitled  to  judicial
protection  against  classifications  based  upon  his  ra-
cial  or  ethnic  background  because  such  distinctions
impinge  upon  personal  rights,  rather  than  the  indi-
vidual  only  because  of his  membership  in  a  particu-
lar  group...  .”  438  U.S.  at  299  (quoted  in Adarand
Constructors,  515  U.S.  at  224).

As  this  Court  has  explained,  the  “fundamental
principle  of  equal  protection  as  a  personal  right,”
Adarand  Constructors,  515  U.S.  at  235,  follows  di-
rectly  from  the  plain  text  of the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment:

.

The  Equal  Protection  Clause  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment  provides  that
“Injo  State  shall  .
.  deny  to  any  person
within  its  jurisdiction  the  equal  protec-
tion  of the  laws.”  (Emphasis  added.)  As
this  Court  has  noted  in  the  past,  the
“rights  created  by the  first  section  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment  are,  by  its
terms,  guaranteed  to  the  individual.
are  personal
The  rights  established
rights.”

J.A.  Croson  Co.,  488  U.S.  at  493  (quoting  Shelley  v.
Kraemer,  334  U.S.  1,  22  (1948)).  The  political  proc-
ess  doctrine’s  conception  of  equal  protection  as  a

17

group  right  simply  cannot  be  reconciled  with  “the
long  line  of cases  understanding  equal  protection  as
a  personal  right.”  Adarand  Constructors,  515  U.S.  at
230;  see  also  Coalition  for  Econ.  Equity,  122  F.3d  at
705  &  n.13  (noting  “the  tension  between  the  protec-
tion  that  the  ‘political  structure’  cases  afford  to  the
‘ability  of minority  groups  to  achieve  beneficial  legis-
lation’  and  the  ‘fundamental  principle  of  equal  pro-
tection  as  a  personal  right’  ”  (citation  omitted)).

C.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Pre-
sumes  that  Individuals’  Interests
and  Goals  Are  Determined  by Their
Race.

Not  only  does  the  political  process  doctrine
impermissibly  seek  to  protect  groups  rather  than  in-
dividuals,  it  ascribes  to  each  group  collective  “inter-
ests”  and  “goals”  that  it presumes  are  shared  by  in-
dividuals  simply  by virtue  of their  race.  Supra  at  13-
14.  The  doctrine  thus  “reinforces  the  perception  that
members  of  the  same  racial  group—regardless  of
their  age,  education,  economic  status,  or  the  commu-
nity  in  which  they  live—think  alike,  [and]  share  the
Shaw  v.  Reno,  509  U.S.
same  political  interests.”
630,  647  (1993).
But  this  Court,  of  course,  has
roundly  “rejected  such  perceptions  elsewhere  as  im-
Jd.;  see  also,  e.g.,
permissible  racial  stereotypes.”
Miller,  515  U.S.  at  912,  920  (rejecting  this  “offensive
and  demeaning  assumption”  as  “racial  stereotyping
at  odds  with  equal  protection  mandates”);  Metro
Broad.,  497  U.S.  at  615  (O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting)
(rejecting  “generalizations  impermissibly  equating
race  with  thoughts  and  behavior”);  id.  at  618  (reject-
ing  notion  “that  a  particular  and  distinct  viewpoint
inheres  in certain  racial  groups”).

18

For  as  this  Court  has  explained,  although  they

may  share  a common  ancestry,

it  does  not  follow  from  this  that  indi-
viduals  of the  same  race  share  a  single
political  inierest.  The  view  that  they  do
is “based  on  the  demeaning  notion  that
members  of  the  defined  racial  groups
ascribe  to  certain  ‘minority  views’  that
must  be  different  from  those  of  other
citizens,”  the  precise  use  of  race  as  a
proxy  the  Constitution  prohibits.

Miller,  515  U.S.  at  914  (quoting  Metro  Broad.,  497
U.S.  at  636  (Kennedy,  J., dissenting));  see  also  Rice  v.
Cayetano,  528  U.S.  495,  517  (2000)  (“it  demeans  the
dignity  and  worth  of a  person  to  be judged  by ances-
try  instead  of by  his  or  her  own  merit  and  essential
qualities”).

.

.

At  bottom,  then,  the  political  process  doctrine
rests  on  “stereotypes  that  treat  individuals  as  the
product  of their  race,  evaluating  their  thoughts  and
efforts—their  very  worth  as  citizens—according  to  a
criterion  barred  .
.  by history  and  the  Constitution.”
Miller,  515  U.S.  at  912.  It thus  conflicts  with  “one  of
our  most  cherished  constitutional  and  societal  prin-
ciples  .
.  that  an  individual’s  tastes,  beliefs,  and
abilities  should  be  assessed  on  their  own  merits
rather  than  by  categorizing  that  individual  as  a
member  of a  racial  group  presumed  to  think  and  be-
have  in  a  particular  way.”  Metro  Broad.,  497  U.S.  at
618  (O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting).  In  short,  both  by fo-
cusing  its  protection  on  groups,  rather  than  indi-
viduals,  and  by ascribing  to  individuals  goals  and  in-
terests  based  solely  on  their  race,  the  political  proc-
ess  doctrine  falls  afoul  of the  “simple  command”  “[a]t
the  heart  of  the  Constitution’s  guarantee  of  equal

19

protection  ...  that  the  Government  must  treat  citi-
zens  as  individuals,  not  as  simply  components  of  a
racial,  religious,  sexual  or  national  class.”
Méiller,
515  U.S.  at  911  (internal  quotation  marks  omitted).

D.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Does
Not  Apply  Equally  to All  Races.

Not  only  does  the  political  process  doctrine
protect  groups  rather  than  individuals  (who  are  pro-
tected  only  as  members  of  groups  whose  collective
“interest”  and  “goals”  they  are  presumed  to  share),  it
protects  only  “minority  groups.”  Supra  at  13-14  (em-
phasis  added).
Indeed  the  doctrine’s  focus  on  pro-
tecting  minorities  from  changes  in  the  political  proc-
ess  reflects  the  explicit  assumption  that  “[t]he  major-
ity  needs  no  protection  against  discrimination.”
Hunter,  393  U.S.  at  391.

Sounder,  more  recent  cases,  however,  have  re-
jected  “a reading  of the  guarantee  of equal  protection
under  which  the  level  of scrutiny  varies  according  to
the  ability  of  different  groups  to  defend  their  inter-
ests  in  the  representative  process.”  J.A.  Croson,  488
U.S.  at  495.  This  Court  has  thus  embraced  Justice
Powell’s  statement  that  “[t]he  guarantee  of  equal
protection  cannot  mean  one  thing  when  applied  to
one  individual  and  something  else  when  applied  to  a
person  of another  color.  If both  are  not  accorded  the
same  protection,  it is  not  equal.”  Bakke,  438  U.S.  at
289-90;  accord  J.A.  Croson,  488  U.S.  at  494.  Accord-
ingly,  this  Court  has  repeatedly  held  that  the  re-
quirements  of  equal  protection  “obtai[n]  with  equal
force  regardless  of  ‘the  race  of  those  burdened  or
Miller,  515  U.S.  at  904  (quoting  J.A.
benefitted.’  ”
Croson,  488  U.S.  at  494);  see  also,  e.g.,  Adarand  Con-

20

structors,  515  U.S.  at  223,  227  (emphasizing,  among
the  “general  propositions”  established  by this  Court’s
cases,  the  requirement  of “consistency  of  treatment
irrespective  of the  race  of the  burdened  or  benefited
group’).

In  short,  as  Justice  O’Connor  put  it in  dissent
in  Metro

decision

from  this  Court’s  short-lived
Broadcasting,

We  are  a  Nation  not  of black  and  white
alone,  but  one  teeming  with  divergent
communities  knitted  together  by  vari-
ous  traditions  and  carried  forth,  above
all,  by individuals.  Upon  that  basis,  we
are  governed  by  one  Cunstitution,  pro-
viding  a  single  guarantee  of  equal  pro-
tection,  one  that  extends  equally  to  all
citizens.

497  U.S.  at  610.  The  sharp  distinction  drawn  by the
political  process  doctrine  between  protected  “minor-
ity  groups”  and  the  ~majority—which  “needs  no  pro-
tection’—could  not  be  more  at  odds  with  this  under-
standing.

E.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Is at
Cross-Purposes  with  the  Ultimate
Goal  of the  Fourteenth  Amend-
ment.

Far  from  viewing  this  Nation  as  one  built  from
“divergent  communities  knitted  together  by  various
traditions  and  carried  forth,  above  all,  by  individu-
als,”  the  political  process  doctrine  rests  on  “the  con-
ception  of a  Nation  divided  into  racial  blocs,”  Metro
Broad.,  497  U.S.  at  603,  610  (O’Connor,  J.,  dissent-
ing),  in  which  the  “majority”  and  the  “minority”  are

21

engaged  in  a  zero-sum  competition  to  protect  their
conflicting  “interests”  and  achieve  their  divergent
“goals.”
Cf.  Pet.  App.  15a  (“In  effect,  the  political-
process  doctrine  hews  to  the  unremarkable  notion
that  when  two  competitors  are  running  a  race,  one
may  not  require  the  other  to  run  twice  as  far  or  to
scale  obstacles  not  present  in  the  first  runner’s
course.”);  id.  at  16a  (“Hunter  and  Seattle  .
.  set  the
benchmark  for  when  the  majority  has  not  only  won,
but  has  rigged  the  game  to  reproduce  its  success  in-
definitely.”).

.

This  Court  should  no  longer  countenance  this
tribal  conception  of  our  Nation,  which  les  at  the
heart  of the  political  process  doctrine,  for  such  a  con-
ception  can  “contribut[e]  to  an  escalation  of  racial
hostility  and  conflict,”  Metro  Broad.,  497  U.S.  at  603
(O’Connor,  J.,  dissenting);  it  “may  balkanize  us  into
competing  racial  factions;  it  threatens  to  carry  us
further  from  the  goal  of a  political  system  in  which
race  no  longer  matters—a  goal  that  the  Fourteenth
and  Fifteenth  Amendments  embody,  and  to  which
the  Nation  continues  to  aspire.”  Miller,  515  U.S.  at
912  (quoting  Shaw,  509  U.S.  at  657);  cf.  Shaw,  509
U.S.  at  648-49  (“When  racial  or  religious  lines  are
drawn  by  the  State,  the  multiracial,  multireligious
communities  that  our  Constitution  seeks  to  weld  to-
gether  as  one  become  separatist;  antagonisms  that
relate  to  race  or  to  religion  rather  than  to  political
issues  are  generated;  communities  seek  not  the  best
representative  but  the  best  racial  or  religious  parti-
san.  Since  that  system  is at  war  with  the  democratic
ideal,  it  should  find  no  footing  here.”);  Miller,  515
U.S.  at  927  (“If our  society  is to  continue  to  progress
as  a  multi-racial  democracy,  it  must  recognize  that
the  automatic  invocation  of race  stereotypes  retards

22

that  progress  and  causes  continued  hurt  and  in-
jury.”).

Ill.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Is Incon-
sistent  with  Principles  of Federalism  and
the  Separate  Sovereignty  of the  States.

Under  the  political  process  doctrine,  the  Fed-
eral  Constitution  is violated  when  a  State’s  “political
mechanisms  are  modified  to  place  effective  decision-
making  authority  over  a  racial  issue  at  a  different
level  of government.”  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at
This  doctrine  cannot  be  reconciled  with  the
474.
States’  traditional  authority  as  separate  sovereigns
to  structure  their  governments  and  allocate  (or  real-
locate)  their  lawmaking  authority  as  they  see  fit.

“The  principle  is  well  settled  that  local  gov-
ernmental  units  are  created  as  convenient  agencies
for  exercising  such  of the  governmental  powers  of the
State  as  may  be  entrusted  to  them  in  its  absolute
discretion.  Whether  and  how  to  use  that  discretion
is  a  question  central  to  state  self-government.”  City
of Columbus,  536  U.S.  at  437  (citations  and  internal
quotation  marks  omitted);
also  Gregory  v.
Ashcroft,  501  U.S.  452,  460  (1991)  (“Through  the
structure  of its  government...  a  State  defines  itself
as  a  sovereign.”).  Thus,  as  this  Court  explained  in
Hunter  v.  City  of Pittsburgh,

see

The  number,  nature,  and  duration  of
the  powers  conferred  upon  [its  “political
subdivisions”]  rests  in  the  absolute  dis-
cretion  of  the  state....
The  state,
therefore,  at  its  pleasure,  may  modify  or
In  all
withdraw  all  such  powers  ....
these  respects  the  state  is supreme,  and

23

its  legislative  body,  conforming  its  ac-
tion  to the  state  Constitution,  may  do  as
it  will,  unrestrained  by any  provision  of
the  Constitution  of the  United  States.

207  U.S.  161,  178-79  (1907).

To  be  sure,  subsequent  decisions  have  recog-
nized  certain  limitations  on  these  general  principles.
But  City  of Pittsburgh  and  the  principles  it  enunci-
ates  “continu[e]  to  have  substantial  constitutional
significance  in  emphasizing  the  extraordinarily  wide
latitude  that  States  have  in  creating  various  types  of
political  subdivisions  and  conferring  authority  upon
them.”  Holt  Civic  Club,  439  U.S.  at  71;  accord  City
of  Columbus,  536  U.S.  at  437.
Thus,  “while  the
Equal  Protection  Clause  provides  a  check  on  such
state  authority,”  this  Court’s  “scrutiny  will  not  be  so
demanding  where  [it]  deal[s]  with  matters  resting
firmly  within  a  State’s  constitutional  prerogatives.
This  rule  is  no  more  than  ..
.  a  recognition  of  a
State’s  constitutional  responsibility  for  the  estab-
lishment  and  operation  of its  own  government...
.”
Gregory,  501  U.S.  at  462  (citation  and  internal  quo-
tation  marks  omitted).

The  strict  scrutiny  applied  by  the  political
process  doctrine  to  reallocations  of  state  decision-
making  authority  simply  cannot  be  squared  with  the
“extraordinarily  wide  latitude”  and  “wide  leeway”  af-
forded  States  “when  experimenting  with  the  appro-
priate  allocation  of  state  legislative  power.”
Holt
Civic  Club,  439  U.S.  at  71.  Indeed,  as  explained  by
Justice  Powell  in  dissenting  from  the  holding  in  Se-
attle  School  District,  the  decisions  setting  forth  the
political  process  doctrine

24

offe[r]  no  authority  or  relevant  explana-
tion  for  this  extraordinary  subordina-
tion  of the  ultimate  sovereign  power  of a
State  to  act  with  respect  to  racial  mat-
ters  by  subordinate  bodies.
It  is  a
strange  notion—alien  to  our  system—
that  local  governmental  bodies  can  for-
ever  preempt  the  ability  of  a  State—the
sovereign  power—to  address  a  matter  of
The
compelling  concern  to  the  State.
Constitution  of  the  United  States  does
not  require  such a bizarre  result.

458  U.S.  at  495;  see  also  id.  at  496  (“The  political
process  in  Washington,  as  in  other  States,  permits
persons  who  are  dissatisfied  at  a  local  level  to  appeal
to  the  state  legislature  or  the  people  of the  State  for
redress.  It permits  the  people  of a  State  to  pre-empt
local  policies,  and  to  formulate  new  programs  and
regulations.  Such  a  process  is  inherent  in  the  con-
tinued  sovereignty  of  the  States.
This  is  our  sys-
tem.”);  Pet.  App.  85a-86a  (Sutton,  J.,  dissenting)
(“Odder  still,  the  United  States  Constitution  gener-
ally  does  not  meddle  in  the  way  the  States  structure
their  governments.  ...
[I]t is surely  the  case  that  the
Fourteenth  Amendment  does  not  dictate  the  level  of
government  at  which  a  State  must  enact  a  statewide
ban  on  race  discrimination.”).

IV.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Is Malle-
able  and  Unprincipled.

In  addition  to  the  significant  constitutional
problems  it  poses,  the  political  process  doctrine  is
unpredictable  in  practice,  as  well  illustrated  by  the
numerous  opinions  from  the  en  banc  proceedings  in

25

this  case,  see  Pet.  App.  6a,  5la,  56a,  79a,  80a,  94a,
the  previous  opinion  from  the  Sixth  Circuit  vacating
a  preliminary  injunction  of  Proposal  2,  see  id.  at
224a-254a,  and  the  opinions  from  the  Ninth  Circuit
and  California  Supreme  Court  rejecting  closely
analogous  political  process  claims,  see  Coalition  for
Econ.  Equity,  122  F.3d  692;  Coral  Constr.,  235  P.3d
947.  These  conflicting  results  reflect  the  malleable
and  unprincipled  nature  of  the  test  established  by
the  political  process  cases.

As  Seattle  School  District  makes  clear,  the  po-
litical  process  doctrine  does  not  apply  to  “laws  struc-
turing  political  institutions  or  allocating  political
power  according  to  ‘neutral  principles,  ”  but  only
those  that  use  “the  racial  nature  of an  issue  to  define
458
the  governmental  decisionmaking  structure.”
U.S.  at  470.  The  doctrine  is not  limited,  however,  to
laws  explicitly  addressing  race,  id.,  but  applies  more
broadly  to  laws  that  have  a  “racial  focus,”  id.  at  474,
or  that  alter  “the  political  process  or  the  decision-
making  mechanism  used  to  address  racially  con-
scious  legislation,”  id.  at  485.

These  open-ended  formulations  are  very  diffi-
cult  to  apply  with  any  degree  of consistency,  as  can
be  seen  simply  by  comparing  the  decision  in  Seattle
School  District  with  Crawford,  a  similar  case  decided
the  very  same  day.  On  the  one  hand,  the  Court  in
Seattle  School  District  held  that  a  popularly  enacted
constitutional  amendment  prohibiting  school  busing
(except  as  a  remedy  for  a  constitutional  violation  or
for  a  variety  of  enumerated  reasons  unrelated  to
race)  had  a  “racial  focus”  and  thus  was  subject  to  the
political  process  doctrine.
See  458  U.S.  at  462-63,
474;  see  also  id.  at  471  (“despite  its  facial  neutrality
there  is little  doubt  that  the  initiative  was  effectively

26

drawn  for  racial  purposes”).  On  the  other  hand,  in
Crawford,  the  Court  rejected  an  equal  protection
challenge  to
a  popularly  enacted  constitutional
amendment  forbidding  state  courts  from  ordering
“pupil  school  assignment  or  transportation  in the  ab-
sence  of  a  Fourteenth  Amendment  violation,”  even
though  it was  “clear  that  court-ordered  busing  in  ex-
cess  of that  required  by the  Fourteenth  Amendment,
as  one  means  of desegregating  schools,  prompted  the
initiation  and  probably  the  adoption  of  [the  amend-
ment}.”  458  U.S.  at  537,  538  n.18;  see  also  id.  at  538
(distinguishing  “between  state  action  that  discrimi-
nates  on  the  basis  of  race  and  state  action  that  ad-
dresses,  in  neutral  fashion,  race-related  matters”).
These  conflicting  results,  reached  by shifting  majori-
ties  of the  same  Court,  well  illustrate  the  unpredict-
ability  the  political  process  doctrine  introduces  into
equal  protection  analysis.

Further,  Seattle  School  District  held  that  the
amendment  at  issue  had  “a  racial  focus,”  despite  its
“facial  neutrality,”  because  it  made  “busing  for  inte-
gration”  impossible  to  adopt  locally  and  because,
“(flor  present  purposes,  it  is  enough  that  minorities
may  consider  busing  for  integration  to  be  legislation
that  is in  their  interest.”  458  U.S.  at  471,  474  (anter-
nal  quotation  marks  omitted).  Taken  at  face  value,
this  breathtakingly  broad  formulation  would  seem-
ingly  extend  the  reach  of  the  political  process  doc-
trine  to  laws  making  it  more  difficult  to  enact  any
policy  that  minorities  might  consider  to  be  in  their
interest,  however  facially  neutral  such  laws  may  be.
As  such,  it  is  difficult  to  reconcile  with  James  uv.
Valtierra,  402  U.S.  137  (1971),  which  declined  to  ap-
ply  the  political  process  doctrine  to  a  state  constitu-
tional  amendment  imposing  unique  procedural  hur-
dles  to  the  public  development,  construction,  or  ac-

27

quisition  of “low-rent  housing  project[s],”  id.  at  139,
on  the  ground  that  the  amendment  applied  to  “any
low-rent  public  housing  project,  not  only  for  projects
which  will  be occupied  by a  racial  minority”  and  thus
was  “seemingly  neutral  on  its  face,”  id.  at  141;  but  ef.
Village  of Arlington  Heights  v.  Metropolitan  Hous.
Dev.  Corp.,  429  U.S.  252,  269  (1977)  (acknowledging
that  a  challenged  restriction  on  low  income  housing
“djid]  arguably  bear  more  heavily  on  racial  minori-
ties”).

Elsewhere,  the  Seattle  School  District  Court
offered  a  number  of alternative  formulations,  assert-
ing  that  the  initiative  amendment  challenged  there
had  a  racial  focus  because  the  practice  it  restricted
“at  bottom  inures  primarily  to  the  benefit  of the  mi-
nority,  and  is designed  for  that  purpose,”  458  U.S.  at
472,  and  because  “the  initiative  makes  the  enact-
ment  of racially  beneficial  legislation  difficult  ...  ,”
id.  at  483;  see  also  id.  at  467  (political  process  doc-
trine  apphes  to  laws  that  “place  special  burdens  on
the  ability  of  minority  groups  to  achieve  beneficial
legislation”);  id.  at  470  (doctrine  applies  to  laws  that
“make  it more  difficult  for certain  racial  and  religious
minorities  .
.  to  achieve  legislation  that  is  in  their
interest”).

.

Similarly,  in  applying  the  political  process
doctrine  in  this  case,  the  Sixth  Circuit  stated,  alter-
natively,  that  that  doctrine  applies  to  laws  that  place
comparative  burdens  on  “issues  of  importance  to
[minority  groups],”  Pet.  App.  15a,  on  “minority  objec-
tives,  whatever  those  objectives  may  be,”  id.  at  41a,
or  on  “any  legislation  in the  interest  of racial  minori-
ties,”  id.  at  40a,  and  thus  make  it  more  difficult  for
minority  groups  “to  achieve  their  political  objectives,”
id.  37a,  or  “to  enact  policies  that  are  in  their  favor,”

28

id.  at  39a;  see  also  id.  140a  (doctrine  applies  to  bur-
dens  on  “a  political  program  of particular  importance
to  racial  minorities”);  id.  114a  (“issues  of importance
to  racial  minorities”).

It is far  from  clear  that  these  varying  formula-
tions  in  any  way  limit  the  reach  of the  political  proc-
ess  doctrine.  They  do,  however,  add  to  its  malleabil-
ity and  unpredictable  application.  How,  for example,
is one  to  determine  whether  a  policy  made  more  dif-
ficult  to  adopt  by  a  reallocation  of  decisionmaking
authority  to  a  higher  level  of  government  “inures
primarily  to  the  benefit  of  the  minority,”  furthers
“minority  objectives,”  or  is  “in  the  interest  of  racial
minorities,”  “racially  beneficial,”  or  “of particular  im-
portance  to  racial  minorities”?  Does  one  look  simply
to  the  racial  make-up  of those  who  support  the  policy
in  question?
See  id.  at  24a  (quoting  Seattle  Sch.
Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  474)  (“given  that  racial  minorities
lobbied  for  the  implementation  of  the  very  policies
that  Proposal  2 permanently  eliminates,  it is beyond
question  that  Proposal  2  targets  policies  that  ‘mi-
.  [to be]  in  their  interest’  ”).
norities  may  consider  .
But  as  the  Court  acknowledged  in Seattle  School  Dis-
trict—which,  as  explained  above,  held  that  “busing
for  integration”  was  such  a  policy—“[iJjt  undoubtedly
is true...  that  the  proponents  of mandatory  integra-
tion  cannot  be classified  by race:  Negroes  and  whites
may  be  counted  among  both  the  supporters  and  the
opponents
[the  constitutional
amendment  invalidated  in  that  case].”  458  U.S.  at
472.  And  the  Court  acknowledged  that  the  same  was
true  of the  burdened  policy  in  Hunter,  as  well:  “we
may  fairly  assume  that  members  of the  racial  major-
ity  both  favored  and  benefited  from  Akron’s  fair
housing  ordinance.”  Jd.

of  Initiative

350

.

29

Alternatively,  does  one  attempt  to  determine
who  benefits  from  the  policy?
See  Pet.  App.  24a
(“There  is  no  material  difference  between  the  enact-
ment  in  Seattle  and  Proposal  2, as  both  targeted  poli-
cies  that  benefit  minorities  by  enhancing  their  edu-
cational  opportunities  and  promoting  classroom  di-
versity.”);  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  472  (busing
for  integration  “at  bottom  inures  primarily  to  the
benefit  of the  minority,  and  is designed  for  that  pur-
pose”).  But  again,  as  the  Court  in  Seattle  School  Dis-
trict  recognized,  “it  should  be  equally  clear  that
white  as  well  as  Negro  children  benefit  from  expo-
sure  to  ‘ethnic  and  racial  diversity  in  the  class-
room.”
Id.;  see  also  id.  (acknowledging  that  the
same  was  true  of policy  at  issue  in Hunter).

More  important  still,  the  issue  of who  benefits
from  a  particular  policy  such  as  the  racial  admissions
preferences  at  issue  here  or  the  mandatory  busing  in
Seattle  School  District  is  often  sharply  disputed.
Thus,  as  Justice  Powell  explained  in  Seattle  School
District,  “[i]t  is far from  clear  that  in the  absence  of a
constitutional  violation,  mandatory  busing  necessar-
ily benefits  racial  minorities  or  that  it is even  viewed
with  favor  by racial  minorities.”  Jd.  at  497  n.12;  cf.
Crawford,  458  U.S.  at  537  (amendment  barring
court-ordered  busing  absent  Fourteenth  Amendment
violation  “seeks  to  confer  {a benefit]—neighborhood
schooling—([that]  is  made  available  regardless  of
race”).  Nor  is  it undisputed  that  racial  minorities  as
a  whole  have  benefitted  from  racially  preferential
See,  e.g.,  RICHARD  H.
college  admissions  policies.
SANDER  &  STUART  TAYLOR,  JR.,  MISMATCH:  HOW  AF-
FIRMATIVE  ACTION  HURTS  STUDENTS  IT’S  INTENDED  TO

HELP,  AND  WHY  UNIVERSITIES  WON’T  ADMIT  IT
(2012).5

At  bottom,  then,  the  political  process  doctrine
may  simply  license—or  even  require—judges  to  de-
termine,  by  their  own  lights,  whether  policies  bur-
dened  by  state  reallocations  of  decisionmaking  au-
Seattle
thority  are  or  are  not  “racially  beneficial.”
Sch.  Dist.,  458  U.S.  at  483.  If that  is so,  the  inconsis-
tent  and  diverging  applications  of  the  political  proc-
In  all
ess  doctrine  should  come  as  no  surprise.
events,  this  amorphous  and  malleable  doctrine  has
little  to  recommend  it  as  a  principled  rule  of consti-
tutional  law.

5  Wholly  apart  from  their  (disputed)  effect  on  members
of racial  minorities  afforded  preferential  treatment,  racial  pref-
erences  plainly  operate  to  the  disadvantage  of many  individuals
of  other  racial  minorities,  such  as  Asian  Americans,  who  are
denied  such  preferences.  See,  e.g.,  Brief  for  Richard  Sander  &
Stuart  Taylor,  Jr.  as  Amici  Curiae  in  Support  of Neither  Party
at  4  n.4,  16,  Fisher  v.  University  of Tex.,  No.11-345  (U.S.  May
29,  2012);  Brief  for  the  Louis  D.  Brandeis  Center  for  Human
Rights  Under  Law,  the  80-20  National  Asian-American  Educa-
tional  Foundation,  et  al.  as  Amicus  Curiae  in  Support  of Peti-
tioner  at  5-9,  13-20,  Fisher  v.  University  of Tex.,  No.11-345  (U.S.
May  29,  2012).  The  harm  suffered  by  Asian  Americans  under
programs  employing  racial  preferences  illustrates  that  one  of
the  pohtical  process  doctrine’s  most  basic  premises——-that  our
national  community  is  divided  neatly  into  a  “majority”  group
and  a  “minority”  group,  each  of which  share  collective  interests
and  goals—is  both  simplistic  and  counterproductive.

31

V.

The  Political  Process  Doctrine  Is Unnec-
essary  To Vindicate  the  Equal  Protection
Clause.

Finally,  conventional  equal  protection  doc-
trine—which  applies  strict  scrutiny  to  laws  drawing
race-based  classifications  as  well  as  to  facially  neu-
tral  laws  that  have  a  discriminatory  purpose—
suffices  to  ferret  out  “the  racial  discrimination  which
it  was  the  object  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  to
eliminate”  and  thus  to  vindicate  “the  principle  of
equality  at  the  heart”  of that  Amendment.  Loving  v.
Virginia,  388  U.S.  1,  11,  12  (1967).
Indeed,  to  the
best  of  our  knowledge,  this  Court  has  expressly  re-
lied  on  the  political  process  doctrine  to  invalidate  a
law  only  twice  in  the  forty-four  years  since  the  doc-
trine  was  invented.*  The  rareness  with  which  it has
been  invoked  strongly  suggests  that  the  doctrine  is
dispensable.
Cf.  Pet.  App.  96a  (Griffin,  J.,  dissent-
ing)  (“The  infrequent  use  of the  doctrine  is  not  sur-
prising  given  its  lack  of a  constitutional  basis.”).

To  be  sure,  this  Court’s  decision  in  Seattle
School  District  likely  would  have  come  out  differ-
ently  but  for  application  of the  political  process  doc-
trine.
But  we  respectfully  submit  that  that  deci-
sion—which  invalidated  a  popularly  enacted  consti-

6  In  addition  to  the  two  times  this  Court  has  expressly
relied  on  the  political  process  doctrine  to  invalidate  a  law,  the
Court  also  affirmed  without  opinion  a  three-judge  district  court
decision  applying  the  political  process  doctrine  to  invalidate  a
New  York  law  limiting  the  ability  of appointed  school  boards  to
employ  busing  or  mandatory  school  assignments  to  eliminate  de
facto  school  segregation.
See  Nyquist  v.  Lee,  402  U.S.  935
(1971),  affirming  Lee  v.  Nyquist,  318  F.  Supp.  710  (W.D.NLY.
This  case  thus  was  much  like  Seattle  School  District.
1970).
See  458  U.S.  at  469  (relying  on  Lee  v.  Nyquist).

32

tutional  amendment  that  prohibited  busing  “for  ra-
cially-balancing  purposes”  in  the  absence  of a  consti-
tutional  violation,  458  U.S.  at  463,  and  thus  insu-
lated  from  state  control  a  local  busing  plan,  a  subse-
quent  iteration  of which  was  ultimately  determined
to  itself  violate  the  Constitution,  see  Parents  In-
volved,  551  U.S.  701—was  not  a  necessary,  or  even
faithful,  application  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment.
The  decision,  accordingly,  should  be overruled.

By  contrast,  this  Court’s  decision  in  Hunter
likely  would  have  come  out  the  same  way  even  in the
absence  of  the  political  process  doctrine.
In  that
case,  after  the  city  council  of Akron,  Ohio,  enacted  an
ordinance  prohibiting  housing  discrimination  based
on  “race,  color,  religion,  ancestry  or  national  origin,”
the  voters  of that  city  amended  the  city  charter  ret-
roactively  “to  prevent  the  city  council  from  imple-
menting  any  ordinance  dealing  with  racial,  religious,
or  ancestral  discrimination  in  housing  without  the
approval  of  the  majority  of  the  voters  of  Akron.”
Hunter,  393  U.S.  at  386.

On  the  facts  of  Hunter,  it  is  difficult  to  con-
ceive  of any  reasonable,  nondiscriminatory  basis  for
this  amendment  of  the  city  charter.
Accordingly,
even  assuming  this  amendment  could  properly  be
characterized  as  facially  neutral,  it  very  likely  re-
flected  a  racially  discriminatory  purpose.
Indeed,
Hunter  may  well  have  presented  one  of  the  “  ‘rare’
statutes  that,  although  race  neutral,  are,  on  their
face,  ‘unexplainable  on  grounds  other  than  race.’  ”
Shaw,  509  U.S.  at  643  (quoting  Arlington  Heights,
429  U.S.  at  266).
For  while  the  city  characterized
the  amendment  “simply  as  a  public  decision  to  move
slowly  in  the  delicate  area  of race  relations,”  Hunter,
393  U.S.  at  392,  this  Court  has  made  clear  that

33

avoiding  “private  [racial]  biases  and  the  possible  in-
jury  they  might  inflict”  is not  a  permissible  basis  for
government  action,  Palmore  v.  Sidoti,  466  U.S.  429,
“The  Constitution  cannot  contro]  such
433  (1984).
prejudices  but  neither  can  it  tolerate  them.  Private
biases  may  be  outside  the  reach  of  the  law,  but  the
law  cannot,  directly  or  indirectly,  give  them  effect.”
Id.;  cf.  Loving,  388  U.S.  at  11  (law  must  reflect  a
“permissible  state  objective,  independent  of  the  ra-
cial  discrimination  which  it  was  the  object  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment  to  eliminate”).  Accordingly,
the  challenged  law  in  Hunter  almost  certainly  would
have  been  subject  to,  and  failed,  strict  scrutiny  even
under  conventional  equal  protection  principles.

*

*

*

This  Court  should  overrule  the  political  proc-
ess  doctrine.
This  malleable  and  unprincipled  doc-
trine  invites  absurd  results,  as  well  illustrated  by the
decision  below.  Furthermore,  the  doctrine  is not  only
unnecessary  to  redress  genuine  violations  of  the
Fourteenth  Amendment,  it  is  irreconcilable  with
fundamental  principles  of  equal  protection  and  in-
fringes  on  the  separate  sovereignty  of the  States  with
respect  to  matters  “central  to  state  self-government.”
City of Columbus,  536  U.S.  at  437.

34

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  this  Court  should
reverse  the  judgment  of the  Sixth  Circuit  invalidat-
ing Proposal)  2.

Respectfully  submitted,

This  commonsense  conclusion—that  states  can
constitutionally
race-based
the
preference  policies  in  admissions—-comes  not  from
Grutter,  Gratz.  or  Fisher,  but  from  the  joint  plurality
opinion  of  Justices  Brennan,  White,  Marshall,  and
Blackmun  in  Regents  of  University  of  California  v.
Bakke,  438  U.S.  265,  379  (1978).  In  other  words.
even  the  Justices  in  Bakke  who  would  have  upheld  a
racial  quota  system  in  university  admissions  saw  no
problem  with  a  state’s  decision  to  ban  racial  prefer-
ences  as  a  means  to  achieve  a  diverse  student  bodv.
Against  this  backdrop,  Respondents  and  their  amici
fail  to  explain  how  Michigan  citizens  violated  equal
protection  when  they  enacted  Article  1,  § 26,  which
requires  that  all  applicants  to  the  State’s  universities
be  treated  equally,  without  regard  to  race  or  sex.

There  are  three  flaws  in  Respondents’  approach.
First.  Respondents  fail
to  acknowledge  that  for
purposes  of  an  equal-protection  analysis,  there  is  a
fundamental  difference  between  enacting  a  new  law
that  creates  an  obstacle  to  special  treatment  (e.g.,
Hunter  v.  Erickson,  393  U.S.  385  (1969)).  and  a  new
law  that  requires  equal  treatment  (e.g.,  § 26).  A  law
that  eschews  the  use  of  race  and  requires  equal
treatment  cannot  be  said  to  “discriminate”  on  the
basis  of  race.  And  any  contrary  conclusion  would
render  every  equal-treatment  law  invalid,  including
the  federal  Fair  Housing  Act.

lo

Second,  even  Respondents  admit  that  “[rjace-
conscious  action  that  does  ‘not  lead  to  different
treatment  based  on  a  classification  that  tells  each
[person]  he  or  she  is to  be  defined  by race’  ordinarily
need  not  satisfy  ‘strict  scrutiny  to  be  found  permis-
sible.”  Cantrell  Br.  29  (quoting  Parents  Involved  in
Cmtyv.  Sch.  v.  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.  No.  1,  551  U.S.  701,
789  (2007)  (Kennedy,  J..  concurring)).  The  logical
corollary  is  that  a  law  barring  racial  classifications  is
not  itself  a  racial  classification  and  need  not  satisfy
“strict  scrutiny  to be  found  permissible.”

Section  26  does  not  authorize  some  racial  groups
to  lobby  admissions  officials  while  prohibiting  others
from  doing  so.  Nor  does  § 26  use  a  race  classification
in  the
to  advantage  or  disadvantage  any  race
admissions  process.  It  prohibits  making  a  racial
classification  in  the  first  place.  Accordingly.  there  is
no  basis  to  subject  § 26  to  strict  scrutiny,  or  even  to
apply  the  political-restructuring  doctrine  to  it  at  all,
unless
discriminatory
can
purpose.  And  the  district  court  granted  summary
judgment  to  the  State  in  part  because  “plaintiffs
cannot  show  that  the  measure  was  enacted  with  a
Pet.  Supp.  App.  319a,  a
discriminatory  intent,”
ruling  Respondents  have  never  challenged.

Respondents

prove.

Third,  a  state  constitutional  amendment  is  an
easier  way  to  change  public-university  admissions
practices  than  pursuing  such  changes  university  by
university.  And  by  arguing  that  Michigan's  citizens
can  eliminate  through  constitutional  amendment  the
use  of  alumni  preferences  or  athletic  ability  but  not
racial  preferences,  it  is  Respondents  and  their  amici
who  single  out  race  for  differing  treatment.

highlights
position.
Supremacy  Clause  preemption  comes  into  play  only
after  a  court  has  determined  that  a  federal  law  is
valid.  And  if  Respondents’  political-restructuring
argument  is  correct,  the  federal  Fair  Housing  Act  (or
any  analogous  state  law)  would  violate  the  Equal
Protection  Clause,  meaning  the  state  law  allowing
preferences  would  reverse-preempt  the  federal  law
requiring  equal  treatment.  The  Supremacy  Clause
would  not  save  federal  law  that  had  just  been
declared  unconstitutional.  And  it  cannot  possibly  be
correct  that  federal  laws  requiring  equal  treatment
must  fall—due  to  the  Equal  Protection  Clause,  of all
things—to  state  or  local  laws  mandating  unequal
treatment,  i.e.,  preferences.

when

race

is

Bush  was  a  racial  gerrymandering  case  where
the  Court  applied  strict  scrutiny  to  congressional
redistricting  legislation  that  was  so  irregular  on  its
face  that  it  could  only  rationally  be  viewed  as  an
“effort  to  segregate  races  for  purposes  of voting.”  517
U.S.  at  958  (per  Justice  O'Connor,  with  the  Chief
Justice  and  one  Justice  concurring,  and  two  Justices
concurring  in  the  judgment).

Contrary  to  the  position  Respondents  take  here,
the  Bush  Court  emphasized  that  strict  scrutiny  does
not  apply  merely  because  redistricting  is  performed
with  consciousness  of race.  517  U.S.  at  95&.  Instead,
a  plaintiff  must  demonstrate  that  racial  considera-
tions  predominated  over  race-neutral  considerations.
Id.  at  964.  And  there  is  no  evidence  that  such  was
the  case  with  § 26,  which  requires  race  neutrality.

Similarly,  Shaw  v.  Reno  involved  redistricting
legislation  so  extremely  irregular  that  it  could  be
viewed  rationally  only  as  an  effort  to  segregate  race
for  purposes  of  voting.  509  U.S.  at  642.  Nothing  in
the  opinion  suggests  the  Court  was  abandoning  the
requirement  that  a  plaintiff  pursuing  an  equal-
protection  claim  must  prove a racial  classification  or
discriminatory  intent.

This  language  makes  clear  that  this  Court  in
Shau:  rejected  racial  gerrymandering  because  it
classified  individuals  based  on  their  race,  which  is
abhorrent  to  the  Constitution.  Article  I, § 26  does  not
do  that;  § 26  is  a  provision  that  requires  race
neutrality.  And  under  this  Court's  well-established
equal-protection  precedent,  there  is  no  basis  to
invalidate  a  law  that  forbids  racial  preferences  and
instead  requires  equal  treatment  on  the  basis  of race.

“race-specific  burden  on  minorities.”  458  U.S.  at
536.  And  analogous  to  Respondents’  argument
here—that  individuals  may  still  lobby  for  non-race-
based  preferences.  such  as  those  based  on  alumni
status—the  Crawford  plaintiffs  emphasized  that
“other  state-created  rights  may  be  vindicated  by  the
state  courts  without  limitation  on  remedies.”  Jd.

Nonetheless,  this  Court  rejected  the  suggestion
that  Proposition  I  imposed  a  “racial  classification.”
458  U.S.  at  537.  That  was  because  Proposition  |
“neither  says  nor  implies  that  persons  are  to  be
treated  differently  on  account  of their  race.  It  simply
forbids  state  courts  to  order  pupil  assignment  or
transportation  in  the  absence  of  a  Fourteenth
Amendment  violation.”  Jd.  (emphasis  added).  “Nor
can  it be  said  that  Proposition  | distorts  the  political

it  allocates

was  enacted  by  a  racially  discriminatory  process  or
has  a  racially  discriminatory  impact.  Neither  is true.

tion.
26  neither  says  nor  implies  that

Moreover,  §  26  does  not  distort  the  political
process  for  racial  reasons,  and  it  does  not  allocate
government  or  judicial  power  on  the  basis  of  a
discriminatory  principle.  Quite  the  opposite,  §  26
bars  Michigan  public  universities  from  using  race  for
any  purpose  at  all.  Because  § 26  neither  embodies  a
racial  classification  nor  stems  from  a  discriminatory

it  cannot

This  point  is  driven  home  by  Respondents
contention
that  §  26  “needlessly  heightens  the
salience  of race  in  the  political  process  and,  by doing
so,  ‘contributes  to  an  escalation  of racial  hostility  and
conflict.”  Cantrell  Br.  32  (quotation  omitted).  That  is
surely  wrong.  Choosing  to  create  a  color-blind  system
furthers  the  goals  of  the  Equal  Protection  Clause,
even  if  the  Clause  allows  some  form  of  affirmative
action.  To  say  that  a  statewide  law  regarding
affirmative  action  (either  forbidding  it  or  approving
it)  should  be  barred  because  it  “contributfes]  to  an
escalation  of  racial  hostility  and  conflict”  is  to  say
that  the  democratic  process  should  not  be  allowed  to
operate  on  controversial  issues  relating  to  race.

reconciled  with  Crawford.  which  makes  clear  that
§ 26  is  facially  neutral  and  does  not  embody a racial
classification.  Consider  Hunter  and  Seattle,  both  of
which  similarly  involved  facially  neutral  laws.  This
Court  did  not  strike  those  laws  down  because  they
embodied  a  racial  classification;  rather,  the  opinions
in  both  cases  relied  on  discriminatory  intent.  Hunter
thwarted  Akron’s  attempts  to  stop  private  racial
discrimination  in  housing;  discriminatory  intent  is
the  only  logical  explanation  for  why  voters  would
repeal  a  law  requiring  equal  treatment.  Seattle
involved  an  initiative  that  sought  to  facilitate  private
discrimination  and  deny  equal  treatment.  City  of

The  question  is  not  who  is  right  and  who  is
wrong  about  whether  preferences  ultimately  help  or
hurt  minorities.  Rather,  the  question  is  whether
Michigan's  citizens  were  motivated  by discriminatory
intent  and  no  other  factor  when  they  cast  their  vote
for  §  26.  And  the  answer  to  that  question  is  an
unequivocal  no,  as  the  district  court  concluded.  et.
Supp.  App.  319a  (“plaintiffs  cannot  show  that  the
measure  was  enacted  with  a  discriminatory  intent”).
And  in  the  absence  of  such  intent  or  a  facial  racial
classification,  there  is no  equal-protection  violation.

Ill. Article  1,  § 26  does  not  create  a  political
process  for  changing  university  admissions
policies  that  is  any  more  difficult  than  the
pre-§  26  political  process.

As  Michigan  explained  in  its  initial  brief,  the
reality  of  the  universitv-admissions  policy-setting
environment  bears  little  resemblance  to
a  true
political  process.  one  where  the  polity  has  both
access  to  and  a  meaningful  ability  to  change  public
policy.  Fur  example.  only  the  Wayne  State  Law
School  faculty  has  the  authority  to  approve  the
admissions  policy:  it  is  not  subject  to  approval  by the
Wayne  State  University  Board  of Governors.  J.A.  21.
And  as  the  Law  School's  Dean  testified,  if the  Board
of Governors  sought  to  change  the  policy,  that  action
“would  precipitate  a  constitutional  crisis.”  J.A.  22.

Respondents  look  past  this  record  evidence  and
instead  try  to  make  the  case  that  university  boards
of trustees  are  legally  empowered  with  overseeing  all
day-to-day  operations  at  Michigan's  public  univer-
sities.  including  admissions.  E.g.,  Bd.  of Governors  of
Wayne  State  Univ.  Br.  1-23;  Regents  of the  Univ.  of
Mich.  Br.  4-17.  This  proposition  may  be  true  as  a
theoretical  matter,  but  it certainly  is  not  reflected  in
the  actual]  university  practice.

In  any  event,  even  if  one  accepts  Respondents
proposition,  that  does  not  alter  the  reality  that  § 26
actually  had  the  effect  of making  it easier  for  interest
sroups  to  change  university  admissions  policies.
Consider  the  political-process  hoops  that  an  advocate
had  to  jump  through  before  § 26  and  compare  them
with  the  process  that  applies  now:

IV.  Response  to  Respondents’

and

amici’s

remaining  arguments

Once  it  is  clear  that  § 26  (1)  does  not  embody  a
racial  classification,  (2)  is  not  a  result  of  a  discrimi-
natory  purpose,  (3)  does  not  create  a  bar  to  equal
treatment  (but  rather  requires  it),  and  (4)  makes  the
political  process  for  amending  university-admission
policies  easier,  not  harder,  there  is  very  little  over
which  to  argue.  That  said,  a  few  additional  points
advanced  by Respondents  are  worth  addressing.

First.  the  Sixth  Circuit  en  banc  majority  correctly
held  that  a  political-restructuring  claim  requires
proof  that  an  enactment  like  §  26  that  targets  a
“policy  or  program  that  ‘inures  primarily  to  the
benefit  of  the  minority,  and  is  designed  for  that
purpose.”  Pet.  App.  22a  (citing  Seattle,  458  U.S.  at
467,  472)  (emphasis  added).  As  Michigan  explained
in  its  initial  brief,  that  is  a  prerequisite  Respondents
cannot  satisfy  here.  because  the  type  of  admissions
policy  for  which  Respondents  would  like  to  lobby—a

306,  330—33  (2003).

statistics,  numbers  which  are  noticeably  absent  from
the  University’s  brief.

published

In  a  press  release  recounting  the  makeup  of  the
University's  first  freshman  class  post  §  26  (the
freshman  class  entering  in  2008),  the  University
noted
the  percentage  of  underrepresented
minorities  (“African  Americans,”  “Hispanic  Ameri-
cans,”  and  “Native  Americans”)  in  the  class  was
“relatively  unchanged”  at  10.47%,  versus  10.85%  for
the  class  entering  in  2007."

Then  a  strange  thing  happened.  Beginning  with
the  class  of  2010  freshmen,  the  University  allowed
incoming  students  to  check  multiple  boxes  represent-
ing  each  student's  applicable  race  category.*  And  for
reasons
the
enrollment  percentages  for  only  those  students  who
“African
identified

a
