at  591  (referring  in  part  to  the  findings  of  the
Michigan  Civil  Rights  Commission).  Thus  supporters
of  Proposal  2  now  offer  as  the  solution  for  those
pursuing  minority  interests  exactly  the  political
process  that  even  with  substantial  financing  from

meet  without  resorting  to  “fraud  and  deception.”

CONCLUSION

Interpreted  as  drafted,  Michigan  Constitution,
Article  1.  §26’s  prohibition  of preferential  treatment
does  nothing  to  change  the  law  as  the  term  should
not  be  applied  to  diversity  admissions  programs.

in  the  same

Interpreted  as  intended,  Michigan  Constitution,
Article  1,  §26  prevents  minority  applicants  from
being  assessed
“holistic”  manner
considered  as  other  applicants.  It  denies  universities
the  academic  freedom  to  create  a  student  body  that
is 1n the  best  interests  of the  university  itself or  of its
the
students,
benefits  of diversity.  It requires  universities  to  treat
race  differently  than  all  other  considerations  about
what  make  applicants  unique  individuals,  and  it
violates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause.

students

denying

thereby

those

Most  dangerously,  §26  creates  a  separate  and
very  unequal  political  process  for  amending  only
university  admissions  policies,  and  specifically  only
those  parts  affecting  minority  interests.  Allowing
majority  rule  to  reset  minority  rights  in  this  manner
is  inconstant  with  not  only  the  letter,  but  also  the
very  spirit,  of equal  protection.

This  Court  should,  by providing  a  clear  definition
for  “preferential  treatment,”  determine  either  that
§26  merely  restates  existing  federal  equal  protection
law  and  thus  does  not  violate  the  Equal  Protection
Clause.  However,  if this  Court  concludes  a  ban  on
preferential  treatment  is  broader  than  a  ban  on
discrimination  then  it  should  find  §26  violates  the
it  compels
Equal
universities  to  discriminate  by  not  considering  the

Protection

because

Clause

41

attributes  of minority  applicants  in the  same  holistic
way  as  others  it, and/or  because  it used  majority  rule
to  erect  political  roadblocks  that  apply  only  to  issues
and  policies  involving  minority  interests.

QUESTION  PRESENTED

Whether  a  state  violates  the  Equal  Protection
Clause  by  amending  its  constitution  to  prohibit  race-
and  sex-based  discrimination  or  preferential  treat-
ment  in  public-university  admissions  decisions.

MSLF  is  a  non-profit,  public  interest  legal  foun-
dation  organized  under  the  laws  of the  State  of Colo-
rado.  MSLF  is  dedicated
and
preservation  of  individual  liberty,  the  right  to  own
and  use  property,  limited  and  ethical  government,
and  the  free  enterprise  system.  Since  its  establish-
ment  in  1977,  MSLF  has  sought  to  protect  individual
liberties  and  defend  the  guarantee  of equal  protection
found  in  the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments.  See,
e.g.,  Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  v.  Pea,  515  U.S.  200
(1995);  Concrete  Works  of Colorado  v.  City  and  County

'  The  parties  have  consented  to  the  filing  of  this  amicus
curiae  brief  by  filing  blanket  consents  with  the  Court.  See
Supreme  Court  Rule  37.3(a).  Pursuant  to  Supreme  Court  Rule
37.6,  the  undersigned  affirms  that  no  counsel  for  a  party
authored  this  brief  in  whole  or  in  part,  and  no  person  or  entity
other  than  MSLF,  its  members,  or  its  counsel,  made  a  monetary

this  brief.

2

of Denver,  321  F.3d  950  (10th  Cir.  2003);  Wygant  v.
Jackson  Board  of Education,  476  U.S.  267  (1986).

Especially  relevant  to  this  case  is  MSLF’s  work
in Adarand  and  Concrete  Works  of Colorado,  in  which
MSLF  argued  for  the  appropriate  interpretation  of
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  in  government  contract-
ing  cases.  MSLF  brings  a  unique  perspective  to  this
case  and  believes  that  its  amicus  curiae  brief  will
assist  this  Court  in  deciding  the  case.  Based  upon  its
experience  and  perspective,  MSLF  respectfully  sub-
mits  this  amicus  curiae  brief in  support  of Petitioner.

—+

STATEMENT  OF  THE  CASE

In  November  2006,  58°  of  voters  in  Michigan
voted  to  amend  Michigan's  State  Constitution  to
prohibit  the  granting  of  preferential  treatment  in
public  education,  government  contracting,  and  public
employment  based  on  race.  sex,  ethnicity,  or  national
origin.  See  Mich.  Const.  art.  I,  § 26  (“§$ 26”).  In  2006,
Coalition  to  Defend  Affirmative  Action  challenged  the
constitutionality  of  the  amendment  in  the  United
States  District  Court  for  the  Eastern  District  of
Michigan.  The  District  Court  entered  a  stipulation  of
preliminary  injunction,  which  postponed  the  imple-
mentation  of  § 26  until  after  the  next  university
admissions  cycle.  The  Sixth  Circuit,  however,  granted
an  emergency  motion  for  a  stay  pending  appeal,
finding  a  “strong  likelihood”  that  the  preliminary

3

injunction  would  be  reversed  on  the  merits.  The  first
Sixth  Circuit  panel  to  hear  the  case  followed  the
Ninth  Circuit’s  rulings  in  Coalition  for  Economic
Equality  v.  Wilson,  122  F.3d  692  (9th  Cir.  1997),  and
Coalition  to  Defend  Affirmative  Action  v.  Brown,  674
F.3d  1128  (9th  Cir.  2012)  and  rejected  plaintiff's
political  restructuring  claim.  The  political  restructur-
ing  theory  of  equal  protection  provides  that  if  an
enactment:  (1)  has  a  racial  focus,  targeting  a  policy  or
program  that  “inures  primarily  to  the  benefit  of the
minority”;  Washington  v.  Seattle  School  District  No.  1,
458  U.S.  457,  485  (1982)  (“Seattle  School  District”),
and  (2)  reallocates  political  power  or  reorders  the
decisionmaking  process  in  a  way  that  places  special
burdens  on  a  minority  group’s  ability  to  achieve  its
goals  through  that  process,  the  enactment  violates
the  Equal  Protection  Clause.  Hunter  v.  Erickson,  393
U.S.  385,  391  (1969).

On  remand,  the  District  Court  upheld  § 26  on  the
merits  but  was  eventually  reversed  by a  second  Sixth
Circuit  panel.  See  Coalition  to  Defend  Affirmative
Action,  Integration  and  Immigrant  Rights  and  Fight
for Equality  By Any  Means  Necessary  v.  Regents  of the
Univ.  of  Michigan,  652  F.3d  607  (6th  Cir.  2011)
(“CDAA  I”).

The  Sixth  Circuit  then  granted  en  banc  review,
and  in  an  8-7  decision,  struck  down  § 26  regarding
public  education,  holding  that  § 26  violates  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  by
denying  minorities  a  “fair  political  process.”  Coalition
to Defend  Affirmative  Action,  Integration  and  Immigrant

4

Rights  and  Fight  for  Equality  By  Any  Means  Neces-
sary  v.  Regents  of the  Univ.  of Michigan,  701  F.3d  466,
474  (6th  Cir.  2012)  (““CDAA  II”).  The  Sixth  Circuit
rejected  the  Ninth  Circuit’s  decisions  in  Wilson,  122
F.3d  692,  and  Brown,  674  F.3d  1128,  and  the  majority
purportedly  relied  on  Hunter,  393  U.S.  385,  and
Seattle  School  District,  458  U.S.  457,  in  striking  down
§ 26.  The  Sixth  Circuit  ruled  that  because  § 26  forced
racial  minorities  to  “surmount  procedural  hurdles  in
reaching  their  objectives  over  which  other  groups  do
not  have  to  leap,”  it  necessarily  violated  the  Equal
Protection  Clause.  CDAA  IJ,  701  F.3d  at  487.  Based
on  the  extra  procedural  hurdles  that  minority  stu-
dents  would  be  forced  to  overcome,  the  Sixth  Circuit
held  that  § 26  denied  “equal  access  to  the  tools  of
political  change,”  with  respect  to  public  university
admissions.  Id.  at  470.

Judge  Gibbons’s  dissent  in  CDAA  II  properly
distinguished  the  cases  relied  upon  by  the  majority.
Judge  Gibbons  noted  that  in  Hunter  this  Court  was
faced  with  the  issue  of the  repeal,  not  the  enactment,
of  a  law  requiring  equal  treatment  of  minorities  in
the  relation  to  the  sale  or  lease  of  real  property.
CDAA  II,  701  F.3d  at  495  (Gibbons,  J.,  dissenting).
Similarly,  the  policy,  which  was  ultimately  invalidat-
ed  by this  Court  in  Seattle  School  District,  was  relat-
ed  only  to  forced  busing  programs  in  the  Seattle  area.
Id.  The  instant  case  involves  a  ballot  initiative  with  a
broad,  state-wide  focus.  Judge  Gibbons  called  the
present  amendment  “a  sea  change  in  state  policy,  of a
kind  not  present  in  Seattle  or  any  other  ‘political

5

structure’  case.”  /d.  at  495  (citations  omitted).  Judge
Gibbons  further  concluded  that  § 26  did  not  reallocate
political  power  in  a  manner  prohibited  by  Hunter  or
Seattle  School  District;  it simply  removed  admissions
policies  from  the  hands  of  university  admissions
personnel,  who  are  unelected  and  unaccountable  to
the  public.  Jd.  at  499.

The  Attorney  General  for  the  State  of  Michigan
petitioned  the  United  States  Supreme  Court  for
review.  This  Court  has  agreed  to  hear  this  case  and  to
to
decide  whether  the  Michigan  voters’  decision
require  equal  treatment  in  public  university  admis-
sions  violates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause.  Schuette
uv.  Coalition  to  Defend  Affirmative  Action,  133  S.Ct.
1633  (Mar.  25, 2013).

+

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT

This  Court  should  reverse  the  Sixth  Circuit’s
erroneous  ruling,  because  § 26  does  not  violate  the
Equal  Protection  Clause.  Indeed,  this  Court  has  ruled
that  States  may  expand  upon  the  protections  found
within  the  United  States  Constitution.  See  PruneYard
Shopping  Center  v.  Robbins,  447  U.S.  74,  81  (1980).
Furthermore,  this  Court  has  determined  that  certain
race-based  admissions  programs  used  by  higher
education  institutions  may  ultimately  violate  the
Equal  Protection  Clause.  See  Fisher  v.  Univ.  of Tex.  at
Austin,  570  U.S.  ___,  2013  WL  3155220,  *15  (2013)
(“If the  Court  were  actually  applying  strict  scrutiny,  it

6

would  require  Texas  either  to  close  the  University  or
to  stop  discrimination  against  applicants  based  on
their  race.”)  (Thomas,  J.,  concurring);  Grutter  v.
Bollinger,  539  U.S.  306,  330,  334  (2003)  (ruling  that
racial  balancing  in  education  is  patently  unconstitu-
tional  and  ruling  that  race-based  admissions  pro-
grams  must  have  an  end  date);  see  also  Palmore  v.
Sidoti,  466  U.S.  429,  432  (1989)  (ruling  that  “[a}  core
purpose  of  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  was  to  do
away  with  all  governmentally  imposed  discrimination
based  on  race.”).  The  voters  of  Michigan  exercised
their  rights  under  the  State’s  democratic  system  and
voted  to  enact  §$ 26,  which  expanded  the  protections
provided  by  the  Constitution  and  is  in  accord  with
this  Court’s  precedent.  The  Michigan  voters  simply
moved  up  the  end  date  for  preferential  treatment
based  on  race  and  ethnicity  in  public  university
admissions  decisions,  which  does  not  violate  the
Constitution.  As  a  result,  allowing  the  Sixth  Circuit’s
decision  to  stand  would  disrupt  this  Court’s  precedent
in  Grutter.

Moreover,  allowing  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  ruling  to
stand  may  cause  devastating  consequences  for  public
contracting.  Under  this  Court’s  holding  in  Adarand,
public  contractors  have  a  mght  to  expect  consistency
amongst  government  contracting  decisions.  515  U.S.
at  230.  Contractors  are  also  entitled  to  be  judged
against  other  contractors  on  an  even  playing  field.
Northeastern  Fla.  Chapter,  Associated  General  Con-
tractors  of America  v.  City  of Jacksonville,  Fla.,  508
U.S.  656,  667  (1993)  (“City  of  Jacksonville”).  The

7

Sixth  Circuit’s  erroneous  interpretation  of the  Equal
Protection  Clause  invites  future  challenges  regarding
public  contracting  and  public  employment  and  will
undoubtedly  create  confusion  with  respect  to  the
permissible  use  of  race  and  ethnicity  in  public  con-
tracting  and  public  employment.  Therefore,  MSLF
urges  this  Court  to  reverse  the  Sixth’s  Circuit  deci-
sion.

ARGUMENT

I.

THE  EQUAL  PROTECTION  CLAUSE
REQUIRES  THIS  COURT  TO  REVERSE
THE  SIXTH  CIRCUIT.

The  Equal  Protection  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  provides  that  “[njo  State  shall  ...  deny
to  any  person  within  its jurisdiction  the  equal  protec-
tion  of  the  laws.”  U.S.  Const.  amend.  XIV,  § 1.  The
guarantee  of equal  protection  found  in  both  the  Fifth
and  the  Fourteenth  Amendments  requires  State  and
federal  governments  to  provide  equal  protection  of
the  law  to  all  persons,  regardless  of  race  or  gender.
Schweiker  v.  Wilson,  450  U.S.  221,  226  n.6  (1981)
(“the  Fifth  Amendment  imposes  on  the  Federal  Gov-
ernment  the  same  standard  required  of state  legisla-
tion  by the  Equal  Protection  Clause  of the  Fourteenth
Amendment”);  Weinberger  v.  Salfi,  422  U.S.  749,  768-
70  (1975).

If a  law  discriminates  based  upon  an  individual’s
race,  the  Court  will  strike  it down  unless  it is narrowly

8

tailored  to  serve  a  compelling  government  interest
(the  “strict  scrutiny  test”).  Plyler  v.  Doe,  457  U.S.  202,
216-17  (1982);  see  also  Fisher,  570  U.S.  ___,  2013  WL
3155220  at  *8  (Applying  strict  scrutiny  to  race-based
public  uaiversity  admissions  programs).  Additionally,
if a  law  discriminates  against  an  individual  without
focusing  on  race  or  ethnicity,  courts  “will  not  overturn
such  a  [law|  unless  the  varying  treatment  of different
groups  or  persons  is  so  unrelated  to  the  achievement
of  any  combination  of  legitimate  purposes  that  [the
court]  can  only  conclude  that  the  [people’s}  actions
were  irrational”  (the  “rational  basis  test”).  Gregory  v.
Ashcroft,  501  U.S.  452,  470  (1991).

It  has  long  been  established  that  “distinctions
between  citizens  solely  because  of  their  [race  or]
ancestry  are  by  their  very  nature  odious  to  a  free
are  founded  upon  the
people  whose  institutions
doctrine  of  equality.”  Hirabayashi  v.  United  States,
320  U.S.  81,  100  (1943).  Indeed,  “racial  characteris-
...  seldom  provide  a  relevant  basis  for  disparate
tics
treatment,  and  ...  classifications  based  on  race  are
potentially  ...  harmful  to  the  entire  body  politic.”
City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Company,  488  U.S.
469,  505  (1989)  (“Croson”).  Furthermore,  “the  equal
protection  principle,”  “purchased  at  the  price  of
immeasurable  human  suffering,”  reflects  “our  Na-
tions  understanding  that  such  classifications  ulti-
mately  have  a  destructive  impact  on  the  individual
and  society.”  Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  240  (Thomas,  J.
concurring).  Indeed,  discrimination  based  on  race  is
“illegal,  immoral,  unconstitutional,  inherently  wrong,

9

and  destructive  of a  democratic  society.”  Croson.  488
U.S.  at  521  (Scalia,  J.  concurring).  Finally,  “(ijt  would
be  paradoxical  to  conclude  that  by adopting  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of the  Fourteenth  Amendment,  the
voters  of the  State  thereby  had  violated  it.”  Crawford
vu.  Board  of Education  of City  of Los  Angeles,  458  U.S.
527,  535  (1982).

The  Equal  Protection  Clause,  by  its  own  terms,
must  be  applied  to  individuals  uniformly.  As  this
Court  has  provided,  allowing  the  government  to
classify  individuals  differently  based  on  race,  ethnici-
ty,  or  national  origin  can  lead  to  harmful  consequenc-
es  for  society.  See  Regents  of Univ.  of Cal.  v.  Bakke,
438  U.S.  265,  307  (1978)  (“Preferring  members  of any
one  group  for  no  reason  other  than  race  or  ethnic
origin  is  discrimination  for  its  own  sake.  This  the
Constitution  forbids.”)  (citing  Loving  v.  Virginia,  388
U.S.  1,  11  (1967);  McLaughlin  v.  Florida,  379  U.S.
184,  196  (2007);  Brown  v.  Board  of Education,  347
U.S.  483  (1954));  see  also  Hirabayashi,  320  U.S.  at
100  (“|DJistinctions  between  citizens  solely  because  of
their  [race  or]  ancestry  are  by  their  very  nature
odious  to  a  free  people.”).  To  reiterate  their  commit-
ment  to  equality  and  to  avoid  the  “illegal,  immoral,
unconstitutional,  inherently  wrong  and  destructive”
results  that  can  stem  from  discrimination,  the  major-
ity  of voters  in  the  State  of Michigan  voted  to  enact
§ 26.  Id.  The  Michigan  Constitution  has  contained  an
equal  protection  guarantee  since  its  amendment  in
1963.  See  Mich.  Const.  art.  I, § 2.  This  section  states
that  “[njo  person  shall  be  denied  the  equal  protection

10

of  the  laws;  nor  shall  any  person  be  denied  the  en-
joyment  of  his  civil  or  political  rights  or  be  discrimi-
nated  against  in  the  exercise  thereof  because  of
religion,  race,  color  or  national  origin.  The  legislature
shall  implement  this  section  by  appropriate  legisla-
tion.”  Jd.  Indeed,  when  the  voters  approved  § 26,  they
were  carrying  out  the  intentions  of the  State  Consti-
tution’s  drafters  by implementing  appropriate  legisla-
tion,  reaffirming  the  State’s  commitment  to  equal
protection.  The  voters  were  also  reaffirming  the
Equal  Protection  guarantee  found  in  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  and  as  this  Court  pointed  out  in  Craw-
ford,  it  would  be  paradoxical  to  hold  that  the  voters
had  violated  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  by  adding
its  principles  to  their  State  Constitution.

Although  this  Court  has  allowed  public  universi-
ties  to  continue  considering  race  in  admissions  appli-
cations  in  certain  limited  circumstances,  it has  never
held  that  race-based  admissions  programs  are  neces-
sary  to  guarantee  equal  protection  under  the  Consti-
tution.  Compare  Grutter,  539  U.S.  at  335  (considering
race  as  one  factor  during  a  holistic  review  of  each
application  is  sufficiently  narrowly  tailored  to  pass
strict  scrutiny  analysis),  with  Gratz  v.  Bollinger,  539
U.S.  244,  273-75  (2003)  (automatically  awarding
points  to  minority  applicants  violates  the  Equal
Protection  Clause);  Bakke,  438  U.S.  265  (holding  that
use  of  quotas  in  university  admissions  process  vio-
lates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause);  see  also  Fisher,
570  U.S.  ___,  2013  WL  3155220  at  *9  (“{I|t  remains  at
all  times  the  University’s  obligation  to  demonstrate,

1]

and  the  Judiciary’s  obligation  to  determine,  that  the
admissions  processes  ensure  that  each  applicant  is
evaluated  as  an  individual  and  not  in  a  way  that
makes  an  applicant’s  race  or  ethnicity  the  defining
feature  of his  or  her  application.”)  (internal  citations
omitted).  Because  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  “does
not  require  what  it  barely  permits,”  the  Sixth  Cir-
cuit’s  holding  is erroneous.  Wilson,  122  F.3d  at  709.

In  Grutter,  this  Court  was  asked  to  explore  the
constitutionality  of considering  race  in  public  univer-
sity  admissions.  539  U.S.  at  306.  This  Court  applied
the  strict  scrutiny  analysis  and  upheld  the  University
of  Michigan  Law  School's  admissions  program  be-
cause  it  was  appropriately  narrowly  tailored.  Jd.  at
334.  Although  the  Law  School  used  a  “race-conscious
admissions  program,”  it did  not  utilize  a  quota  system
and  considered  race  and  ethnicity  in  a  flexible  man-
ner.  Id.  at  336-37.  Importantly,  this  Court  held  that  it
“expect[s}  that  25  years  from  now,  the  use  of  racial
preferences  will  no  longer  be  necessary  to  further  the
interest  approved  today.”  /d.  at  343.  This  Court  also
provided  that  the  nation  continues  to  aspire  to  the
goal  of  the  Equal  Protection  Clause,  which  is  to
embody  “a  political  system  in  which  race  no  longer
matters.”  Shaw  v.  Reno,  509  U.S.  630,  657  (1993).

Although  this  Court  declined  to  strike  down  the
admissions  program  challenged  in  Grutter  its  opinion
provided  that  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  demands
that  affirmative  action  programs  play  a  limited  role
in  public  university  admissions  decisions.  Grutter  was
decided  in  2003,  meaning  that  the  Court  imagined

12

that  by  2028  race-based  admissions  programs  wouid
no  longer  be  necessary.  Grutter,  539  U.S.  at  343.
Following  this  Court’s  guidance,  the  majority  of
voters  in  Michigan  recognized  that  race-based  admis-
sions  programs  must  have  an  end  date.  These  voters
responded  to  this  Court’s  direction  and  decided  for
themselves  that  they  need  not  wait  until  2028  to  end
race  conscious  admissions  programs.  Indeed,  the
voters  have  exercised  their  rights  and  taken  the  steps
required  by  the  democratic  process  to  show  that  the
government  should  stop  giving  preferential  treatment
based  upon  race,  ethnicity,  gender  and  national  origin
in  public  education,  public  employment,  and  public
contracting.  In  fact,  the  actions  of the  voters  in  Mich-
igan  affirm  the  State’s  commitment  to  equal  protec-
tion  under  the  iaws  of  the  State  and  carry  forward
the  goal  set  forth  in  Shaw  —  the  goal  of  progressing
toward  a  “political  system  in  which  race  no  longer
matters.”  Shaw,  509  U.S.  at  657.  The  Sixth  Circuit’s
opinion  cannot  stand  under  the  Equal  Protection
Clause.  Striking  down  § 26  would  undermine  the
sanctity  of the  democratic  voting  system  and  after  all,
a  State  “is  free  as  a  matter  of its  own  law”  to  treat
citizens  of  all  races  and  nationalities  equally.  Oregon
v.  Hass,  420  U.S.  714,  718  (1975).

More  recently,  in  Fisher,  this  Court  again  consid-
ered  the  constraints  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause  in
relation  to  public  university  admissions  programs.
570  U.S.  ___,  2013  WL  3155220.  Petitioner  in  Fisher
claimed  that  the  University  of Texas  at  Austin’s  race-
based  admissions  program  violated  the  Equal  Protection

13

Clause.  Id.  at  *5.  Without  overruling  Grutter,  this
Court  reiterated  that  if a  public  university  chooses  to
consider  race  as  a  factor  when  considering  admissions
applications,  it  must  demonstrate  that  “each  appli-
cant  is  evaluated  as  an  individual  and  not  in  a  way
that  makes  an  applicant's  race  or  ethnicity  the  defin-
ing feature  of his  or  her  application.”  /d.  at  *7  (citing
Grutter,  539  U.S.  at  337).  Ultimately,  this  Court
remanded  the  case  back  to  the  Fifth  Circuit  to  con-
sider  whether  the  University  of  Texas  at  Austin’s
race-based  admissions  policy  passes  a  strict  scrutiny
analysis;  however,  Justice  Thomas  wrote  a  separate
concurring  opinion  expressing  his  belief  that  “a
State’s  use  of  race  in  higher  education  admissions
decisions  is  categorically  prohibited  by  the  Equal
Protection  Clause.”  Jd.  at  “11.

In  his  concurrence,  Justice  Thomas  characterizes
Grutter  as  a  “radical  departure  from  |Supreme  Court]
strict-scrutiny  precedents.”  /d.  at  *13.  Justice  Thom-
as  argues  that  this  Court  misapplied  its  strict-
scrutiny  analysis  in  Grutter  by  deferring  to  the  Uni-
versity’s  determination  that  the  benefits  that  result
from  diversity  amongst  the  student  body  were  “suffi-
ciently  compelling  to justify  racial  discrimination.”  Jd.
Justice  Thomas’s  concurrence  makes  clear  that  “the
pursuit  of  diversity  as  an  end  is  nothing  more  than
impermissible  ‘racial  balancing.’”  7d.  at  *14  (citing
Grutter,  539  U.S.  at  329-30).  Furthermore,  there  is  no
“principled
University’s
assertion  that  diversity  yields  educational  benefits
and  the  segregationists’  assertion  that  segregation

distinction

between

the

14

yielded  those  same  benefits.”  Jd.  at  *18.  As  such,
Justice  Thomas  maintains  that  no  compelling  inter-
est  can  justify  racial  discrimination  in  the  university
the
admissions
“Equal  Protection  Clause  strips  States  of all  authority
to  use  race  as  a  factor  in  providing  education,”  race-
based  admissions  programs  violate  the  Constitution.

process

and,

Because  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  does  not
guarantee  an  individual  the  right  to  preferential
treatment,  this  Court  should  reverse  the  Sixth  Cir-
cuit’s  decision  and  uphold § 26.

Il.  THE  NINTH  CIRCUIT  CORRECTLY  HELD
THAT  PROHIBITING  RACE-BASED  AD-
MISSIONS  PROGRAMS  IS  CONSTITU-
TIONAL.

Voters  in  the  State  of  California  passed  Proposi-
tion  209  (“Prop.  209”)  on  November  5,  1996.  Wilson,
122  F.2d  692  (9th  Cir.  1997).  Prop.  209  is  textually
identical  to  § 26.  Compare  Cal.  Const.  art.  I, § 31(a),
with  Mich.  Const.  art.  I, § 26.  Despite  multiple  chal-
lenges  in  both  State  court  and  federal  court,  Prop.
209  has  remained  a  part  of California’s  Constitution.
The  Ninth  Circuit  considered  Prop.  209  in  Wilson,  122
F.2d  692,  and  in  Brown,  674  F.3d  1128,  and  upheld  it
in  both  cases.  In  Wilson,  the  Ninth  Circuit  held  “as  a
matter  of  law,  Proposition  209  does  not  violate  the
United  States  Constitution.”  122  F.2d  at  711.  The
court  in  Wilson  considered  substantially  the  same
arguments  as  the  Sixth  Circuit  in  the  instant  case

15

and  concluded  that  “|w}then  the  government  prefers
individuals  on  account  of  their  race  or  gender,  it
correspondingly  disadvantages  individuals  who  fortu-
itously  belong  to  another  race  or  to  the  other  gender.”
Wilson,  122  F.3d  at  702.  Furthermore,  the  Ninth
Circuit  correctly  held  that  legislation  prohibiting  “the
State  from  classifying  individuals  by race  or  gender  a
fortiori  does  not  classify  individuals  by race  or  gender.
Proposition  209’s  ban  on  race  and  gender  preferences,
as  a  matter  of  law  and  logic,  does  not  violate  the
Equal  Protection  Clause  in  any  conventional  sense.”
Id.  Finally,  the  Ninth  Circuit  held  that  “[wJhile  the
Constitution  protects  against  obstructions  to  equal
treatment,
to  preferential
treatment  by  its  own  terms.”  Jd.  at  708.  Thus,  the
Ninth  Circuit  found  no  violations  of the  Equal  Protec-
tion  Clause  under  the  political-structure  analysis.  Id.
at  704-09.  Later  in  Brown,  a  second  group  of  plain-
tiffs  challenged  Prop.  209,  but  the  Ninth  Circuit
again  upheld  the  constitutionality  of the  amendment.
674  F.3d  at  1135.

obstructions

it  erects

The  Ninth  Circuit’s  reasoning  for  upholding  Prop.
209  is equally  applicable  to  § 26.  If this  Court  upholds
the  Sixth  Circuit’s  ruling,  it  will  essentially  abrogate
Prop.  209,  which  has  lawfully  existed  for  the  past
sixteen  years.  While  the  United  States  Constitution
expressly
unequal
application  of  the  law,  it  certainly  does  not  require
that  States  grant  preferential  treatment  to  minori-
ties.  See  Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  240  (Thomas,  J.  con-
curring)  (“Government  cannot  make  us  equal;  it  can

discrimination

prohibits

and

16

only  recognize,  respect,  and  protect  us  as  equal  before
the  law.”).  Moreover,  merely  reiterating  the  federal
Constitution  within  a  State  Constitution  to  extend
Constitutional  protections  is  permissible.  See  Prune-
Yard  Shopping  Center,  447  U.S.  at  81  (Acknowledging
a  State’s  “sovereign  right  to  adopt  in  its  own  Consti-
tution  individual  liberties  more  expansive  than  those
conferred  by  the  Federal  Constitution.”).  As  a  result,
this  Court  should  reverse  the  Sixth  Circuit  and  adopt
the  superior  Ninth  Circuit  reasoning.

lil,  SECTION  26  DOES  NOT  VIOLATE  THE
POLITICAL  RESTRUCTURING  DOCTRINE,

Legislation  violates  the  political  restructuring
doctrine  if  it:  (1)  has  a  racial  focus  that  targets  a
policy  or  program  that  “inures  primarily  to  the  bene-
fit  of  the  minority’;  Seattle  School  District,  458  U.S.
at  485,  and  (2)  reallocates  political  power  or  reorders
the  decision  making  process  in  a  way  that  places
a  minority  group’s  ability  to
special  burdens  on
achieve  its  goals  through  that  process.  See  Hunter,
393  U.S.  at  391.  In  the  instant  case,  the  Sixth  Circuit
misinterpreted  this  Court’s  reasoning  in  both  Hunter
and  Seattle  School  District  to  strike  down  § 26.

In  Hunter,  the  city  of Akron,  Ohio  amended  its
city  charter  to  prevent  the  city’s  fair  housing  ordi-
nance  from  being  applied  until  a  majority  of the  city’s
voters  voted  to  approve  the  amendment.  393  U.S.  at
337.  Despite  the  fact  that  the  city  council  had  enacted
a  fair  housing  ordinance  in  1964,  the  city  argued  that

17

the  ordinance  was  not  effective  because  it  had  not  yet
been  enacted  by  a  majority  of  the  city’s  voters.  /d.
This  Court  held  that  Akron’s  amendment  to  its  city
charter  violated  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  because
it  clearly  “discriminated  against  minorities,  and
constitute{d]  a  real,  substantial,  and  invidious  denial
of the  equal  protection  of the  laws.”  Jd.  at  561-62.

In  Seattle  School  District,  the  State  approved  an
that  banned  busing  programs  between
iniliative
school  districts  by  prohibiting  school  boards  from
requiring  students  to  attend  schools  other  than  the
public  school  geographically  nearest  to  the  student’s
residence.  458  U.S.  at  462.  Although  the  initiative
contained  a  number  of  broad  exceptions,  this  Court
struck  down  the  ordinance  and  found  that  it  violated
the  equal  protection  guarantees  within  the  Constitu-
tion  by  imposing  “substantial  and  unique  burdens  on
racial  minorities.”  Jd.  at  470.  This  Court  found  that
the  racial  focus  of the  legislation  “triggeried|  applica-
tion  of  the  Hunter  doctrine  and  unfairly  burdened
minorities.”  /d.  at  474.

In  this  case,  the  Sixth  Circuit  found  that  § 26
placed  special  burdens  on  minorities,  making  it  more
difficult  for  minorities  to  “achieve  beneficial  legisla-
tion.”  CDAA  IJ],  701  F.3d  at  474.  In  applying  a  two-
part  test  derived  from  Hunter  and  Seattle  School
District,  the  Sixth  Circuit  found  that:  (1)  programs
targeting  classroom  diversity  have  a  racial  focus;
and  (2)  the  “comparative  structural  burden”  placed
upon  minorities  by  § 26  impermissibly  reorders  the
political  process.  Jd.  at  478,  484.  The  Sixth  Circuit’s

18

analysis  is flawed  because  it necessarily  assumes  that
minorities  have  the  right  to  preferential  treatment  in
the  university  admissions  program.  Neither  Hunter
nor  Seattle  School  District  ever  considered  a  minori-
ty’s  right  to  preferential  treatment.  Instead,  both
Hunter,  393  U.S.  at  562,  and  Seattle  School  District,
458  U.S.  at  487,  considered  only  the  Constitution's
guarantee  of  equal  protection  to  all  citizens.  Despite
this  Court's  holding  in  Grutter,  which  upheld  the
consideration  of  race,  when  considered  as  part  of  a
holistic  review  of  applicants  to  the  University  of
Michigan  Law  School,  539  U.S.  at  309,  such  a  holistic
review  is  neither  required  nor  guaranteed  by  the
Equal!  Protection  Clause.  It  has  long  been  held  that
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  “is  not  violated  by  the
mere  repeal  of race-related  legislation  or  policies  that
were  not  required  by the  Federal  Constitution  in  the
first  place.”  CDAA  II,  701  F.3d  at  511  (Sutton,  J.
dissenting)  (citing  Crawford,  458  U.S.  at  538-39).
Because  the  Equal  Protection  Clause  does  not  guar-
antee  racial  minorities  any  right  to  preferential
treatment,  § 26  cannot  violate  the  political  restruc-
turing  doctrine,  and  as  such,  the  Sixth  Circuit's
ruling  must  be  reversed.

19

IV.  UPHOLDING  THE

CIRCUIT’S
DECISION  WOULD  HAVE  DEVASTATING
CONSEQUENCES  ON
CON-
TRACTING.

PUBLIC

SIXTH

A.  Allowing  The  Sixth  Circuit’s  Erroneous
Ruling  To  Stand  Invites  Future  Litiga-
tion  Regarding  Public  Contracting.

The  mapjority’s  holding  in  the  instant  case  is
“limited  to  ‘racial  minonties’  and  our  court’s  declara-
tion  ‘[fjinding  those  provisions  of  [§ 26]  affecting
Michigan's  public  colleges  and  universities  unconsti-
tutional...  .’”  CDAA  I/,  701  F.3d  at  514  (Griffin,  J.
dissenting).  Although  the  language  of  § 26  applies  to
public  education,  public  contracting  and  public  em-
ployment,  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  holding  1s  limited  to  the
application  of  § 26  to  public  university  admissions
decisions.  The  present  action  began  in  November
2006.  CDAA  II,  701  F.3d  at  471.  Now,  over  six  years
later,  litigation  continues.  Affirming  the  Sixth  Cir-
cuit’s  erroneous  ruling  may  open  the  door  to  future
lawsuits  challenging  § 26  as  it  pertains  to  public
contracting  or  public  employment.  Litigation  of  this
sort  would  be  duplicative,  time  consuming,  and  costly.
Forcing  the  federal  judicial  system  to  utilize
its
limited  resources  to  re-hear  such  a  substantially
similar  case  would  be  burdensome  and  inefficient.
The  principles  of judicial  efficiency  are  not  served  by
ruling.  Therefore,  this  Court
the  Sixth  Circuit’s
should  emphatically  reverse  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  deci-
sion  and  uphold  the  constitutionality  of § 26.

20

B.  Allowing  The  Sixth  Circuit’s  Ruling  To
Stand  Would  Conflict  With  Other  Rul-
ings  Regarding  Preferential
Treat-
ment  In  Public  Contracting.

This  Court  has  held  that  a  racial  classification  is
“a  line  drawn  on  the  basis  of  race  and|/or|  ethnic
status,”  and  that  the  Constitution’s  guarantee  of
equal  protection  is  violated  if  persons  of  different
races  “are  not  accorded  the  same  protection.”  Bakke,
438  U.S.  at  289.  Indeed,  the  Constitution  forbids
States  from  discriminating  against  citizens  based  on
their  race.  Bolling  v.  Sharpe,  347  U.S.  497,  499  (1954)
(Holding  “that  the  Constitution  of the  United  States,
in  its  present  form.  forbids,  so  far  as  civil  and  political
rights  are  concerned,  discrimination  by  the  general
government,  or  by  the  States,  against  any  citizen
because  of  his  race.”);  see  Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  230
(Finding  that  an  “individual  suffers  an  injury  when
he  or  she  is  disadvantaged  by  the  government  be-
cause  of  his  or  her  race,  whatever  that  race  may  be.
This  Court  clearly  stated  that  principle  in  Croson.”
(citing  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.,  488  U.S.
469,  493-94;  520-510)).

Both  “the  Fifth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments
protect  persons,  not  groups.”  Adarand,  515  U.S.  at
201;  see  also  Missouri  v.  Jenkins,  515  U.S.  70,  120-21
(1995)  (Thomas,  J.  concurring)  (“At  the  heart  of  ...
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  lies  the  principle  that
the  government  must  treat  citizens  as  individuals,

and  not  as  members  of  racial,  ethnic,  or  religious
eroups.”).  The  issue  of  consistency  is  frequently

21

raised  in  regard  to  Equal  Protection  claims  and  the
principle  recognizes  “that  any  individual  suffers  an
injury  when  he  or  she  is  disadvantaged  by  the  gov-
ernment  because  of his  or  her  race.”  Wilson,  122  F.2d
at  702,  (citing  Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  230).  “The  prin-
ciple  of consistency  simply  means  that  whenever  the
government  treats  any  person  unequally  because  of
his  or  her  race,  that  person  has  suffered  an  injury
that  falls  squarely  within  the  language  and  spirit  of
the  Constitution’s  guarantee  of  equa!  protection.”
Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  230.  An  injury  is  also  suffered
when  a  “discriminatory  classification  prevent(s]  the
plaintiff  from  competing  on  equal  footing.”  City  of
Jacksonville,  508  U.S.  at  667.

Regardless  of  which  group  is  benefited  by  a  law,
such  a  benefit  is  impermissible  under  the  Equal
Protection  Clause.  Therefore,  § 26  should  be  upheld
in  all  regards  because  it  protects  individuals  and
ensures  equal  treatment  of  all  the  State’s  citizens.
Upholding  the  present  challenge  to  § 26  would  indi-
cate  that  individuals  engaged  in  applying  for  univer-
sity  admissions  somehow  have  different  rights  than
individuals  applying  for  public  employment  or  bid-
ding  for  a  public  contract.  Contractors  bidding  for
government  jobs  are  protected  as  individuals  under
the  federal  Constitution  and  as  such:  (1)  they  are
entitled  to  be  treated  consistently  by the  government,
Adarand,  515  U.S.  at  230;  and  (2)  they  are  entitled  to
compete  on  equal  footing  with  other  contractors.  City
of Jacksonville,  508  U.S.  at  667.  Section  26  ensures
that  contractors  in  the  State  of Michigan  will  be treated

22

fairly  and  each  considered  based  on  individual  merits,
not  at  an  advantage  or  disadvantage  because  of racial
or  ethnic  characteristics.

of  offering

In  Adarand,  a  sub-contractor  challenged  the
federal  government’s
financial
policy
incentives  to  general  contractors  who  chose  to  employ
“socially  and  economically  disadvantaged  individu-
als.”  515  U.S.  at  204.  This  Court  held  that  strict
scrutiny  is  the  appropriate  standard  of  review  to
apply  whenever  racial  classifications  are  analyzed  by
a  court,  no  matter  whether  they  were  imposed  by the
federal,  State,  or  local  government.  /d.  at  227.  Al-
though  this  Court  remanded  the  case  to  the  lower
court  for  a  final  decision,  it  guided  the  lower  court’s
decision  by  providing  that  the  Equal  Protection
Clause  does  not  permit  the  government  to  treat
individuals  differently  under  the  law  or  disadvantage
individuals  based  upon  their  race  or  ethnicity.  /d.  at
230.

Bearing  this  important  holding  in  mind,  a  future
challenge  to  § 26’s  protection  of contractors  would  not
be  trivial.  In  fact,  if the  Sixth  Circuit’s  ruling  in  the
Instant  case  were  upheld,  a  future  challenge  to  § 26
could  threaten  the  constitutional  rights  of contractors
bidding  and  working  in  the  State  of  Michigan  and
would  also  contradict  this  Court’s  rulings  regarding
the  correct  application  of the  Equal  Protection  Clause
in  many  public  contracting  cases.  Adarand,  515
U.S.  200;  Croson,  488  U.S.  469;  City  of Jacksonville,
508  U.S.  656.  Just  as  Respondents  in  the  instant
action  argued  that  § 26  unfairly  disadvantages  racial

23

The

bids.

Fourteenth

minorities  in  the  public  university  admissions  pro-
cess,  minority  contractors  may  claim  they  too  are
disadvantaged  by  the  application  of  § 26  to  public
Amendment's
contracting
protections  should  not  be  distinguished  between
student  and  contractor,  or  between  student  and
employee.  The  Fourteenth  Amendment  necessarily
guarantees  that  the  law  will  treat  all  individuals
equally.  Adarand,  515  US.  at  229-30  (“|Wjhenever
the  government  treats  any  person  unequally  because
of his  or  her  race,  that  person  has  suffered  an  injury
that  falls  squarely  within  the  language  and  spirit  of
the  Constitution’s  guarantee  of equal  protection.”);  see
also  id.  at  240  (Thomas,  J.  concurring)  (“Government
cannot  make  us  equal;  it  can  only  recognize,  respect,
and  protect  us  as  equal  before  the  law.”).  Because  of
the  potentially  devastating  effects  a  challenge  to
§ 26’s  applicability  to  public  contracting  may  have,
and  to  maintain  consistency  with  previous  public
contracting  rulings,  this  Court  should  reverse  the
Sixth  Circuit’s  erroneous  ruling.

«

24

CONCLUSION

For  the  foregoing  reasons,  this  Court  should
reverse  the  Sixth  Circuit’s  ruling  and  emphatically
rule  that  § 26  is  constitutional.

INTEREST  OF  AMICI  CURIAE

This  brief amicus  curiae  is submitted  on  behalf  of
the  National  Education  Association  (“NEA”);  the
Michigan  Education  Association  (“MEA”),  an  affiliate
of  NEA;  and  the  Service  Employees  International
Union  (“SEIU”).

NEA  is  a  nationwide  employee  organization  with
approximately  three  million  members,  the  vast
majority  of  whom  serve  as  educators  and  education
support  professionals  in our  nation’s  public  schools,
colleges,  and  universities.  MEA  is  the  largest  single
public  employee  union  in  Michigan,  and  the  third
largest  education  association  in  the  United  States,
representing  more  than  140,000  K-12  teachers,  higher
education  faculty,  professional  education  staff,  edu-
cational  support  staff,  and  retired  school  employees
throughout  the  State  of  Michigan.  SEIU  represents
2.1  million  men  and  women  working  in  health  care,
property  services,  and  public  services,  including  edu-
cators  and  support  personnel  in  public  schools,  uni-
versities,  and  early  education  settings.

Amici  are  dedicated  to  furthering  the  goals  and
employment  interests  of their  members,  and  believe
that  every  citizen  has  the  absolute  right  to  partici-
pate—free  of  racially  motivated  distortions  or  bur-
dens—in  the  political  processes  used  to  shape  our
nation’s  public  educational  institutions.  Amici  also

‘Letters  of  consent  from  all  parties  are  on  file  with  the
Clerk.  No counsel  for a party  authored  this  brief  in whole  or  in
part,  and  no  person  or  entity  other  than  amici  curiae  made  a
monetary  contribution  to  the  preparation  or  submission  of the
brief.

2

have  a  deep  and  abiding  commitment  to equal  oppor-
tunity  in  all  aspects  of  our  society  and  believe  that
racial  integration  and  ethnic  diversity  in  our  institu-
tions  of  public  education  serve  legitimate  and  com-
pelling  societal  interests.

SUMMARY  OF  ARGUMENT

Proposal  2  creates  a  distinct  and  more  onerous
political  process  for  those  advocating  for  constitu-
tionally  permissible  race-conscious  admissions  poli-
cies  at  public  universities  in  Michigan.  While  those
seeking  different,  non-race-based  changes  to  univer-
sity  admissions  policies—including  legacy  prefer-
ences  or  geographic  set-asides—may  use  a variety  of
political  channels  to  pursue  their  goals,  citizens  who
propose  that  public  universities  consciously  promote
the  benefits  of  a  racially  diverse  student  body
through  the  kinds  of programs  this  Court  has  held  to
be  constitutionally  permissible  have  but  one  option
that  is  arduous,  time  consuming,  and  prohibitively
expensive:  amending  the  Michigan  Constitution.  As
this  Court  has  recognized  in prior  cases,  this  kind  of
racially  motivated  restructuring  and  distortion  of the
political  process  violates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause
of the  Fourteenth  Amendment.

Prior  to  the  enactment  of  Proposition  2,  there
existed  a  well-functioning,  longstanding  political
process  for  determining  the  policies  and  objectives
that  would  be  pursued  by  public  universities  in
Michigan,  including  those  policies  that  affected
admission  criteria  for prospective  students.  As  is the
case  in  many  states,  Michigan  makes  the  governing
bodies  of  its  public  universities  directly  politically
accountable  to  its  citizenry.  These  bodies  are  en-

3

trusted  to  make  judgments  about  educational  policy
that  go to the  heart  of a public  education  institution’s
mission.

Whether  to  consider  race  as  a constitutionally  per-
missible  factor  in  admission  policies  is precisely  the
kind  of  educaticnal  policy  judgment  that  has  tradi-
tionally  been  exercised  by these  politically  account-
able  bodies.  As  part  of their  mission  to  provide  stu-
dents  with  the  skills  and  knowledge  necessary  to
realize  their  full  potential,  as  well  as  to  instill  in them
the  values  on  which  our  society  rests,  educational
institutions  may—consistent  with  the  Fourteenth
Amendment—seek  to obtain  the educational  benefits
that  flow  from  an  ethnically  diverse  student  body.
Those  benefits  include  promoting  cross-racial  under-
standing,  fostering  more  enlightening  classroom  dis-
cussion,  promoting  better  learning  outcomes,  and
preparing  students  for  an  increasingly  diverse  work-
force  and  society.

It  is  clear  that  admissions  policies  in  Michigan's
public  universities  have  been  and  will—with  the
exception  the  subjects  removed  by  Proposition  2—
continue  to be part of a political  process.  But with  par-
ticular  regard  to the  key issue  of whether  race  may  be
considered  as  a  constitutionally-permissible  factor  in
admissions  decisions,  Proposal  2  has  completely
restructured  that process  by prohibiting  those  who  are
in  favor  of  such  policies  from  lobbying  elected  offi-
cials  for changes  that they desire.  Proposal  2 therefore
imposes  a  special,  racially  motivated  burden  on
minorities  by creating  two  different  political  process-
es  for  advocating  for  changes  in  admissions  criteria.
Based  on  the  precedent  laid  down  by  this  Court,

dq

Proposal  2  must  be  held  to  violate  the  Fourteenth
Amendment,  for  the  State  has  neither  offered  a  com-
pelling  justification  in  support  of  Proposition  2  nor
endeavored  to  argue  that  the  measure  is narrowly  tai-
lored  to  advance  such a justification.

ARGUMENT

Proposal  2  violates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause
insofar  as  it purports  to keep one  group—those  advo-
cating  for  constitutionally  permissible  race-con-
scious  admissions  policies  at  public  universities  in
Michigan—from  using  the  same  multiple  political
channels  available  to  others  who  seek  different,  non-
race-based  changes  to  those  same  admissions  poli-
cies.
Although  a  majority  may  generally  defeat  a
minority’s  political  proposals  simply  because  it is the
majority,  Proposition  2 falls  into  the  limited  category
of  government  action  that  the  Equal  Protection
Clause  forbids  absent  a  showing  that  the  action  is
narrowly  tailored  to  advance  a  compelling  state
interest.  Because  race  is the  predominant  factor  that
explains  Proposition  2’s  creation  of  a  distinct  and
more  onerous  political  process,  Proposition  2  is  a
governmental  action  that  creates  a  suspect  racial
classification  subject  to  strict  scrutiny.  And  because
the  state  has  not  suggested—much  less  proven—that
Proposition  2  survives  the  rigors  of  strict  scrutiny,
the  enactment  must  be  held  unconstitutional.

A.  A State’s  Racial  Gerrymander  of an  Existing
Political  Process  is  Subject  to  Strict
Scrutiny  Under  the  Equal  Protection  Clause

This  Court’s  cases  make  clear  that  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  protects  the  ordinary  mechanisms

5

of  governmental  decisionmaking  against  intentional
efforts  to  rig  or  skew  the  political  process  against
particular  policies  simply  because  of their  racial  sub-
ject  matter.

This  principle  was  first  clearly  enunciated  in
Hunter  v.  Erickson,  393  U.S.  385  (1969),  when  this
Court  struck  down  a  charter  amendment  enacted  by
a  white  majority  in  Akron,  Ohio  that  sought  to  sus-
tain  de facto  segregation  by  requiring  that  fair  hous-
ing  ordinances  be  subject  to  a  referendum  vote.  In
striking  down  the  amendment,  this  Court  declared
that  the  “State  may  no  more  disadvantage  any  partic-
ular  group  by making  it more  difficult  to  enact  legis-
lation  in  its  behalf  than  it  may  dilute  any  person’s
vote  or  give  any  group  a  smaller  representation  than
another  of comparable  size.”  Id.  at  393.  The  rights
protected  by Hunter  were  defined  broadly  to  include
the  right  of a racial  minority  to  equal  political  proce-
dures  when  it  sought  “to  enact  legislation  in  its
behalf.”  Jd.

This

Court

reaffirmed

Hunter's  _ political-
restructuring  rationale  in  Washington  v.  Seattle
School  District  No.  1, 458  U.S.  457  (1982),  when  it
struck  down  Initiative  350,  a  Washington  state
constitutional  amendment  that  attempted  to  main-
tain  de  facto  segregation  of  Seattle’s  schools  by
giving  local  school  boards  the  power  to  use  busing
for  any  purpose  except  racial  integration.  Relying  on
Hunter,  this  Court  held  that  the  amendment  violated
the  Equal  Protection  Clause  because  it  imposed  an
unequal  political  process  on  those  seeking  to  end
school  segregation.  As  this  Court  noted,  “the  practi-
cal  effect  of Initiative  350  is to  work  a reallocation  of

6

power  of the  kind  condemned  in Hunter.  The  initia-
tive  removes  the  authority  to  address  a  racial  prob-
lem—and  only  a  racial  problem—from  the  existing
decisionmaking  body,  in  such  a  way  as  to  burden
Although  Seattle
minority  interests.”  /d.  at  474.
explicitly  stated  that  “the  political  majority  may  gen-
erally  restructure  the  political  process  to place obsta-
cles  in  the  path  of  everyone  seeking  to  secure  the
benefits  of  governmental  action,”  it  clarified  that
when  legislation  “allocates  governmental  power  non-
neutrally,  by  explicitly  using  the  racial  nature  of  a
decision  to  determine  the  decisionmaking  process,”
it  violates  the  Equal  Protection  Clause.
/d.  at  470
(emphasis  in original).

The  political-restructuring  doctrine  laid  down
in  Hunter  and  Seattle  applies  only  in  a  limited  set
of  circumstances—viz,  those  rare  instances  in
which  race  is the  predominant  factor  in  the  creation
of  a  distinct  and  more  onerous  political  process.
Yet,  the  doctrine  remains  an  essential  component
for  the  complete  vindication  of  the  rights  protected
by  the  Fourteenth  Amendment.  By  ensuring  that
decisions  as  to  the  constitutionally  permissible
consideration  of  race  in  the  provision  of  govern-
ment  services  or  benefits  are  made  through  an
undistorted  political  process,  the  doctrine  prevents
one  side  of  the  debate  over  such  policies  from
and
permanently  entrenching
thereby  “contributing  to  an  escalation  of  racial
hostility  and  conflict.”  Purents  Involved  in  Cmty.
Sch.  v.  Seattle  Sch.  Dist.  No.  1,  551  U.S.  701,  746
(2007)  (plurality  opinion)  (internal  quotation  marks
omitted).

its  preferences

7

Process,

B.  Prior  to  the  Enactment  of  Proposition  2,
University  Admissions  Policies  Were  Subject
to  a  Long  Established  and  High-Functioning
Political
and  Determinations
Concerning  the  Constitutionally  Permissible
Use  of Race  as  a  Factor  in Admissions  Were
Properly  Made  by  Publicly  Accountable
Governing  Boards  in  the  Context  of  the
University’s  Educational  Mission

Prior  to  the  enactment  of  Proposition  2,  there
existed  a  well-functioning,  longstanding  political
process  for  determining  the  policies  and  objectives
that  would  be  pursued  by  public  universities  in
Michigan,  including  those  policies  that  affected
admission  criteria  for  prospective  students.
This
process  has  functioned  effectively  over  the  years  to
place  in  the  hands  of  the  publicly  accountable  gov-
erning  boards  the  educational  policy  judgments
over  the  conduct  of  the  university,  including  the
question  of whether  to engage  in constitutionally  per-
missible  consideration  of race  as  a factor  in  universi-
ty admission.

1.  As  is  the  case  in  many  states,  Michigan  makes
the  governing  bodies  of its  public  universities  direct-
ly politically  accountable  to  the  citizens  of the  state.
Indeed,  reflecting  the  importance  of  the  public  uni-
versities  in  the  state,  Michigan’s  constitution  recog-
nizes  its  public  universities’  governing  boards  as  sep-
arate  constitutional  entities  that  are  directly  respon-
sible  to  the  people.  See  Mich.  Const.  art.  8, § 5; Mich.
Const.  of  1908  art.  11,  § 3; Mich.  Const.  of  1850,  art.
13, §§ 8-9.  By so  doing,  the  people  of Michigan  chose
to  remove  the  public  university  system  from  the

8

direct  control  of  the  legislature  and,  instead,  “to
intrust”  its  operations  to  elected  governing  boards
who  are  “directly  responsible  and  amenable  to  the
people.”  Sterling  v.  Regents  of the  Univ.  of Mich.,  68
N.W.  253,  254  (Mich.  1896).  This  judgment,  as  the
record  of the  state  constitutional  convention  of  1850
reflects,  was  to  establish  a  very  specific  political
process  rendering  the  state’s  institutions  of  higher
education  accountable  to  the  citizenry  of  the  state:
“[Rjegents  will  be  distributed  over  every  part  of  the
state,  and  the  public  will  thus  obtain  a  knowledge  of
this  institution  .  ..
.  They  will  obtain  very  important
knowledge  in  regard  to  the  establishment,  and  the
people  among  whom  they  live  will  become  informed
as  to  the  nature  of  this  institution,  and  will  become
interested  in it.”  Jd.  (quoting  Mich.  Const.  Debates,
782  (1850)).

The  resulting  governing  boards  of each  university
have  plenary  authority  to  change  any  governance
policy  of  the  university,  as  well  as  the  composition
and  authority  of  the  administrative  units  they  over-
see.  The  Michigan  Constitution  empowers  each
board  with  “general  supervision  of  its  institution.”
See  Mich.  Const.  art.  8, § 5.  In addition,  state  statutes

:  Michigan  undoubtedly  inibued  its  public  universities  with
direct  political  accountability  to  the  citizenry  with  a  full  under-
standing  that  those  institutions  would  be  central  to  the  state's
economy  and  the  overall  wellbeing  of its  citizens.  See  general-
ly  SRI  International,  The  Economic  Impact  of  Michigan's
Public  Universities  (May  2002)  (discussing  the  many  social
and  economic  contributions  that  Michigan  public  universities
make  within  the  state),  available  at  http://www.michigan
business.org/cm/Files/Reports/univimpactreport. pdf.

9

give  each  board  complete  governing  authority  over
the  university,  including  the  power  to  “enact  ordi-
nances,  by-laws  and  regulations  for  the  government
of  the  university.”  See  Mich.  Comp.  Laws.  §§  390.1-
.23;  see  also  id.  §§  390.101-.123;  390.641-.649.  With
that  authority  necessarily  comes  the  power  to  influ-
ence  changes  to  a  wide  variety  of  governance  poli-
cies—including  admissions  policies.

Along  with  the  authority  granted  by the  Michigan
Constitution  and  Michigan  statutes,  the  governance
documents  for  each  public  university  in  Michigan
establish  that  their  governing  boards  have  the  power
to  revise  admissions  policies  as  they  see  fit.
Accordingly,  the  Bylaws  of  the  University  of
Michigan  provide  that  “[t]he  faculty  of  each  school
and  college  shall  from  time  to time  recommend  to the
.  requirements  for  admission
Board  for  approval  .
and  graduation.”
Similarly,  the  Bylaws  of Michigan
State  University’s  Board  of Trustees  provide,  “Upon
the  recommendation  of the  President  the  Board  may
determine  and  establish  the  qualifications  of  stu-
So too,  Wayne
dents  for admission  at any  level...  .”
State’s  University  Statutes  establish  the  admission
standards  for  graduates  and  undergraduates.

Not  surprisingly,  these  publicly  elected  boards
have  been  responsive  to  public  influences,  and  have
been  particularly  influenced  by  the  opinions  of stu-

dents  and  faculty.  Because  they  are  popularly  elect-
ed,  candidates  for positions  on  these  boards  routine-
ly include  their  views  about  a  number  of university-
related  policies  when  they  are  seeking  office.
Indeed,  board  candidates  regularly  include  their
views  On  race-conscious  admissions  policies  in  their
platforms  when  running  for  election.  Once  elected,
board  members  have  the  authority  to amend  the  gov-
erning  documents  and  policies  of the  applicable  pub-
lic  university  by  way  of  changing  any  of  the  delega-
tions  of  authority  to  the  various  committees
that  make  university  policy,  including  admissions
policies.

2.  It is the  mission  of educational  institutions  like
the  public  universities  in  Michigan  to  provide  stu-
dents  with  the  skills  and  knowledge  necessary  to
realize  their  full  potential,  see  Wisconsin  v.  Yoder,
406  U.S.  205,  239  (1972),  as  well  as  to  instill  in  them
“the  values  on  which  our  society  rests,”  Ambach  v.
Norwick,  441  U.S.  68,  76  (1979).  The  fostering  of  a
diverse  student  body  though  admission  criteria  is  a
central  feature  of that  mission  because,  particularly
in  this  era  of  ever-expanding  globalization,  nothing
less  than  the  “nation’s  future  depends  upon  leaders
trained  through  wide  exposure  to  the  ideas  and
mores  of students  as  diverse  as  this  Nation  of many
peoples.”  Regents  of Univ.  of Cal.  v.  Bakke,  438  U.S.
265,  313  (1978)  (opinion  of Powell,  J.)  (citation  and

internal  quotation  marks  omitted).  As  this  Court  has
recognized,  racial  diversity  in  higher  education  pro-
motes  “‘cross-racial  understanding,  helps  to  break
down  racial  stereotypes,”  precipitates  “more  enlight-
ening”  classroom  discussion,  promotes  better  “learn-
ing outcomes,”  and  “better  prepares  students  for  an
increasingly  diverse  workforce  and  society.”  Grutter
v.  Bollinger,  539  U.S.  306,  330  (2003)  (internal  quota-

“strict

Thus,  while  this  Court  has  been  unyielding  in  its
insistence  that  any  use  of  racial  classifications  in
such  decisions  must  satisfy
scrutiny,”
Adarand  Constructors,  Inc.  v.  Pena,  515  U.S.  200,
222  (1995),  so  as  to  “smoke  out’  illegitimate  uses  of
race  by assuring  that  the  legislative  body  is pursuing
a  goal  important  enough  to  warrant  use  of a  highly
suspect  tool,”  City  of Richmond  v.  J.A.  Croson  Co.,
488  U.S.  469,  493  (1989)  (plurality  opinion),  this
Court  has  been  equally  clear  that  diversity  in
schools  and  classes,  including  racial  and  ethnic
diversity,  is  “a  goal  important  enough”  to  warrant
consideration  of  race  in  making  such  decisions.

the  government  can  have  a  “compelling”  interest  in

A public  university's  interest  in “obtaining  the  edu-
cational  benefits  that  flow  from  an  ethnically  diverse

12

student  body,”  Bakke,  438  U.S.  at  306,  is  as  com-
pelling  today  as  it  was  when  this  Court  decided
Bakke  and  Grutter.  Because  of  the  changing
demographics  of American  society,  the  ability  of an
individual  to  function  in  a  racially  and  ethnically
diverse  environment  is  increasingly  important.  By
2025,  the  year  in  which  children  who  are  entering
first  grade  this  year  will  graduate  from  high  school,
over  half  of  all  children  will  be  Black,  Hispanic,
American  Indian,  Native  Hawaiian,  or  multiracial,
and  over  42%  of  the  overall  population  will  be
descended  from these  historically  minority  racial  and
ethnic  groups.  These  demographic  shifts  reflect  an
ongoing  trend  in which  “[t]he  country  as  a whole  and
the  workforce  in  particular  is  becoming  more,  not
less  [racially]  diverse.””

Yet,  at  the  same  time  that  American  society  is
becoming  more  racially  diverse,  the  nation’s  schools
remain  highly  segregated.  A  student’s  race  is,  by
itself,  largely  predictive  of the  racial  composition  of
the  elementary  and  secondary  schools  he  or  she  will
attend.  The  most  serious  segregation  affects  Black
and  Hispanic  students:  “in  both  of these  populations
about  two  of every  five  students  attend  intensely  seg-

regated  schools.”  The  trend  toward  higher  levels  of
concentrated  segregation  for  Black  and  Hispanic  stu-

racial  isolation.  Nearly  nine  times  out  of ten,  segre-
gated  schools  with  high  percentages  of  students  of
color  are  also  high  poverty  schools.
The  result  of
this  pervasive  segregation  is  all  too  predictable.
Students  who  attend  high-poverty,  minority-segregat-
ed  primary  and  secondary  schools  have  poorer  edu-
cational  outcomes  and  are  often  not  as  well  equipped
to  enter institutions  of higher  education  or  the  work-
force. Not  only  are  these  inequities  growing  worse

Illinois,  Michigan  consistently  ranks ,as one  of most
segregated  states  for Black  students. *  Moreover,  col-
lege  completion  rates  among  Black  students  in  the
state  are  dropping:  between  2002  and  2010,  the  rate
for four-year  public  colleges  fell  by 5.1%,  and  the  rate
for two-year  colleges  fell  by 8.5%.

In  the  face  of this  grim  portrait  of enduring  racial
disparity  and  de facto  segregation,  educational  insti-
tutions  confront  a  choice.  They  can  ignore  the  per-
sistent  inequalities  in the  education  systems  that  seg-
regate  students  by race,  depriving  all  students  of an
education  enriched  by exposure  to diversity.  Or, they
can  take  that  reality  into  account  in some  limited  and

15

measured  way  by,  for  example,  considering  racial
and  ethnic  background  as  one  of many  factors  used
in admitting  a truly  diverse  student  body.

The  decision  whether  or  not  to  enact  admission
policies  that  promote  racial  diversity  is  inseparable
from  the  underlying  mission  of  educational  institu-
tions.  A substantial  body  of empirical  evidence  indi-
cates  that  learning  in  a  racially  diverse  setting  fur-
thers  students’  cognitive  and  intellectual  develop-
ment,  thus  providing  an  important  educational  bene-
fit to students  of every  race.  Diverse  classrooms  also
teach  students  to judge  others  as  individuals,  rather
than  according  to  stereotypes  and  prejudices.  These
benefits  provide  a powerful  catalyst  toward  integra-
tion  over  the  course  of a student’s  lifetime.

Over  the  past  two  decades,  social  science  research
has  become  increasingly  sophisticated  in its ability  to
isolate  and  identify  the  educational  detriments  of
segregation  and  the  concomitant  benefits  of integra-
tion.  As  a whole,  this  body  of research  unambiguous-
ly finds  that  racial  diversity  in  classrooms  positively
affects  minority  students’  math  and  verbal  achieve-
ment,  and,  conversely,  that  racial segregation  nega-
These  findings
tively  affects  their  performance.
provide  consistent  and  unambiguous  evidence  that

7  Roslyn  Arlin  Mickelson  &  Martha  Bottia,  /ntegrated
Education  and  Mathematics  Outcomes:  A Synthesis  of Social
Science  Research,  88  N.C.  L.  Rev.  993,  1032-34  &  nn.150-156
(Mar.  2010)  (collecting  studies).  See  also  Bernadette  Gray-
Little  & Robert  A.  Carels,  The  Effect  of Racial  Dissonance  on
Academic  Self-Esteem  and  Achievement  in  Elementary,
Junior  High,  and  High  School  Students,  7  J.  Res.  ON
ADOLESCENCE  109,  123,  125-26  (1997);  Geoffrey  Borman  &

16

learning  in  a  racially  diverse  classroom  is positively
related  to  outcomes  for  most  students—irrespective
of the  student’s  age,  race,  or  socioeconomic  status.

Beyond  the  well-documented  educational  benefits
of racially  diverse  classrooms,  there  are  broad  socie-
tal benefits  to be realized  from  the  interracial  contact
that  comes  with  diverse  classroom  environments.
Racial  isolation  breeds  stereotypes  and  prejudice,
whereas  “equal  status  contact  between  majority  and
minority  groups  in the  pursuit  of common  goals”  is a
critical  ingredient  in  improving  relations  between
members  of those  groups,  especially  if such  contact
“is  of a  sort  that  leads  to  the  perception  of common
interests  and  common  humanity  between  the  mem-
bers  of  the  two  groups.”
Interracial  cooperative
contact  among  students  of different  races  in our  pub-
lic schools  has repeatedly  been  linked  with  increased
levels  of  tolerance  for  children  of  other  races,  and
increased  likelihood  that  children  of different  races
will  become  and  remain  friends.

Context  and  Racial  Composition  Matter  for  the  Reading
Achievement  Growth  of Young  Children?,  112  TEACHERS  COLL.

17

Classroom  diversity  offers  enduring  benefits  to  a
multiethnic,  democratic  society,  and  its  citizens.
Students  who  learn  to  interact  with  individuals  of
other  races  in school  are  far  “more  likely  to  function
in desegregated  environments  in later  life.  As  adults,
they  more  frequently  live[]  in desegregated  neighbor-
hoods,  halve]  children  who  attend[]  desegregated
schools,  and  ha{ve}  close  friends  of the  other  race{s]
than  did adults  ...  who  had  attended  segregated
schools.”
They  are  also  more  likely  as  adults  to
interact  with  individuals  of other  races  than  are  stu-
dents  educated  in racially  homogeneous  schools.»

18

Their  decisions  to  do  so  were  subject  to  the  estab-
lished  political  processes  that  govern  every  facet  of
the  universities’  administration,  including  their
adnussions  policies.

C.  Proposition  2 Violates  the  Equal  Protection
Clause  by  Restructuring  a  Functioning
Political  Process  to  Create  a  Separate  and
More  Onerous  Process  based  on  Explicitly
Racial  Considerations

It is clear  from  the  foregoing  that  admissions  poli-
cies  in  Michigan's  public  universities  have  been  and
will—with  the  exception  of the  subjects  removed  by
Proposition  2—-continue  to  be  part  of  a  political
process.  But  with  particular  regard  to  the  key  issue
of whether  race  may  be considered  as  a constitution-
ally  permissible  factor  in  admissions  decisions,
Proposal  2 has  completely  restructured  the  political
process  by prohibiting  those  who  are  in favor  of such
policies  from  lobbying  elected  officials  for  changes
that  .hey  desire.

Proposal  2  therefore  imposes  a  special,  racially
motivated  burden  for  minorities  by creating  two  dif-
ferent  political  processes  for  advocating  for  changes
in  admissions  criteria.  Because  members  of the  gov-
erming  boards  of  Michigan's  public  universities  are
elected,  citizens  are  free  to  advocate  for  changes  or
enhancements  to  admissions  criteria  by using  politi-
cal  pressure  to  influence  the  board  members’  deci-
sions  and  by electing  their  preferred  board  member
candidates.  Indeed,  board  members  regularly  state
their  positions  with  respect  to  admissions  policies  in
Thus,  with  respect  to  all
their  campaign  materials.
admissions  factors  with  the  distinct  exception  of

19

race,  the  boards’  traditional  control  over  the  admis-
For  example,  the
sions  process  is  preserved.
University  of Michigan  has  long  departed  from  base-
line  admissions  criteria,  such  as  GPA  and  SAT  scores,
for  a  variety  of  purposes,  including  taking  into
account  factors  such  as  legacy  status,  geographic
background,  athletic  ability,  and  many  other  lawful
factors.  But  under  Proposal  2, proponents  of includ-
ing constitutionally  permissible  consideration  of race
in  admissions  have  been  excluded  from  these  long-
established  political  processes  for  influencing  the
conduct  of the state's  institutions  of higher education
and  instead  must  pass  a  constitutional  amendment—
an  extraordinary  burden—before  the  Board  could
even  contemplate  such  policies.

Given  that  result,  it is evident  that  Proposal  2 rests
“on  distinctions  based  on  race”  and  targets  those  dis-
tinctions  for  “peculiar  and  disadvantageous  treat-
ment.”  Seattle,  458  U.S.  at  485  (“[W]hen  the  political
process  or  the  decisionmaking  mechanism  used  to
address  racially  conscious  legislation—and  only
such  legislation—is  singled  out  for  peculiar  and  dis-
advantageous  treatment,  the  governmental  action
plainly  rests  on  distinctions  based  on  race.” ) (empha-
sis  in  original)  (internal  quotation  marks  omitted);
see  also  Hunter,  393  U.S.  at  393  (“[T]he  State  may  no
more  disadvantage  any  particular  group  by making  it
more  difficult  to  enact  legislation  in  its  behalf  than  it
may  dilute  any  person’s  vote  or  give  any  group  a
smaller  representation  than  another  of  comparable
size.”).

The  results  of  this  racial  gerrymandering  have
been  dramatic.  Since  Proposal  2 was  adopted,  racial

20

diversity  in  the  state’s  higher  education  institutions
has  dropped  significantly.»  For  example,  in  1994,
8.7% of total  enrollment  at the  University of Michigan
were  African-American  students.  But since  the pas-
sage  of  Proposal  2,  African-American,  Latino,  and
Native  American  students in  the  entering  undergrad-
uate  classes  already  have  dropped  by  one  third.
Graduate  student  enrollment  in  the  law,  dental,  and
medical  schools  has  seen  even  larger drops in  under-
represented  minority  enrollment.
By  2011,  the  per-
centage  of African-American  students  made up  only

“Similarly,  one  need  only  look  at  the  effects  of  California’s
Proposal  209,  that  statute  on  which  Michigan's  Proposal  2 is
modeled,  to  see  the  reality  of  the  place  of  race-conscious
admissions  policies  in  American  education  today.  Since  the
passage  of Proposition  209,  the  percentage  of underrepresent-
ed  minority  students  enrolled  in  the  University  of  California
system  has  declined  significantly,  especially  at  the  system’s
most  selective  and  prestigious  schools.  From  1995  to 2002,  the
percentage  of  underrepresented  minority  freshmen  out  of all
freshmen  enrolled  has  decreased  on  five  of eight  UC  campus-
es,  and  the  two  flagship  canppuses—UC  Berkeley  and  UCLA—
experienced  the  steepest  declines  of underrepresented  minor-
ity  freshmen  enrollment,  with  both  campuses  experiencing  a
56% decrease  from  1995  levels.  See  lan  Wang,  Finding  a  Silver
Lining:  Positive  Impact  of Looking  Beyond  Race  Amidst  the
Negative  Effects  of Proposal  209,  2008  BYU  Epuc.  &  L.J.  149,
157- 58 & nn.  51-52  (2008).

Mandate:  A  Seven-Year  Progress  Report,  1987-1994  2 (Nov.
1995),  available  at  http://milproj.dc.umich.edu/Michigan
_Mandate/pdf/Michigan-Mandate-Progress. pdf.

~  See  Univ.  of  Mich.  Office  of  the  Registrar,  Ethnicity
Reports,  Table  844,  available  at  http://www.ro.umich.edu/
enrollment/ethnicity. php.

See  id.  at  Table  843.

21

4.77%  of  the  student  body  despite  the  fact  that  they
comprised  16.16  % of four-year high school  graduates
in the  state  that  year.

The  Petitioner’s  contention  that  Proposal  2  does
not  have  a “racial  focus,”  because  it “does  not  burden
minority  interests  and  minority  interests  alone”  is
Although  Proposal  2 also  prohibits
without  merit.
consideration  of  sex  as  a  factor  in  the  admission
process,  this  inclusion  does  not  invalidate  the
Respondent's  claim  of  selective  political  restructur-
ing based  on  race.  In Hunter,  the  initiative  burdened
both  racial  and  non-racial  minorities  but  this  Court
still  found  that  the  law  had a  racial  focus.  See  393
U.S.  at  387,  390-91.  And,  as  the  Sixth  Circuit  noted,
“(t]he  history  of  Proposal  2 and  its description  on  the
ballot  leave  little  doubt”  that  “[hjere,  as  in  Hunter,
the  clear  focus
of  the  challenged  amendment  is
race.”  See  Pet.  App.  26a  n.4.

As  it  now  stands,  Michigan’s  public  universities
can  consider  virtually  every  aspect  of an  applicant’s
background  except for  race.  Under  Proposal  2, citi-
zens  can  continue  to  campaign  for  admissions  poli-
cies  that  advance  their  own  interests  without  under-
taking  the  enormous  burden  of seeking  a  state  con-
stitutional  amendment.  Without  having  to  undertake

“  See  Univ.  of Mich.  Office  of the  Registrar,  Total  Enrollment
Overview  (Fall  2011),  available  at  http://www.ro.umich.edu/
report/llenrollmentoverview.pdf;
for  Educ.
Performance  &  Information,  2011  Cohort  Four-Year,  2010
Cohort  Five-Year  and  2009  Sia-Year  Graduation  and
Dropout  Rates  including  Subgroups,  available  at  http://
www.  michigan.gov/cepi/0,  1607,7-113-21423_  30451  51357---
,00. html.

Mich.

Ctr.

22

the  enormous  burden  of passing  a  statewide  consti-
tutional  amendment,  those  who  advocate  considera-
tion  of  legacy  status,  residency  in  the  Upper
Peninsula,  or  other  non-racial  characteristics  as  fac-
tors  in  admissions  policies  are  free  to  lobby  the  uni-
versities  governing  boards  and  vote  for  governance
board  candidates  who  have  stated  their  position  in
favor  or  against  particular  admission  policies.

Based  on  the  teachings  of  Hunter  and  Seattle,
Proposal  2  violates  the  Fourteenth  Amendment
because  it restructures  the  decision-making  process
in a way  that  requires  a ballot  initiative  only for those
citizens  who  want  race  to  be  considered  as  part  of
university  admissions  policies.  This  restructuring
effectively  closes  the  ordinary  and  longstanding
political  processes  for  influencing  the  admissions
policies  to  the  state’s  higher  education  institutions  to
any  citizen  who  believes  that  race  and  diversity  are
important  considerations  in  university  admissions.
Proposal  2 nullifies  any  use  of  the  regular  political
processes  available  to  others  to  advocate  for  race-
conscious  admissions  policies.

Indeed,  Proposal  2 is precisely  the  type  of legisla-
tion  that  Hunter  and  Seattle  prohibit,  because  it not
only  removes  race-conscious  admissions  policies
from  the  political  process,  but  it also  makes  it nearly
impossible  for  advocates  to  restore  these  policies
through  ordinary  political  means.  See  Seattle,  458
U.S.  at  470.  Instead  of  lobbying  school  administra-
tors  and  members  of the  boards  for  each  university,
Proposal  2  requires  that  proponents  of  race-con-
scious  admissions  policies  persuade  the  entire  state
of Michigan  to  amend  the  state  constitution  in  order

23

to  have  those  policies  considered.  This  requirement
presents  a  significantly  higher  hurdle  for  minorities
seeking  to  advocate  for their  interests.

The  State  has  not  offered  any  compelling  justifica-
tion  in  support  of  Proposition  2.  Nor  has  it endeav-
ored  to  argue  that  Proposition  2  is  “narrowly  tai-
lored”  to achieve  such  an  interest.  Adarand,  515  U.S.
at 227.  That  being  the  case,  Proposition  2 cannot  sur-
vive  the  Respondents’  Equal  Protection  challenge.

CONCLUSION

The  judgment  of  the  United  States  Court  of
Appeals  for  the  Sixth  Circuit—which  strikes  down
Proposal  2 insofar  as  it  applies  to  constitutionally-
permissible  consideration  of race  as  a  factor  in  uni-
versity  admission  criteria—should  be  affirmed.

Respectfully  submitted,

SUMMARY  OF  THE  ARGUMENT

It  this  Court  upholds  Article  I,  Section  26  of
the  Michigan  Constitution  (“Section  26”),  Michigan’s
primary  and  secondary  public  schools
will  be
prevented  from  taking  research-based,  otherwise
constitutional  measures  to  meet  the  needs  of  all
students  for  a  diverse  learning  environment.  Section
26  is  not  limited  to  prohibiting  the  use  of  race  in
higher  education.  It  includes  broad  prohibitions
against  reliance  on  race  by “the  state.”  MICH.  CONST.
art.  I, § 26  (2006)  (“The  state  shall  not  discriminate
against,  or  grant  preferential  treatment  to,  any
individual  or  group  on  the  basis  of  race,  sex,  color,
ethnicity,  or  national  origin  in the  operation  of public
public
employment,
contracting.”).  The  state  is  defined  to  include  any
“school
of
Michigan.”  Jd.  That  prohibition  is identical  to  those
currently  found  in  eight  other  states.  CAL.  CONST.
art.  I,  § 31  (adopted  1996);  Fla.  Executive  Order,  99-
281  (issued  1996);  WASH.  CODE  ANN.  § 49.60.400(1)
(adopted  1998);  ARIZ.  CONST.  art.  II, § 26(A)  (adopted
2010);
NEB.  CONST.  art.  I, § 30  (adopted  2008);  OKLA.
CoNnsT.  art.  Il,  § 36  (adopted  2012);  N.H.  Rev.  STAT.
ANN.  § 187-A:16-a(l)  (adopted  2011);  MAss.  GEN.
LAWS  ch.  151B,  § 4 (adopted  2012).
